ADAM BEDE 
 by George Eliot 
 Book one 
 chapter I 
 the Workshop 
 with a single drop of ink for a mirror , the egyptian sorcerer undertake 
 to reveal to any chance comer far - reach vision of the past . this be 
 what I undertake to do for you , reader . with this drop of ink at the 
 end of my pen , I will show you the roomy workshop of Mr. Jonathan Burge , 
 carpenter and builder , in the village of Hayslope , as it appear on the 
 eighteenth of June , in the year of our Lord 1799 . 
 the afternoon sun be warm on the five workman there , busy upon door 
 and window - frame and wainscoting . a scent of pine - wood from a tentlike 
 pile of plank outside the open door mingle itself with the scent of 
 the eld - bush which be spread their summer snow close to 
 the open window opposite ; the slanting sunbeam shine through the 
 transparent shaving that fly before the steady plane , and light up the 
 fine grain of the oak panelling which stand prop against the wall . 
 on a heap of those soft shaving a rough , grey shepherd dog have 
 make himself a pleasant bed , and be lie with his nose between his 
 fore - paw , occasionally wrinkle his brow to cast a glance at the 
 tall of the five workman , who be carve a shield in the centre of 
 a wooden mantelpiece . it be to this workman that the strong barytone 
 belong which be hear above the sound of plane and hammer singing - 
 Awake , my soul , and with the sun 
 Thy daily stage of duty run ; 
 shake off dull sloth ... 
 here some measurement be to be take which require more concentrated 
 attention , and the sonorous voice subside into a low whistle ; but it 
 presently break out again with renew vigour - 
 let all thy converse be sincere , 
 thy conscience as the noonday clear . 
 such a voice could only come from a broad chest , and the broad chest 
 belong to a large - boned , muscular man nearly six foot high , with a 
 back so flat and a head so well poise that when he draw himself up 
 to take a more distant survey of his work , he have the air of a soldier 
 stand at ease . the sleeve roll up above the elbow show an arm 
 that be likely to win the prize for feat of strength ; yet the long 
 supple hand , with its broad finger - tip , look ready for work of 
 skill . in his tall stalwartness Adam Bede be a Saxon , and justify his 
 name ; but the jet - black hair , make the more noticeable by its contrast 
 with the light paper cap , and the keen glance of the dark eye that 
 shine from under strongly mark , prominent and mobile eyebrow , 
 indicate a mixture of celtic blood . the face be large and roughly 
 hew , and when in repose have no other beauty than such as belong to an 
 expression of good - humour honest intelligence . 
 it be clear at a glance that the next workman be Adam 's brother . he be 
 nearly as tall ; he have the same type of feature , the same hue of hair 
 and complexion ; but the strength of the family likeness seem only to 
 render more conspicuous the remarkable difference of expression both in 
 form and face . Seth 's broad shoulder have a slight stoop ; his eye 
 be grey ; his eyebrow have less prominence and more repose than his 
 brother 's ; and his glance , instead of be keen , be confide and 
 benign . he have throw off his paper cap , and you see that his hair be 
 not thick and straight , like Adam 's , but thin and wavy , allow you 
 to discern the exact contour of a coronal arch that predominate very 
 decidedly over the brow . 
 the idle tramp always feel sure they could get a copper from Seth ; they 
 scarcely ever speak to Adam . 
 the concert of the tool and Adam 's voice be at last break by Seth , 
 who , lift the door at which he have be work intently , place 
 it against the wall , and say , " there ! I have   finish my door to - day , 
 anyhow . " 
 the workman all look up ; Jim Salt , a burly , red - haired man know as 
 Sandy Jim , pause from his planing , and Adam say to Seth , with a sharp 
 glance of surprise , " what ! dost think thee'st finish the door ? " 
 " aye , sure , " say Seth , with answering surprise ; " what be awante to't ? " 
 a loud roar of laughter from the other three workman make Seth look 
 round confusedly . Adam do not join in the laughter , but there be a 
 slight smile on his face as he say , in a gentler tone than before , 
 " why , thee'st forget the panel . " 
 the laughter burst out afresh as Seth clap his hand to his head , and 
 colour over brow and crown . 
 " Hoorray ! " shout a small lithe fellow call Wiry Ben , run forward 
 and seize the door . " we will hang up th ' door at fur end o ' th ' shop an ' 
 write on't   ' Seth Bede , the Methody , his work . ' here , Jim , lend 's hould 
 o ' th ' red pot . " 
 " nonsense ! " say Adam . " let it alone , Ben Cranage . you will mayhap be 
 make such a slip yourself some day ; you will laugh o ' th ' other side o ' 
 your mouth then . " 
 " catch I at it , Adam . it will be a good while afore my head 's full o ' th ' 
 methodie , " say Ben . 
 " Nay , but it be often full o ' drink , and that be bad . " 
 Ben , however , have now get the " red pot " in his hand , and be about 
 to begin write his inscription , make , by way of preliminary , an 
 imaginary s in the air . 
 " let it alone , will you ? " Adam call out , lay down his tool , 
 stride up to Ben , and seize his right shoulder . " let it alone , or 
 I will shake the soul out o ' your body . " 
 Ben shake in Adam 's iron grasp , but , like a plucky small man as he be , 
 he do not   mean to give in . with his left hand he snatch the brush from 
 his powerless right , and make a movement as if he would perform the feat 
 of write with his left . in a moment Adam turn he round , seize his 
 other shoulder , and , push he along , pin he against the wall . but 
 now Seth speak . 
 " let be , Addy , let be . Ben will be joke . why , he be I ' the right to 
 laugh at I - I canna help laugh at myself . " 
 " I shall not   loose he till he promise to let the door alone , " say Adam . 
 " Come , Ben , lad , " say Seth , in a persuasive tone , " do not   let us have a 
 quarrel about it . you know Adam will have his way . you may be well try 
 to turn a waggon in a narrow lane . say you will leave the door alone , and 
 make an end on't . " 
 " I binna fright at Adam , " say Ben , " but I donna mind sayin ' as I will 
 let ' t alone at your askin ' , Seth . " 
 " come , that be wise of you , Ben , " say Adam , laugh and relax his 
 grasp . 
 they all return to their work now ; but Wiry Ben , have have the bad 
 in the bodily contest , be bent on retrieve that humiliation by a 
 success in sarcasm . 
 " which be ye thinkin ' on , Seth , " he begin - " the pretty parson 's face or 
 her sarmunt , when ye forget the panel ? " 
 " come and hear she , Ben , " say Seth , good - humouredly ; ' she be go to 
 preach on the Green to - night ; happen ye'd get something to think on 
 yourself then , instead o ' those wicked song you be so fond on . ye might 
 get religion , and that ' ud be the good day 's earning y ' ever make . " 
 " all I ' good time for that , Seth ; I will think about that when I be a - go 
 to settle I ' life ; bachelor do not   want such heavy earnin 's . happen 
 I shall do the coortin ' an ' the religion both together , as ye do , Seth ; 
 but ye wouldna ha ' I get convert an ' chop in atween ye an ' the pretty 
 preacher , an ' carry her aff ? " 
 " no fear o ' that , Ben ; she be neither for you nor for I to win , I doubt . 
 only you come and hear she , and you will not   speak lightly on she again . " 
 " well , I be half a mind t ' ha ' a look at she to - night , if there be not 
 good company at th ' Holly Bush . what will she take for her text ? happen ye 
 can tell I , Seth , if so be as I shouldna come up I ' time for't . will't 
 be - what come ye out for to see ? a prophetess ? yea , I say unto you , and 
 more than a prophetess - a uncommon pretty young woman . " 
 " Come , Ben , " say Adam , rather sternly , " you let the word o ' the Bible 
 alone ; you be go too far now . " 
 " what ! be ye a - turnin ' roun ' , Adam ? I think ye war dead again th ' 
 woman preachin ' , a while agoo ? " 
 " Nay , I be not turnin ' noway . I say nought about the woman preachin ' . 
 I say , you let the Bible alone : you have   get a jest - book , han't   you , as 
 you be rare and proud on ? keep your dirty finger to that . " 
 " why , y ' be gettin ' as big a saint as Seth . Y ' be go to th ' 
 preachin ' to - night , I should think . Ye'll do finely t ' lead the singin ' . 
 but I don ' know what Parson Irwine ' ull say at his gran ' favright Adam 
 Bede a - turnin ' Methody . " 
 " never do you bother yourself about I , Ben . I be not a - go to turn 
 Methodist any more nor you be - though it be like enough you will turn 
 to something bad . Mester Irwine 's get more sense nor to meddle wi ' 
 people 's do as they like in religion . that be between themselves and 
 God , as he be say to I many a time . " 
 " aye , aye ; but he be none so fond o ' your dissenter , for all that . " 
 " maybe ; I be none so fond o ' Josh Tod 's thick ale , but I do not   hinder you 
 from make a fool o ' yourself wi't . " 
 there be a laugh at this thrust of Adam 's , but Seth say , very 
 seriously . " Nay , nay , Addy , thee mustna say as anybody 's religion 's 
 like thick ale . Thee dostna believe but what the dissenter and the 
 Methodists have get the root o ' the matter as well as the church folk . " 
 " Nay , Seth , lad ; I be not for laugh at no man 's religion . let them 
 follow their conscience , that be all . only I think it ' ud be well if 
 their conscience ' ud let them stay quiet I ' the church - there be a deal 
 to be learn there . and there be such a thing as be oversperitial ; we 
 must have something beside Gospel I ' this world . look at the canal , an ' 
 th ' aqueduc 's , an ' th ' coal - pit engine , and Arkwright 's mill there at 
 Cromford ; a man must learn summat beside Gospel to make they thing , I 
 reckon . but t ' hear some o ' them preacher , you would think as a man must be 
 do nothing all 's life but shut 's eye and look what be agoe 
 on inside he . I know a man must have the love o ' God in his soul , and 
 the Bible 's God 's word . but what do the Bible say ? why , it say as God 
 put his sperrit into the workman as build the tabernacle , to make he do 
 all the carve work and thing as want a nice hand . and this be my 
 way o ' look at it : there be the sperrit o ' God in all thing and all 
 times - weekday as well as Sunday - and I ' the great work and invention , 
 and I ' the figuring and the mechanic . and God help we with our 
 headpiece and our hand as well as with our soul ; and if a man do 
 bit o ' job out o ' work hour - build a oven for 's wife to save she 
 from go to the bakehouse , or scrat at his bit o ' garden and make 
 two potato grow istead o ' one , he be do more good , and he be just as 
 near to God , as if he be run after some preacher and a - pray and 
 a - groaning . " 
 " well do , Adam ! " say Sandy Jim , who have pause from his planing to 
 shift his plank while Adam be speak ; " that be the good sarmunt I have 
 hear this long while . by th ' same token , my wife 's be a - plaguin ' on 
 I to build she a oven this twelvemont . " 
 " there be reason in what thee say'st , Adam , " observe Seth , gravely . " but 
 thee know'st thyself as it be hear the preacher thee find'st so much 
 fault with have turn many an idle fellow into an industrious un . it be 
 the preacher as empty th ' alehouse ; and if a man get religion , he will 
 do his work none the bad for that . " 
 " On'y he will lave the panel out o ' th ' door sometimes , eh , Seth ? " say 
 Wiry Ben . 
 " ah , Ben , you have   get a joke again ' I as will last you your life . but it 
 isna religion as be I ' fault there ; it be Seth Bede , as be allay a 
 wool - gathering chap , and religion hasna cure he , the more be the pity . " 
 " Ne'er hee I , Seth , " say Wiry Ben , " y ' be a down - right good - hearte 
 chap , panel or no panel ; an ' ye donna set up your bristle at every 
 bit o ' fun , like some o ' your kin , as be mayhap cliverer . " 
 " Seth , lad , " say Adam , take no notice of the sarcasm against himself , 
 " thee mustna take I unkind . I wasna drive at thee in what I say just 
 now . some be get one way o ' look at thing and some be get another . " 
 " Nay , nay , Addy , thee mean'st I no unkindness , " say Seth , " I know that 
 well enough . thee't like thy dog Gyp - thee bark'st at I sometimes , but 
 thee allay lick'st my hand after . " 
 all hand work on in silence for some minute , until the church clock 
 begin to strike six . before the first stroke have die away , Sandy Jim 
 have loose his plane and be reach his jacket ; Wiry Ben have leave a 
 screw half drive in , and throw his screwdriver into his tool - basket ; 
 Mum Taft , who , true to his name , have keep silence throughout the 
 previous conversation , have fling down his hammer as he be in the act 
 of lift it ; and Seth , too , have straighten his back , and be put 
 out his hand towards his paper cap . Adam alone have go on with his work 
 as if nothing have happen . but observe the cessation of the tool , he 
 look up , and say , in a tone of indignation , " look there , now ! I can not 
 abide to see man throw away their tool I ' that way , the minute the 
 clock begin to strike , as if they take no pleasure I ' their work and 
 be afraid o ' do a stroke too much . " 
 Seth look a little conscious , and begin to be slow in his 
 preparation for go , but Mum Taft break silence , and say , " aye , aye , 
 Adam lad , ye talk like a young un . when y ' be six - an'-forty like I , 
 istid o ' six - an'-twenty , ye wonna be so flush o ' workin ' for nought . " 
 " nonsense , " say Adam , still wrathful ; " what be age get to do with it , I 
 wonder ? ye arena get stiff yet , I reckon . I hate to see a man 's arm 
 drop down as if he be shoot , before the clock 's fairly strike , just as 
 if he would never a bit o ' pride and delight in 's work . the very grindstone 
 ' ull go on turn a bit after you loose it . " 
 " Bodderation , Adam ! " exclaim Wiry Ben ; " lave a chap aloon , will ' ee ? 
 Ye war afinde faut wi ' preacher a while agoo - y ' be fond enough o ' 
 preachin ' yoursen . Ye may like work well nor play , but I like play 
 well nor work ; that will ' commodate ye - it lave ye th ' more to do . " 
 with this exit speech , which he consider effective , Wiry Ben 
 shoulder his basket and leave the workshop , quickly follow by Mum 
 Taft and Sandy Jim . Seth linger , and look wistfully at Adam , as if 
 he expect he to say something . 
 " Shalt go home before thee go'st to the preaching ? " Adam ask , look 
 up . 
 " Nay ; I have   get my hat and thing at Will Maskery 's . I shall not   be home 
 before go for ten . I will happen see Dinah Morris safe home , if she be 
 willing . there 's nobody come with she from Poyser 's , thee know'st . " 
 " then I will tell mother not to look for thee , " say Adam . 
 " Thee artna go to Poyser 's thyself to - night ? " say Seth rather 
 timidly , as he turn to leave the workshop . 
 " Nay , I be go to th ' school . " 
 Hitherto Gyp have keep his comfortable bed , only lift up his head and 
 watch Adam more closely as he notice the other workman depart . 
 but no soon do Adam put his ruler in his pocket , and begin to twist 
 his apron round his waist , than Gyp run forward and look up in his 
 master 's face with patient expectation . if Gyp have have a tail he would 
 doubtless have wag it , but be destitute of that vehicle for his 
 emotion , he be like many other worthy personage , destine to appear 
 more phlegmatic than nature have make he . 
 " what ! art ready for the basket , eh , Gyp ? " say Adam , with the same 
 gentle modulation of voice as when he speak to Seth . 
 Gyp jump and give a short bark , as much as to say , " of course . " poor 
 fellow , he have not a great range of expression . 
 the basket be the one which on workday hold Adam 's and Seth 's dinner ; 
 and no official , walk in procession , could look more resolutely 
 unconscious of all acquaintance than Gyp with his basket , trot at 
 his master 's heel . 
 on leave the workshop Adam lock the door , take the key out , and 
 carry it to the house on the other side of the woodyard . it be a 
 low house , with smooth grey thatch and buff wall , look pleasant 
 and mellow in the evening light . the leaded window be bright and 
 speckless , and the door - stone be as clean as a white boulder at ebb 
 tide . on the door - stone stand a clean old woman , in a dark - strip linen 
 gown , a red kerchief , and a linen cap , talk to some speckled fowl 
 which appear to have be draw towards she by an illusory expectation 
 of cold potato or barley . the old woman 's sight seem to be dim , for 
 she do not recognize Adam till he say , " here be the key , dolly ; lay it 
 down for I in the house , will you ? " 
 " aye , sure ; but wunna ye come in , Adam ? Miss Mary 's I ' th ' house , and 
 Mester Burge ' ull be back anon ; he would be glad t ' ha ' ye to supper wi'm , 
 I will be 's warrand . " 
 " no , dolly , thank you ; I be off home . good evening . " 
 Adam hasten with long stride , Gyp close to his heel , out of the 
 workyard , and along the highroad lead away from the village and down 
 to the valley . as he reach the foot of the slope , an elderly horseman , 
 with his portmanteau strap behind he , stop his horse when Adam 
 have pass he , and turn round to have another long look at the 
 stalwart workman in paper cap , leather breech , and dark - blue worst 
 stocking . 
 Adam , unconscious of the admiration he be exciting , presently strike 
 across the field , and now break out into the tune which have all day 
 long be run in his head : 
 let all thy converse be sincere , 
 thy conscience as the noonday clear ; 
 for God 's all - see eye survey 
 Thy secret thought , thy work and way . 
 Chapter II 
 the preaching 
 about a quarter to seven there be an unusual appearance of excitement 
 in the village of Hayslope , and through the whole length of its 
 little street , from the Donnithorne Arms to the churchyard gate , the 
 inhabitant have evidently be draw out of their house by something 
 more than the pleasure of lounge in the evening sunshine . the 
 Donnithorne Arms stand at the entrance of the village , and a small 
 farmyard and stackyard which flank it , indicate that there be a 
 pretty take of land attach to the inn , give the traveller a promise 
 of good feed for himself and his horse , which might well console he 
 for the ignorance in which the weather - beat sign leave he as to the 
 heraldic bearing of that ancient family , the donnithorne . Mr. Casson , 
 the landlord , have be for some time stand at the door with his hand 
 in his pocket , balance himself on his heel and toe and look 
 towards a piece of unenclosed ground , with a maple in the middle of it , 
 which he know to be the destination of certain grave - look man and 
 woman whom he have observe pass at interval . 
 Mr. Casson 's person be by no mean of that common type which can be 
 allow to pass without description . on a front view it appear to 
 consist principally of two sphere , bear about the same relation to 
 each other as the earth and the moon : that be to say , the low sphere 
 might be say , at a rough guess , to be thirteen time large than the 
 upper which naturally perform the function of a mere satellite and 
 tributary . but here the resemblance cease , for Mr. Casson 's head be 
 not at all a melancholy - look satellite nor be it a ' spotty globe , " 
 as Milton have irreverently call the moon ; on the contrary , no head and 
 face could look more sleek and healthy , and its expression - which be 
 chiefly confine to a pair of round and ruddy cheek , the slight 
 knot and interruption form the nose and eye be scarcely worth 
 mention - be one of jolly contentment , only temper by that sense of 
 personal dignity which usually make itself feel in his attitude and 
 bearing . this sense of dignity could hardly be consider excessive in 
 a man who have be butler to " the family " for fifteen year , and who , in 
 his present high position , be necessarily very much in contact with 
 his inferior . how to reconcile his dignity with the satisfaction of his 
 curiosity by walk towards the Green be the problem that Mr. Casson 
 have be revolve in his mind for the last five minute ; but when 
 he have partly solve it by take his hand out of his pocket , and 
 thrust they into the armhole of his waistcoat , by throw his 
 head on one side , and provide himself with an air of contemptuous 
 indifference to whatever might fall under his notice , his thought be 
 divert by the approach of the horseman whom we lately see pausing to 
 have another look at our friend Adam , and who now pull up at the door 
 of the Donnithorne Arms . 
 " take off the bridle and give he a drink , ostler , " say the traveller 
 to the lad in a smock - frock , who have come out of the yard at the sound 
 of the horse 's hoof . 
 " why , what be up in your pretty village , landlord ? " he continue , get 
 down . " there seem to be quite a stir . " 
 " it be a Methodis ' preaching , sir ; it be be gev hout as a young woman 's 
 a - go to preach on the Green , " answer Mr. Casson , in a treble and 
 wheezy voice , with a slightly mince accent . " will you please to step 
 in , sir , an ' tek somethink ? " 
 " no , I must be get on to Rosseter . I only want a drink for my horse . 
 and what do your parson say , I wonder , to a young woman preach just 
 under his nose ? " 
 " Parson Irwine , sir , do not   live here ; he live at Brox'on , over the 
 hill there . the parsonage here be a tumble - down place , sir , not fit for 
 gentry to live in . he come here to preach of a Sunday afternoon , sir , 
 an ' put up his hoss here . it be a grey cob , sir , an ' he set great store 
 by't . he be allay put up his hoss here , sir , iver since before I he d the 
 Donnithorne Arms . I be not this countryman , you may tell by my tongue , 
 sir . they be cur'ous talker I ' this country , sir ; the gentry 's hard 
 work to hunderstand them . I be bring hup among the gentry , sir , an ' 
 get the turn o ' their tongue when I be a bye . why , what do you think 
 the folk here say for ' hevn't   you ? ' - the gentry , you know , say , 
 ' hevn't   you ' - well , the people about here say ' hanna yey . ' it be what 
 they call the dileck as be speak hereabout , sir . that be what I have   hear 
 Squire Donnithorne say many a time ; it be the dileck , say he . " 
 " aye , aye , " say the strange , smile . " I know it very well . but you have 
 not get many Methodists about here , surely - in this agricultural spot ? I 
 should have think there would hardly be such a thing as a Methodist to 
 be find about here . you be all farmer , be not   you ? the Methodists can 
 seldom lay much hold on they . " 
 " why , sir , there be a pretty lot o ' workman round about , sir . there be 
 Mester Burge as own the timber - yard over there , he undertek a good bit 
 o ' build an ' repair . an ' there be the stone - pit not far off . there be 
 plenty of emply I ' this countryside , sir . an ' there be a fine batch o ' 
 methodisse at Treddles'on - that be the market town about three mile 
 off - you will maybe ha ' come through it , sir . there be pretty nigh a score 
 of them on the Green now , as come from there . that be where our people 
 get it from , though there be only two man of them in all Hayslope : that be 
 will Maskery , the wheelwright , and Seth Bede , a young man as work at 
 the carpenterin ' . " 
 " the preacher come from Treddleston , then , do she ? " 
 " Nay , sir , she come out o ' Stonyshire , pretty nigh thirty mile off . 
 but she be a - visitin ' hereabout at Mester Poyser 's at the Hall Farm - it be 
 they barn an ' big walnut - trees , right away to the left , sir . she be own 
 niece to Poyser 's wife , an ' they will be fine an ' vex at she for make 
 a fool of herself I ' that way . but I have   hear as there be no holding 
 these Methodisses when the maggit 's once get I ' their head : many of them 
 go stark starin ' mad wi ' their religion . though this young woman 's 
 quiet enough to look at , by what I can make out ; I have   not see she 
 myself . " 
 " well , I wish I have time to wait and see she , but I must get on . I have 
 be out of my way for the last twenty minute to have a look at that 
 place in the valley . it be Squire Donnithorne 's , I suppose ? " 
 " yes , sir , that be Donnithorne Chase , that be . fine hoak there , be not 
 there , sir ? I should know what it be , sir , for I have   live butler there 
 a - go I ' fifteen year . it be Captain Donnithorne as be th ' heir , 
 sir - Squire Donnithorne 's grandson . he will be comin ' of hage this 
 ' ay-'arvest , sir , an ' we shall hev fine doin 's . he own all the land 
 about here , sir , Squire Donnithorne do . " 
 " well , it be a pretty spot , whoever may own it , " say the traveller , 
 mount his horse ; " and one meet some fine strap fellow about 
 too . I meet as fine a young fellow as ever I see in my life , about 
 half an hour ago , before I come up the hill - a carpenter , a tall , 
 broad - shoulder fellow with black hair and black eye , march along 
 like a soldier . we want such fellow as he to lick the French . " 
 " Aye , sir , that be Adam Bede , that is , I will be bind - Thias Bede 's son 
 everybody know he hereabout . he be an uncommon clever stiddy fellow , 
 an ' wonderful strong . Lord bless you , sir - if you will hexcuse I for 
 say so - he can walk forty mile a - day , an ' lift a matter o ' sixty 
 ston ' . he be an uncommon favourite wi ' the gentry , sir : captain 
 Donnithorne and Parson Irwine mek a fine fuss wi ' he . but he be a 
 little lift up an ' peppery - like . " 
 " well , good evening to you , landlord ; I must get on . " 
 " your servant , sir ; good evenin ' . " 
 the traveller put his horse into a quick walk up the village , but when 
 he approach the Green , the beauty of the view that lie on his right 
 hand , the singular contrast present by the group of villager with 
 the knot of Methodists near the maple , and perhaps yet more , curiosity 
 to see the young female preacher , prove too much for his anxiety to get 
 to the end of his journey , and he pause . 
 the Green lie at the extremity of the village , and from it the road 
 branch off in two direction , one lead far up the hill by the 
 church , and the other wind gently down towards the valley . on the 
 side of the Green that lead towards the church , the broken line of 
 thatch cottage be continue nearly to the churchyard gate ; but on 
 the opposite northwestern side , there be nothing to obstruct the view 
 of gently swell meadow , and woode valley , and dark mass of distant 
 hill . that rich undulating district of Loamshire to which Hayslope 
 belong lie close to a grim outskirt of Stonyshire , overlook by its 
 barren hill as a pretty bloom sister may sometimes be see link in 
 the arm of a rugged , tall , swarthy brother ; and in two or three hour ' 
 ride the traveller might exchange a bleak treeless region , intersect 
 by line of cold grey stone , for one where his road wind under the 
 shelter of wood , or up swell hill , muffle with hedgerow and long 
 meadow - grass and thick corn ; and where at every turn he come upon some 
 fine old country - seat nestle in the valley or crown the slope , some 
 homestead with its long length of barn and its cluster of golden rick , 
 some grey steeple look out from a pretty confusion of tree and 
 thatch and dark - red tile . it be just such a picture as this last 
 that Hayslope Church have make to the traveller as he begin to mount the 
 gentle slope lead to its pleasant upland , and now from his station 
 near the Green he have before he in one view nearly all the other 
 typical feature of this pleasant land . high up against the horizon be 
 the huge conical mass of hill , like giant mound intend to fortify 
 this region of corn and grass against the keen and hungry wind of the 
 north ; not distant enough to be clothe in purple mystery , but with 
 sombre greenish side visibly speck with sheep , whose motion be only 
 reveal by memory , not detect by sight ; woo from day to day by the 
 change hour , but respond with no change in themselves - leave for 
 ever grim and sullen after the flush of morning , the wing gleam of 
 the April noonday , the parting crimson glory of the ripen summer 
 sun . and directly below they the eye rest on a more advanced line of 
 hang wood , divide by bright patch of pasture or furrow crop , 
 and not yet deepen into the uniform leafy curtain of high summer , but 
 still show the warm tint of the young oak and the tender green of 
 the ash and lime . then come the valley , where the wood grow thick , 
 as if they have roll down and hurry together from the patch leave 
 smooth on the slope , that they might take the well care of the tall 
 mansion which lift its parapet and send its faint blue summer smoke 
 among they . Doubtless there be a large sweep of park and a broad glassy 
 pool in front of that mansion , but the swell slope of meadow would 
 not let our traveller see they from the village green . he see instead 
 a foreground which be just as lovely - the level sunlight lie like 
 transparent gold among the gently curve stem of the feather grass 
 and the tall red sorrel , and the white ambel of the hemlock line 
 the bushy hedgerow . it be that moment in summer when the sound of 
 the scythe be whet make we cast more lingering look at the 
 flower - sprinkle tress of the meadow . 
 he might have see other beauty in the landscape if he have turn 
 a little in his saddle and look eastward , beyond Jonathan Burge 's 
 pasture and woodyard towards the green corn - field and walnut - tree of 
 the Hall Farm ; but apparently there be more interest for he in the 
 live group close at hand . every generation in the village be there , 
 from old " Feyther Taft " in his brown worst night - cap , who be bent 
 nearly double , but seem tough enough to keep on his leg a long while , 
 lean on his short stick , down to the baby with their little round 
 head loll forward in quilt linen cap . now and then there be a 
 new arrival ; perhaps a slouch labourer , who , have eat his supper , 
 come out to look at the unusual scene with a slow bovine gaze , willing 
 to hear what any one have to say in explanation of it , but by no mean 
 excited enough to ask a question . but all take care not to join the 
 Methodists on the Green , and identify themselves in that way with the 
 expectant audience , for there be not one of they that would not have 
 disclaim the imputation of have come out to hear the " preacher 
 woman " - they have only come out to see " what war a - go on , like . " the 
 man be chiefly gather in the neighbourhood of the blacksmith 's shop . 
 but do not imagine they gather in a knot . villager never swarm : a 
 whisper be unknown among they , and they seem almost as incapable of an 
 undertone as a cow or a stag . your true rustic turn his back on his 
 interlocutor , throw a question over his shoulder as if he mean to 
 run away from the answer , and walk a step or two far off when the 
 interest of the dialogue culminate . so the group in the vicinity of the 
 blacksmith 's door be by no mean a close one , and form no screen in 
 front of Chad Cranage , the blacksmith himself , who stand with his black 
 brawny arm fold , lean against the door - post , and occasionally 
 send forth a bellowing laugh at his own joke , give they a 
 mark preference over the sarcasm of Wiry Ben , who have renounce the 
 pleasure of the Holly Bush for the sake of see life under a new 
 form . but both style of wit be treat with equal contempt by Mr. 
 Joshua Rann . Mr. Rann 's leathern apron and subdue griminess can leave 
 no one in any doubt that he be the village shoemaker ; the thrust out 
 of his chin and stomach and the twirling of his thumb be more subtle 
 indication , intend to prepare unwary stranger for the discovery that 
 they be in the presence of the parish clerk . " Old Joshway , " as he 
 be irreverently call by his neighbour , be in a state of simmer 
 indignation ; but he have not yet open his lip except to say , in a 
 resound bass undertone , like the tuning of a violoncello , " Sehon , 
 king of the Amorites ; for his mercy endureth for ever ; and Og the king 
 of Basan : for his mercy endureth for ever " - a quotation which may seem 
 to have slight bearing on the present occasion , but , as with every other 
 anomaly , adequate knowledge will show it to be a natural sequence . Mr. 
 Rann be inwardly maintain the dignity of the Church in the face of 
 this scandalous irruption of methodism , and as that dignity be bind up 
 with his own sonorous utterance of the response , his argument naturally 
 suggest a quotation from the psalm he have read the last Sunday 
 afternoon . 
 the strong curiosity of the woman have draw they quite to the edge of 
 the Green , where they could examine more closely the Quakerlike costume 
 and odd deportment of the female Methodists . underneath the maple there 
 be a small cart , which have be bring from the wheelwright 's to serve 
 as a pulpit , and round this a couple of bench and a few chair have 
 be place . some of the Methodists be rest on these , with their 
 eye close , as if wrapt in prayer or meditation . other choose to 
 continue stand , and have turn their face towards the villager 
 with a look of melancholy compassion , which be highly amusing to Bessy 
 cranage , the blacksmith 's buxom daughter , know to her neighbour as 
 Chad 's Bess , who wonder " why the folk war amakin ' face a that'ns . " 
 Chad 's Bess be the object of peculiar compassion , because her hair , 
 be turn back under a cap which be set at the top of her head , 
 expose to view an ornament of which she be much prouder than of her 
 red cheek - namely , a pair of large round ear - ring with false garnet 
 in they , ornament condemn not only by the Methodists , but by her own 
 cousin and namesake Timothy 's Bess , who , with much cousinly feeling , 
 often wish " they ear - ring " might come to good . 
 Timothy 's Bess , though retain her maiden appellation among her 
 familiar , have long be the wife of Sandy Jim , and possess a handsome 
 set of matronly jewel , of which it be enough to mention the heavy 
 baby she be rock in her arm , and the sturdy fellow of five in 
 knee - breech , and red leg , who have a rusty milk - can round his neck by 
 way of drum , and be very carefully avoid by Chad 's small terrier . 
 this young olive - branch , notorious under the name of Timothy 's Bess 's 
 Ben , be of an inquire disposition , unchecke by any false modesty , 
 have advance beyond the group of woman and child , and be walk 
 round the Methodists , look up in their face with his mouth wide 
 open , and beat his stick against the milk - can by way of musical 
 accompaniment . but one of the elderly woman bend down to take he by 
 the shoulder , with an air of grave remonstrance , Timothy 's Bess 's Ben 
 first kick out vigorously , then take to his heel and seek refuge 
 behind his father 's leg . 
 " ye gallow young dog , " say Sandy Jim , with some paternal pride , " if 
 ye donna keep that stick quiet , I will tek it from ye . what dy'e mane by 
 kickin ' foulk ? " 
 " here ! gie he here to I , Jim , " say Chad Cranage ; " I will tie hir up 
 an ' shoe he as I do th ' hosse . well , Mester Casson , " he continue , 
 as that personage saunter up towards the group of man , " how be ye 
 t ' naight ? be ye coom t ' help groon ? they say folk allay groon when 
 they be hearkenin ' to th ' Methodys , as if they war bad I ' th ' inside . 
 I mane to groon as loud as your cow do th ' other naight , an ' then the 
 praicher ' ull think I be I ' th ' raight way . " 
 " I would advise you not to be up to no nonsense , Chad , " say Mr. Casson , 
 with some dignity ; " Poyser would not   like to hear as his wife 's niece be 
 treat any way disrespectful , for all he may not   be fond of her taking 
 on herself to preach . " 
 " Aye , an ' she be a pleasant - look un too , " say Wiry Ben . " I will stick 
 up for the pretty woman preachin ' ; I know they would persuade I over a deal 
 soon nor th ' ugly man . I shouldna wonder if I turn Methody afore the 
 night 's out , an ' begin to coort the preacher , like Seth Bede . " 
 " why , Seth 's look rether too high , I should think , " say Mr. Casson . 
 " this woman 's kin would not   like she to demean herself to a common 
 carpenter . " 
 " Tchu ! " say Ben , with a long treble intonation , " what be folk 's kin get 
 to do wi't ? not a chip . Poyser 's wife may turn her nose up an ' forget 
 bygone , but this Dinah Morris , they tell I , be as poor as iver she 
 be - work at a mill , an 's much ado to keep hersen . a strappin ' young 
 carpenter as be a ready - make Methody , like Seth , wouldna be a bad match 
 for she . why , poyser make as big a fuss wi ' Adam Bede as if he war a 
 nevvy o ' their own . " 
 " idle talk ! idle talk ! " say Mr. Joshua Rann . " Adam an ' Seth 's two man ; 
 you wunna fit they two wi ' the same last . " 
 " maybe , " say Wiry Ben , contemptuously , " but Seth 's the lad for I , 
 though he war a Methody twice o'er . I be fair beat wi ' Seth , for I have 
 be teasin ' he iver sin ' we have   be workin ' together , an ' he bear I 
 no more malice nor a lamb . an ' he be a stout - hearted feller too , for when 
 we see the old tree all afire a - comin ' across the field one night , an ' 
 we think as it war a boguy , Seth make no more ado , but he up to't 
 as bold as a constable . why , there he come out o ' will Maskery 's ; an ' 
 there be Will hisself , lookin ' as meek as if he couldna knock a nail o ' 
 the head for fear o ' hurtin't . an ' there be the pretty preacher woman ! my 
 eye , she be get her bonnet off . I mun go a bit near . " 
 several of the man follow Ben 's lead , and the traveller push his 
 horse on to the Green , as Dinah walk rather quickly and in advance of 
 her companion towards the cart under the maple - tree . while she be near 
 Seth 's tall figure , she look short , but when she have mount the cart , 
 and be away from all comparison , she seem above the middle height of 
 woman , though in reality she do not exceed it - an effect which be due 
 to the slimness of her figure and the simple line of her black stuff 
 dress . the stranger be strike with surprise as he see her approach and 
 mount the cart - surprise , not so much at the feminine delicacy of 
 her appearance , as at the total absence of self - consciousness in her 
 demeanour . he have make up his mind to see her advance with a measured 
 step and a demure solemnity of countenance ; he have feel sure that she 
 face would be mantle with the smile of conscious saintship , or 
 else charge with denunciatory bitterness . he know but two type of 
 Methodist - the ecstatic and the bilious . but Dinah walk as simply as 
 if she be go to market , and seem as unconscious of her outward 
 appearance as a little boy : there be no blush , no tremulousness , which 
 say , " I know you think I a pretty woman , too young to preach " ; no 
 cast up or down of the eyelid , no compression of the lip , no 
 attitude of the arm that say , " but you must think of I as a saint . " 
 she hold no book in her ungloved hand , but let they hang down lightly 
 cross before she , as she stand and turn her grey eye on the people . 
 there be no keenness in the eye ; they seem rather to be shed 
 love than make observation ; they have the liquid look which tell that 
 the mind be full of what it have to give out , rather than impress by 
 external object . she stand with her left hand towards the descending 
 sun , and leafy bough screen she from its ray ; but in this sober 
 light the delicate colouring of her face seem to gather a calm 
 vividness , like flower at evening . it be a small oval face , of a 
 uniform transparent whiteness , with an egg - like line of cheek and chin , 
 a full but firm mouth , a delicate nostril , and a low perpendicular brow , 
 surmount by a rise arch of parting between smooth lock of pale 
 reddish hair . the hair be draw straight back behind the ear , and 
 cover , except for an inch or two above the brow , by a net Quaker cap . 
 the eyebrow , of the same colour as the hair , be perfectly horizontal 
 and firmly pencil ; the eyelash , though no darker , be long and 
 abundant - nothing be leave blur or unfinishe . it be one of those 
 face that make one think of white flower with light touch of colour 
 on their pure petal . the eye have no peculiar beauty , beyond that of 
 expression ; they look so simple , so candid , so gravely loving , that 
 no accuse scowl , no light sneer could help melt away before their 
 glance . Joshua Rann give a long cough , as if he be clear his throat 
 in order to come to a new understanding with himself ; Chad Cranage 
 lift up his leather skull - cap and scratch his head ; and Wiry Ben 
 wonder how Seth have the pluck to think of court she . 
 " a sweet woman , " the stranger say to himself , " but surely nature never 
 mean she for a preacher . " 
 perhaps he be one of those who think that nature have theatrical 
 property and , with the considerate view of facilitate art and 
 psychology , " make up , " her character , so that there may be no mistake 
 about they . but Dinah begin to speak . 
 " dear friend , " she say in a clear but not loud voice " let we pray for 
 a blessing . " 
 she close her eye , and hang her head down a little continue in the 
 same moderate tone , as if speak to some one quite near she : " saviour 
 of sinner ! when a poor woman laden with sin , go out to the well to 
 draw water , she find Thee sit at the well . she know Thee not ; she 
 have not seek Thee ; her mind be dark ; her life be unholy . but Thou 
 didst speak to she , Thou didst teach she , Thou didst show she that her 
 life lie open before Thee , and yet Thou wast ready to give she that 
 blessing which she have never seek . Jesus , Thou art in the midst of we , 
 and Thou know all man : if there be any here like that poor woman - if 
 their mind be dark , their life unholy - if they have come out not 
 seek Thee , not desire to be teach ; deal with they accord to the 
 free mercy which Thou didst show to her Speak to they , Lord , open their 
 ear to my message , bring their sin to their mind , and make they 
 thirst for that salvation which Thou art ready to give . 
 " Lord , Thou art with Thy people still : they see Thee in the 
 night - watch , and their heart burn within they as Thou talk with 
 they by the way . and Thou art near to those who have not know Thee : 
 open their eye that they may see Thee - see Thee weep over they , 
 and say ' ye will not come unto I that ye might have life ' - see Thee 
 hang on the cross and say , ' Father , forgive they , for they know 
 not what they do ' - see Thee as Thou wilt come again in Thy glory to 
 judge they at the last . amen . " 
 Dinah open her eye again and pause , look at the group of 
 villager , who be now gather rather more closely on her right hand . 
 " dear friend , " she begin , raise her voice a little , " you have all of 
 you be to church , and I think you must have hear the clergyman 
 read these word : ' the Spirit of the Lord be upon I , because he hath 
 anoint I to preach the gospel to the poor . ' Jesus Christ speak those 
 word - he say he come to preach the GOSPEL to the POOR . I do not   know 
 whether you ever think about those word much , but I will tell you 
 when I remember first hear they . it be on just such a sort of 
 evening as this , when I be a little girl , and my aunt as bring I up 
 take I to hear a good man preach out of door , just as we be here . I 
 remember his face well : he be a very old man , and have very long white 
 hair ; his voice be very soft and beautiful , not like any voice I have 
 ever hear before . I be a little girl and scarcely know anything , and 
 this old man seem to I such a different sort of a man from anybody 
 I have ever see before that I think he have perhaps come down from 
 the sky to preach to we , and I say , ' Aunt , will he go back to the sky 
 to - night , like the picture in the Bible ? ' 
 " that man of God be Mr. Wesley , who spend his life in do what our 
 bless Lord do - preach the Gospel to the poor - and he enter into 
 his rest eight year ago . I come to know more about he year after , but 
 I be a foolish thoughtless child then , and I remember only one thing 
 he tell we in his sermon . he tell we as ' Gospel ' mean ' good news . ' the 
 Gospel , you know , be what the Bible tell we about God . 
 " think of that now ! Jesus Christ do really come down from heaven , as 
 I , like a silly child , think Mr. Wesley do ; and what he come down 
 for be to tell good news about God to the poor . why , you and I , dear 
 friend , be poor . we have be bring up in poor cottage and have 
 be rear on oat - cake , and live coarse ; and we have not   be to school 
 much , nor read book , and we do not   know much about anything but what 
 happen just round we . we be just the sort of people that want to 
 hear good news . for when anybody be well off , they do not   much mind about 
 hear news from distant part ; but if a poor man or woman 's in trouble 
 and have hard work to make out a living , they like to have a letter to 
 tell them they have   get a friend as will help them . to be sure , we can not 
 help know something about God , even if we have   never hear the Gospel , 
 the good news that our Saviour bring we . for we know everything come 
 from God : do not   you say almost every day , ' this and that will happen , 
 please God , ' and ' we shall begin to cut the grass soon , please God to 
 send we a little more sunshine ' ? we know very well we be altogether 
 in the hand of God . we do not   bring ourselves into the world , we can not 
 keep ourselves alive while we be sleep ; the daylight , and the wind , 
 and the corn , and the cow to give we milk - everything we have come 
 from God . and he give we our soul and put love between parent and 
 child , and husband and wife . but be that as much as we want to know 
 about God ? we see he be great and mighty , and can do what he will : we 
 be lose , as if we be struggle in great water , when we try to think 
 of he . 
 " but perhaps doubt come into your mind like this : can God take much 
 notice of we poor people ? perhaps he only make the world for the great 
 and the wise and the rich . it do not   cost he much to give we our 
 little handful of victual and bit of clothing ; but how do we know he 
 care for we any more than we care for the worm and thing in the 
 garden , so as we rear our carrot and onion ? will God take care of we 
 when we die ? and have he any comfort for we when we be lame and sick and 
 helpless ? perhaps , too , he be angry with we ; else why do the blight 
 come , and the bad harvest , and the fever , and all sort of pain and 
 trouble ? for our life be full of trouble , and if God send we good , he 
 seem to send bad too . how be it ? how be it ? 
 " ah , dear friend , we be in sad want of good news about God ; and what 
 do other good news signify if we have not   that ? for everything else 
 come to an end , and when we die we leave it all . but God last when 
 everything else be go . what shall we do if he be not our friend ? " 
 then Dinah tell how the good news have be bring , and how the mind 
 of God towards the poor have be make manifest in the life of Jesus , 
 dwell on its lowliness and its act of mercy . 
 " so you see , dear friend , " she go on , " Jesus spend his time almost 
 all in do good to poor people ; he preach out of door to they , and 
 he make friend of poor workman , and teach they and take pain with 
 they . not but what he do good to the rich too , for he be full of love 
 to all man , only he see as the poor be more in want of his help . so 
 he cure the lame and the sick and the blind , and he work miracle to 
 feed the hungry because , he say , he be sorry for they ; and he be 
 very kind to the little child and comfort those who have lose their 
 friend ; and he speak very tenderly to poor sinner that be sorry for 
 their sin . 
 " ah , would not   you love such a man if you see he - if he be here in 
 this village ? what a kind heart he must have ! what a friend he would be 
 to go to in trouble ! how pleasant it must be to be teach by he . 
 " well , dear friend , who be this man ? be he only a good man - a very 
 good man , and no more - like our dear Mr. Wesley , who have be take from 
 us? ... he be the Son of God - ' in the image of the Father , ' the Bible 
 say ; that mean , just like God , who be the beginning and end of all 
 thing - the God we want to know about . so then , all the love that 
 Jesus show to the poor be the same love that God have for we . we can 
 understand what Jesus feel , because he come in a body like ours and 
 speak word such as we speak to each other . we be afraid to think what 
 God be before - the God who make the world and the sky and the thunder 
 and lightning . we could never see he ; we could only see the thing he 
 have make ; and some of these thing be very terrible , so as we might 
 well tremble when we think of he . but our blessed Saviour have show 
 we what God be in a way we poor ignorant people can understand ; he have 
 show we what God 's heart be , what be his feeling towards we . 
 " but let we see a little more about what Jesus come on earth for . 
 another time he say , ' I come to seek and to save that which be lose ' ; 
 and another time , ' I come not to call the righteous but sinner to 
 repentance . ' 
 " the LOST! ... SINNERS! ... ah , dear friend , do that mean you and I ? " 
 Hitherto the traveller have be chain to the spot against his will 
 by the charm of Dinah 's mellow treble tone , which have a variety of 
 modulation like that of a fine instrument touch with the unconscious 
 skill of musical instinct . the simple thing she say seem like 
 novelty , as a melody strike we with a new feeling when we hear 
 it sing by the pure voice of a boyish chorister ; the quiet depth of 
 conviction with which she speak seem in itself an evidence for the 
 truth of her message . he see that she have thoroughly arrest her 
 hearer . the villager have press nearer to she , and there be no 
 long anything but grave attention on all face . she speak slowly , 
 though quite fluently , often pause after a question , or before any 
 transition of idea . there be no change of attitude , no gesture ; the 
 effect of her speech be produce entirely by the inflection of her 
 voice , and when she come to the question , " will God take care of we 
 when we die ? " she utter it in such a tone of plaintive appeal that 
 the tear come into some of the hard eye . the stranger have cease 
 to doubt , as he have do at the first glance , that she could fix the 
 attention of her rougher hearer , but still he wonder whether she 
 could have that power of rouse their more violent emotion , which 
 must surely be a necessary seal of her vocation as a Methodist preacher , 
 until she come to the word , " Lost ! - Sinners ! " when there be a great 
 change in her voice and manner . she have make a long pause before the 
 exclamation , and the pause seem to be fill by agitate thought 
 that show themselves in her feature . her pale face become paler ; 
 the circle under her eye deepen , as they do when tear half - gather 
 without fall ; and the mild love eye take an expression of appalled 
 pity , as if she have suddenly discern a destroy angel hover over 
 the head of the people . her voice become deep and muffle , but there 
 be still no gesture . nothing could be less like the ordinary type of 
 the Ranter than Dinah . she be not preach as she hear other preach , 
 but speak directly from her own emotion and under the inspiration of 
 her own simple faith . 
 but now she have enter into a new current of feeling . her manner become 
 less calm , her utterance more rapid and agitated , as she try to bring 
 home to the people their guilt their wilful darkness , their state of 
 disobedience to God - as she dwelt on the hatefulness of sin , the Divine 
 holiness , and the suffering of the Saviour , by which a way have be 
 open for their salvation . at last it seem as if , in her yearning 
 desire to reclaim the lost sheep , she could not be satisfy by 
 address her hearer as a body . she appeal first to one and then to 
 another , beseech they with tear to turn to God while there be 
 yet time ; paint to they the desolation of their soul , lose in sin , 
 feed on the husk of this miserable world , far away from God their 
 Father ; and then the love of the Saviour , who be wait and watch 
 for their return . 
 there be many a responsive sigh and groan from her fellow - methodist , 
 but the village mind do not easily take fire , and a little smoulder 
 vague anxiety that might easily die out again be the utmost effect 
 Dinah 's preaching have work in they at present . yet no one have 
 retire , except the child and " old Feyther Taft , " who be too deaf 
 to catch many word , have some time ago go back to his inglenook . Wiry 
 Ben be feel very uncomfortable , and almost wish he have not come 
 to hear Dinah ; he think what she say would haunt he somehow . yet he 
 could not   help like to look at she and listen to she , though he dread 
 every moment that she would fix her eye on he and address he in 
 particular . she have already address Sandy Jim , who be now hold the 
 baby to relieve his wife , and the big soft - hearted man have rub away 
 some tear with his fist , with a confused intention of be a well 
 fellow , go less to the Holly Bush down by the stone - pit , and 
 clean himself more regularly of a Sunday . 
 in front of Sandy Jim stand Chad 's Bess , who have show an unwonted 
 quietude and fixity of attention ever since Dinah have begin to speak . 
 not that the matter of the discourse have arrest she at once , for she 
 be lose in a puzzling speculation as to what pleasure and satisfaction 
 there could be in life to a young woman who wear a cap like Dinah 's . 
 give up this inquiry in despair , she take to study Dinah 's nose , 
 eye , mouth , and hair , and wonder whether it be well to have such 
 a sort of pale face as that , or fat red cheek and round black eye like 
 her own . but gradually the influence of the general gravity tell upon 
 she , and she become conscious of what Dinah be say . the gentle 
 tone , the love persuasion , do not touch she , but when the more 
 severe appeal come she begin to be frightened . Poor Bessy have always 
 be consider a naughty girl ; she be conscious of it ; if it be 
 necessary to be very good , it be clear she must be in a bad way . she 
 could not   find her place at church as Sally Rann could , she have often 
 be titter when she " curcheye " to Mr. Irwine ; and these religious 
 deficiency be accompany by a corresponding slackness in the minor 
 moral , for Bessy belong unquestionably to that unsoaped lazy class of 
 feminine character with whom you may venture to " eat an egg , an apple , 
 or a nut . " all this she be generally conscious of , and hitherto have not 
 be greatly ashamed of it . but now she begin to feel very much as if 
 the constable have come to take she up and carry she before the justice 
 for some undefined offence . she have a terrified sense that God , whom she 
 have always think of as very far off , be very near to she , and that 
 Jesus be close by look at she , though she could not see he . for 
 Dinah have that belief in visible manifestation of Jesus , which be 
 common among the Methodists , and she communicate it irresistibly to her 
 hearer : she make they feel that he be among they bodily , and might at 
 any moment show himself to they in some way that would strike anguish 
 and penitence into their heart . 
 " see ! " she exclaim , turn to the left , with her eye fix on a 
 point above the head of the people . " see where our blessed Lord stand 
 and weep and stretch out his arm towards you . hear what he say : 
 ' how often would I have gather you as a hen gathereth her chicken 
 under her wing , and ye would not!' ... and ye would not , " she repeat , 
 in a tone of plead reproach , turn her eye on the people again . 
 " see the print of the nail on his dear hand and foot . it be your sin 
 that make they ! ah ! how pale and wear he look ! he have go through all 
 that great agony in the garden , when his soul be exceed sorrowful 
 even unto death , and the great drop of sweat fall like blood to the 
 ground . they spit upon he and buffet he , they scourge he , they 
 mock he , they lay the heavy cross on his bruised shoulder . then 
 they nail he up . ah , what pain ! his lip be parch with thirst , and 
 they mock he still in this great agony ; yet with those parch lip he 
 pray for they , ' Father , forgive they , for they know not what they do . ' 
 then a horror of great darkness fall upon he , and he feel what sinner 
 feel when they be for ever shut out from God . that be the last drop 
 in the cup of bitterness . ' my God , my God ! ' he cry , ' why hast Thou 
 forsake I ? ' 
 " all this he bear for you ! for you - and you never think of he ; for 
 you - and you turn your back on he ; you do not   care what he have go 
 through for you . yet he be not weary of toil for you : he have rise 
 from the dead , he be pray for you at the right hand of God - ' Father , 
 forgive they , for they know not what they do . ' and he be upon this earth 
 too ; he be among we ; he be there close to you now ; I see his wound 
 body and his look of love . " 
 here Dinah turn to Bessy Cranage , whose bonny youth and evident vanity 
 have touch she with pity . 
 " poor child ! poor child ! he be beseech you , and you do not   listen to 
 he . you think of ear - ring and fine gown and cap , and you never think 
 of the Saviour who die to save your precious soul . your cheek will be 
 shrivel one day , your hair will be grey , your poor body will be thin 
 and tottering ! then you will begin to feel that your soul be not save ; 
 then you will have to stand before God dress in your sin , in your 
 evil temper and vain thought . and Jesus , who stand ready to help you 
 now , will not   help you then ; because you will not   have he to be your Saviour , 
 he will be your judge . now he look at you with love and mercy and say , 
 ' come to I that you may have life ' ; then he will turn away from you , 
 and say , ' depart from I into ever - last fire ! ' " 
 Poor Bessy 's wide - open black eye begin to fill with tear , her great 
 red cheek and lip become quite pale , and her face be distort like a 
 little child 's before a burst of cry . 
 " ah , poor blind child ! " Dinah go on , " think if it should happen to you 
 as it once happen to a servant of God in the day of her vanity . she 
 think of her lace cap and save all her money to buy them ; she think 
 nothing about how she might get a clean heart and a right spirit - she 
 only want to have well lace than other girl . and one day when she 
 put her new cap on and look in the glass , she see a bleed face 
 crown with thorn . that face be look at you now " - here Dinah 
 point to a spot close in front of Bessy - " ah , tear off those folly ! 
 cast they away from you , as if they be sting adder . they be 
 sting you - they be poison your soul - they be drag you down 
 into a dark bottomless pit , where you will sink for ever , and for ever , 
 and for ever , far away from light and God . " 
 Bessy could bear it no long : a great terror be upon she , and 
 wrench her ear - ring from her ear , she throw they down before she , 
 sob aloud . her father , Chad , frighten lest he should be " lay hold 
 on " too , this impression on the rebellious Bess strike he as nothing 
 less than a miracle , walk hastily away and begin to work at his anvil 
 by way of reassure himself . " folk mun ha ' hoss - shoe , praichin ' or 
 no praichin ' : the divil canna lie hould o ' I for that , " he mutter to 
 himself . 
 but now Dinah begin to tell of the joy that be in store for the 
 penitent , and to describe in her simple way the divine peace and love 
 with which the soul of the believer be fill - how the sense of God 's 
 love turn poverty into rich and satisfie the soul so that no uneasy 
 desire vexe it , no fear alarm it : how , at last , the very temptation 
 to sin be extinguish , and heaven be begin upon earth , because no cloud 
 pass between the soul and God , who be its eternal sun . 
 " dear friend , " she say at last , " brother and sister , whom I love 
 as those for whom my Lord have die , believe I , I know what this great 
 blessedness be ; and because I know it , I want you to have it too . I be 
 poor , like you : I have to get my living with my hand ; but no lord nor 
 lady can be so happy as I , if they have not   get the love of God in their 
 soul . think what it be - not to hate anything but sin ; to be full of 
 love to every creature ; to be frighten at nothing ; to be sure that all 
 thing will turn to good ; not to mind pain , because it be our Father 's 
 will ; to know that nothing - no , not if the earth be to be burn up , or 
 the water come and drown we - nothing could part we from God who love 
 we , and who fill our soul with peace and joy , because we be sure that 
 whatever he will be holy , just , and good . 
 " dear friend , come and take this blessedness ; it be offer to you ; it 
 be the good news that Jesus come to preach to the poor . it be not like 
 the rich of this world , so that the more one get the less the rest 
 can have . God be without end ; his love be without end - " 
 its stream the whole creation reach , 
 so plenteous be the store ; 
 enough for all , enough for each , 
 enough for evermore . 
 Dinah have be speak at least an hour , and the redden light of the 
 parting day seem to give a solemn emphasis to her closing word . the 
 stranger , who have be interested in the course of her sermon as if 
 it have be the development of a drama - for there be this sort of 
 fascination in all sincere unpremeditated eloquence , which open to one

 friend " ; and as he be still wind down the slope , the voice of the 
 Methodists reach he , rise and fall in that strange blending of 
 exultation and sadness which belong to the cadence of a hymn . 
 Chapter III 
 after the Preaching

 caress , our tender word , our still rapture under the influence

 have be give to too many humble craftsman since the world begin for

 hip and haw of the Cornwall hedge , after exhaust limb and lung

 that afterglow have long fade away ; and the picture we be apt to make

 Presence , sweet as summer to the houseless needy . it be too possible

 but of a very old - fashioned kind . they believe in present miracle , in

 hope , and charity have not always be find in a direct ratio with a 
 sensibility to the three concord , and it be possible - thank Heaven ! - to

 which clumsy Molly spare from her own scanty store that she may carry

 inefficacious remedy ; but the generous stirring of neighbourly kindness 
 that prompt the deed have a beneficent radiation that be not lose . 
 consider these thing , we can hardly think Dinah and Seth beneath our

 Chapter IV 
 Home and its sorrow 
 a green valley with a brook run through it , full almost to

 turnin ' his earnin 's into wood for father , when he be get plenty o ' us

 settle before now . he will never turn round and knock down his own work , 
 and forsake they as it be be the labour of his life to stand by . " 
 " Donna talk to I about 's marr'in ' , " say Lisbeth , cry afresh . " he be 
 set 's heart on that Hetty Sorrel , as ' ull niver save a penny , an ' ' ull 
 toss up her head at 's old mother . an ' to think as he might ha ' Mary 
 Burge , an ' be take partner , an ' be a big man wi ' workman under he , 
 like Mester Burge - Dolly 's tell I so o'er and o'er again - if it warna 
 as he be set 's heart on that bit of a wench , as be o ' no more use nor the 
 gillyflower on the wall . an ' he so wise at bookin ' an ' figurin ' , an ' not 
 to know no well nor that ! " 
 " but , Mother , thee know'st we canna love just where other folk ' ud have 
 we . there be nobody but God can control the heart of man . I could ha ' 
 wish myself as Adam could ha ' make another choice , but I would not 
 reproach he for what he can not   help . and I be not sure but what he try 
 to o'ercome it . but it be a matter as he do not   like to be speak to 
 about , and I can only pray to the Lord to bless and direct he . " 
 " Aye , thee't allay ready enough at prayin ' , but I donna see as thee 
 get much wi ' thy prayin ' . Thee wotna get double earnin 's o ' this side 
 Yule . Th ' Methodies will niver make thee half the man thy brother be , for 
 all they be a - makin ' a preacher on thee . " 
 " it be partly truth thee speak'st there , Mother , " say Seth , mildly ; 
 " Adam 's far before I , an 's do more for I than I can ever do for he . 
 God distribute talent to every man accord as he see good . but thee 
 mustna undervally prayer . prayer mayna bring money , but it bring we 
 what no money can buy - a power to keep from sin and be content with 
 God 's will , whatever he may please to send . if thee wouldst pray to God 
 to help thee , and trust in his goodness , thee wouldstna be so uneasy 
 about thing . " 
 " Unaisy ? I be I ' th ' right on't   to be unaisy . it be well see on THEE what 
 it be niver to be unaisy . thee't gi ' away all thy earnin 's , an ' niver be 
 unaisy as thee'st nothing lay up again ' a rainy day . if Adam have be 
 as aisy as thee , he would niver ha ' have no money to pay for thee . take 
 no thought for the morrow - take no thought - that be what thee't allay 
 sayin ' ; an ' what come on't ? why , as Adam have to take thought for thee . " 
 " those be the word o ' the Bible , Mother , " say Seth . " they do not 
 mean as we should be idle . they mean we should not   be overanxious and 
 worrete ourselves about what will happen to - morrow , but do our duty and 
 leave the rest to God 's will . " 
 " aye , aye , that be the way wi ' thee : thee allay make a peck o ' thy own 
 word out o ' a pint o ' the Bible 's . I donna see how thee't to know as 
 ' take no thought for the morrow ' mean all that . an ' when the Bible 's 
 such a big book , an ' thee canst read all thro't , an ' ha ' the pick o ' the 
 texe , I canna think why thee dostna pick well word as donna mean so 
 much more nor they say . Adam doesna pick a that'n ; I can understan ' the 
 tex as he be allay a - sayin ' , ' God help they as help theirsen . ' " 
 " Nay , Mother , " say Seth , " that be no text o ' the Bible . it come out of 
 a book as Adam pick up at the stall at Treddles'on . it be write by 
 a knowing man , but overworldly , I doubt . however , that say 's partly 
 true ; for the Bible tell we we must be worker together with God . " 
 " well , how'm I to know ? it sound like a tex . but what be th ' matter wi ' 
 th ' lad ? thee't hardly atin ' a bit o ' supper . Dostna mean to ha ' no more 
 nor that bit o ' oat - cake ? an ' thee lookst as white as a flick o ' new 
 bacon . what be th ' matter wi ' thee ? " 
 " nothing to mind about , Mother ; I be not hungry . I will just look in at 
 Adam again , and see if he will let I go on with the coffin . " 
 " ha ' a drop o ' warm broth ? " say Lisbeth , whose motherly feeling now get 
 the well of her " nattering " habit . " I will set two - three stick a - light 
 in a minute . " 
 " Nay , Mother , thank thee ; thee't very good , " say Seth , gratefully ; and 
 encourage by this touch of tenderness , he go on : " let I pray a 
 bit with thee for Father , and Adam , and all of we - it will comfort thee , 
 happen , more than thee thinkst . " 
 " well , I have   nothing to say again ' it . " 
 Lisbeth , though dispose always to take the negative side in her 
 conversation with Seth , have a vague sense that there be some comfort 
 and safety in the fact of his piety , and that it somehow relieve she 
 from the trouble of any spiritual transaction on her own behalf . 
 so the mother and son kneel down together , and Seth pray for the poor 
 wandering father and for those who be sorrow for he at home . and 
 when he come to the petition that Adam might never be call to set 
 up his tent in a far country , but that his mother might be cheer and 
 comfort by his presence all the day of her pilgrimage , Lisbeth 's 
 ready tear flow again , and she weep aloud . 
 when they rise from their knee , Seth go to Adam again and say , " Wilt 
 only lie down for an hour or two , and let I go on the while ? " 
 " no , Seth , no . make Mother go to bed , and go thyself . " 
 Meantime Lisbeth have dry her eye , and now follow Seth , hold 
 something in her hand . it be the brown - and - yellow platter contain 
 the baked potato with the gravy in they and bit of meat which she have 
 cut and mix among they . those be dear time , when wheaten bread 
 and fresh meat be delicacy to work people . she set the dish down 
 rather timidly on the bench by Adam 's side and say , " thee canst pick a 
 bit while thee't workin ' . I will bring thee another drop o ' water . " 
 " Aye , Mother , do , " say Adam , kindly ; " I be get very thirsty . " 
 in half an hour all be quiet ; no sound be to be hear in the house but 
 the loud ticking of the old day - clock and the ringing of Adam 's tool . 
 the night be very still : when Adam open the door to look out at 
 twelve o'clock , the only motion seem to be in the glowing , twinkle 
 star ; every blade of grass be asleep . 
 bodily haste and exertion usually leave our thought very much at the 
 mercy of our feeling and imagination ; and it be so to - night with Adam . 
 while his muscle be work lustily , his mind seem as passive as a 
 spectator at a diorama : scene of the sad past , and probably sad 
 future , float before he and give place one to the other in swift 
 succession . 
 he see how it would be to - morrow morning , when he have carry the coffin 
 to Broxton and be at home again , have his breakfast : his father 
 perhaps would come in ashamed to meet his son 's glance - would sit down , 
 look old and more tottering than he have do the morning before , 
 and hang down his head , examine the floor - quarry ; while Lisbeth 
 would ask he how he suppose the coffin have be get ready , that he have 
 slink off and leave undo - for Lisbeth be always the first to utter 
 the word of reproach , although she cry at Adam 's severity towards his 
 father . 
 " so it will go on , worsen and worsen , " think Adam ; " there be no 
 slip uphill again , and no standing still when once you ' ve   begin 
 to slip down . " and then the day come back to he when he be a little 
 fellow and use to run by his father 's side , proud to be take out 
 to work , and prouder still to hear his father boast to his 
 fellow - workman how " the little chap have an uncommon notion o ' 
 carpentering . " what a fine active fellow his father be then ! when 
 people ask Adam whose little lad he be , he have a sense of distinction

 know Thias Bede - do not   he make the wonderful pigeon - house at Broxton 
 parsonage ? those be happy day , especially when Seth , who be three 
 year the young , begin to go out work too , and Adam begin to be a 
 teacher as well as a learner . but then come the day of sadness , when 
 Adam be someway on in his teen , and Thias begin to loiter at the 
 public - house , and Lisbeth begin to cry at home , and to pour forth her 
 plaint in the hearing of her son . Adam remember well the night of 
 shame and anguish when he first see his father quite wild and foolish , 
 shout a song out fitfully among his drunken companion at the " Waggon 
 Overthrown . " he have run away once when he be only eighteen , make 
 his escape in the morning twilight with a little blue bundle over 
 his shoulder , and his " mensuration book " in his pocket , and say 
 to himself very decidedly that he could bear the vexation of home no 
 long - he would go and seek his fortune , set up his stick at the 
 crossway and bend his step the way it fall . but by the time he get 
 to Stoniton , the thought of his mother and Seth , leave behind to endure 
 everything without he , become too importunate , and his resolution 
 fail he . he come back the next day , but the misery and terror his 
 mother have go through in those two day have haunt she ever since . 
 " no ! " Adam say to himself to - night , " that must never happen again . it 
 ' ud make a poor balance when my doing be cast up at the last , if my 
 poor old mother stand o ' the wrong side . my back 's broad enough and 
 strong enough ; I should be no well than a coward to go away and leave 
 the trouble to be bear by they as be not   half so able . ' they that be 
 strong ought to bear the infirmity of those that be weak , and not to 
 please themselves . ' there be a text want no candle to show't ; it shin 
 by its own light . it be plain enough you get into the wrong road I ' this 
 life if you run after this and that only for the sake o ' make thing 
 easy and pleasant to yourself . a pig may poke his nose into the trough 
 and think o ' nothing outside it ; but if you have   get a man 's heart and 
 soul in you , you can not   be easy a - make your own bed an ' leave the 
 rest to lie on the stone . Nay , nay , I will never slip my neck out o ' the 
 yoke , and leave the load to be draw by the weak uns . Father 's a sore 
 cross to I , an 's likely to be for many a long year to come . what then ? 
 I have   get th ' health , and the limb , and the sperrit to bear it . " 
 at this moment a smart rap , as if with a willow wand , be give at the 
 house door , and Gyp , instead of bark , as might have be expect , 
 give a loud howl . Adam , very much startle , go at once to the door 
 and open it . nothing be there ; all be still , as when he open it 
 an hour before ; the leaf be motionless , and the light of the star 
 show the placid field on both side of the brook quite empty of 
 visible life . Adam walk round the house , and still see nothing except

 wonder ; the sound be so peculiar that the moment he hear it it 
 call up the image of the willow wand strike the door . he could not 
 help a little shudder , as he remember how often his mother have tell 
 he of just such a sound come as a sign when some one be die . Adam 
 be not a man to be gratuitously superstitious , but he have the blood of 
 the peasant in he as well as of the artisan , and a peasant can no 
 more help believe in a traditional superstition than a horse can help 
 tremble when he see a camel . besides , he have that mental combination 
 which be at once humble in the region of mystery and keen in the region 
 of knowledge : it be the depth of his reverence quite as much as 
 his hard common sense which give he his disinclination to doctrinal 
 religion , and he often check Seth 's argumentative spiritualism by 
 say , " eh , it be a big mystery ; thee know'st but little about it . " and

 new building have fall down and he have be tell that this be a divine
