 wall be not   right , else it would not   ha ' come down " ; yet he believe 
 in dream and prognostic , and to his die day he bat his breath a 
 little when he tell the story of the stroke with the willow wand . I 
 tell it as he tell it , not attempt to reduce it to its natural 
 element - in our eagerness to explain impression , we often lose our 
 hold of the sympathy that comprehend they . 
 but he have the good antidote against imaginative dread in the necessity 
 for get on with the coffin , and for the next ten minute his hammer 
 be ring so uninterruptedly , that other sound , if there be any , 
 might well be overpower . a pause come , however , when he have to take 
 up his ruler , and now again come the strange rap , and again Gyp howl . 
 Adam be at the door without the loss of a moment ; but again all be 
 still , and the starlight show there be nothing but the dew - laden 
 grass in front of the cottage . 
 Adam for a moment think uncomfortably about his father ; but of late 
 year he have never come home at dark hour from Treddleston , and 
 there be every reason for believe that he be then sleep off his 
 drunkenness at the " Waggon Overthrown . " besides , to Adam , the conception 
 of the future be so inseparable from the painful image of his father 
 that the fear of any fatal accident to he be exclude by the deeply 
 infixed fear of his continual degradation . the next thought that 
 occur to he be one that make he slip off his shoe and tread 
 lightly upstairs , to listen at the bedroom door . but both Seth and his 
 mother be breathe regularly . 
 Adam come down and set to work again , say to himself , " I will not   open 
 the door again . it be no use star about to catch sight of a sound . 
 maybe there be a world about we as we can not   see , but th ' ear 's quick 
 than the eye and catch a sound from't now and then . some people think 
 they get a sight on't   too , but they be mostly folk whose eye be not 
 much use to them at anything else . for my part , I think it be well to 
 see when your perpendicular 's true than to see a ghost . " 
 such thought as these be apt to grow strong and strong as daylight 
 quench the candle and the bird begin to sing . by the time the red 
 sunlight shine on the brass nail that form the initial on the lid of 
 the coffin , any linger foreboding from the sound of the willow 
 wand be merge in satisfaction that the work be do and the promise 
 redeem . there be no need to call Seth , for he be already move 
 overhead , and presently come downstairs . 
 " now , lad , " say Adam , as Seth make his appearance , " the coffin 's do , 
 and we can take it over to Brox'on , and be back again before half after 
 six . I will take a mouthful o ' oat - cake , and then we will be off . " 
 the coffin be soon prop on the tall shoulder of the two brother , 
 and they be make their way , follow close by Gyp , out of the little 
 woodyard into the lane at the back of the house . it be but about a mile 
 and a half to Broxton over the opposite slope , and their road wound very 
 pleasantly along lane and across field , where the pale woodbine and 
 the dog - rose be scent the hedgerow , and the bird be twitter 
 and trill in the tall leafy bough of oak and elm . it be a strangely 
 mingle picture - the fresh youth of the summer morning , with its 
 Edenlike peace and loveliness , the stalwart strength of the two brother 
 in their rusty working clothe , and the long coffin on their shoulder . 
 they pause for the last time before a small farmhouse outside the 
 village of Broxton . by six o'clock the task be do the coffin nail 
 down , and Adam and Seth be on their way home . they choose a short 
 way homeward , which would take they across the field and the brook in 
 front of the house . Adam have not mention to Seth what have happen in 
 the night , but he still retain sufficient impression from it himself 
 to say , " Seth , lad , if Father be not   come home by the time we have   have our 
 breakfast , I think it will be as well for thee to go over to Treddles'on 
 and look after he , and thee canst get I the brass wire I want . never 
 mind about lose an hour at thy work ; we can make that up . what dost 
 say ? " 
 " I be willing , " say Seth . " but see what cloud have gather since we 
 set out . I be think we shall have more rain . it will be a sore time for 
 th ' haymake if the meadow be flood again . the brook 's fine and 
 full now : another day 's rain ' ud cover the plank , and we should have to 
 go round by the road . " 
 they be come across the valley now , and have enter the pasture 
 through which the brook run . 
 " why , what be that stick against the willow ? " continue Seth , 
 begin to walk fast . Adam 's heart rise to his mouth : the vague 
 anxiety about his father be change into a great dread . he make no 
 answer to Seth , but run forward precede by Gyp , who begin to bark 
 uneasily ; and in two moment he be at the bridge . 
 this be what the omen mean , then ! and the grey - haired father , of whom 
 he have think with a sort of hardness a few hour ago , as certain to 
 live to be a thorn in his side be perhaps even then struggle with 
 that watery death ! this be the first thought that flash through 
 Adam 's conscience , before he have time to seize the coat and drag out 
 the tall heavy body . Seth be already by his side , help he , and 
 when they have it on the bank , the two son in the first moment knelt and 
 look with mute awe at the glaze eye , forget that there be need 
 for action - forget everything but that their father lie dead before 
 they . Adam be the first to speak . 
 " I will run to Mother , " he say , in a loud whisper . " I will be back to thee 
 in a minute . " 
 Poor Lisbeth be busy prepare her son ' breakfast , and their porridge 
 be already steam on the fire . her kitchen always look the pink of 
 cleanliness , but this morning she be more than usually bent on make 
 her hearth and breakfast - table look comfortable and inviting . 
 " the lad ' ull be fine an ' hungry , " she say , half - aloud , as she stir 
 the porridge . " it be a good step to Brox'on , an ' it be hungry air o'er 
 the hill - wi ' that heavy coffin too . eh ! it be heavy now , wi ' poor Bob 
 Tholer in't . Howiver , I have   make a drap more porridge nor common this 
 mornin ' . the feyther ' ull happen come in arter a bit . not as he will ate 
 much porridge . he swaller sixpenn'orth o ' ale , an ' save a hap'orth o ' 
 por - ridge - that be his way o ' layin ' by money , as I have   tell he many a 
 time , an ' am likely to tell he again afore the day 's out . eh , poor mon , 
 he take it quiet enough ; there be no denyin ' that . " 
 but now Lisbeth hear the heavy " thud " of a running footstep on the 
 turf , and , turn quickly towards the door , she see Adam enter , look 
 so pale and overwhelm that she scream aloud and rush towards he 
 before he have time to speak . 
 " Hush , mother , " Adam say , rather hoarsely , " do not   be frighten . 
 Father 's tumble into the water . belike we may bring he round again . 
 Seth and I be go to carry he in . get a blanket and make it hot as 
 the fire . " 
 in reality Adam be convince that his father be dead but he know there 
 be no other way of repress his mother 's impetuous wail grief than 
 by occupy she with some active task which have hope in it . 
 he run back to Seth , and the two son lift the sad burden in 
 heart - stricken silence . the wide - open glaze eye be grey , like 
 Seth 's , and have once look with mild pride on the boy before whom 
 Thias have live to hang his head in shame . Seth 's chief feeling be awe 
 and distress at this sudden snatch away of his father 's soul ; but 
 Adam 's mind rush back over the past in a flood of relent and pity . 
 when death , the great Reconciler , have come , it be never our tenderness 
 that we repent of , but our severity . 
 chapter V 
 the Rector 
 before twelve o'clock there have be some heavy storm of rain , and the 
 water lie in deep gutter on the side of the gravel walk in the garden 
 of Broxton Parsonage ; the great Provence rose have be cruelly toss 
 by the wind and beat by the rain , and all the delicate - stem border 
 flower have be dash down and stain with the wet soil . a melancholy 
 morning - because it be nearly time hay - harvest should begin , and 
 instead of that the meadow be likely to be flood . 
 but people who have pleasant home get indoor enjoyment that they would 
 never think of but for the rain . if it have not be a wet morning , Mr. 
 Irwine would not have be in the dining - room playing at chess with his 
 mother , and he love both his mother and chess quite well enough to pass 
 some cloudy hour very easily by their help . let I take you into that 
 dining - room and show you the Rev. Adolphus Irwine , Rector of Broxton , 
 Vicar of Hayslope , and Vicar of Blythe , a pluralist at whom the severe 
 Church reformer would have find it difficult to look sour . we will 
 enter very softly and stand still in the open doorway , without awake 
 the glossy - brown setter who be stretch across the hearth , with her 
 two puppy beside she ; or the pug , who be doze , with his black muzzle 
 aloft , like a sleepy president . 
 the room be a large and lofty one , with an ample mullione oriel window 
 at one end ; the wall , you see , be new , and not yet paint ; but the 
 furniture , though originally of an expensive sort , be old and scanty , 
 and there be no drapery about the window . the crimson cloth over the 
 large dining - table be very threadbare , though it contrast pleasantly 
 enough with the dead hue of the plaster on the wall ; but on this cloth 
 there be a massive silver waiter with a decanter of water on it , of the 
 same pattern as two large one that be prop up on the sideboard 
 with a coat of arm conspicuous in their centre . you suspect at once 
 that the inhabitant of this room have inherit more blood than wealth , 
 and would not be surprised to find that Mr. Irwine have a finely cut 
 nostril and upper lip ; but at present we can only see that he have a 
 broad flat back and an abundance of powdered hair , all throw backward 
 and tie behind with a black ribbon - a bit of conservatism in costume 
 which tell you that he be not a young man . he will perhaps turn round 
 by and by , and in the meantime we can look at that stately old lady , his 
 mother , a beautiful aged brunette , whose rich - toned complexion be well 
 set off by the complex wrapping of pure white cambric and lace about 
 her head and neck . she be as erect in she comely embonpoint as a statue 
 of Ceres ; and her dark face , with its delicate aquiline nose , firm proud 
 mouth , and small , intense , black eye , be so keen and sarcastic in its 
 expression that you instinctively substitute a pack of card for the 
 chess - man and imagine she tell your fortune . the small brown hand 
 with which she be lift her queen be laden with pearl , diamond , and 
 turquoise ; and a large black veil be very carefully adjust over the 
 crown of her cap , and fall in sharp contrast on the white fold 
 about her neck . it must take a long time to dress that old lady in the 
 morning ! but it seem a law of nature that she should be dress so : she 
 be clearly one of those child of royalty who have never doubt their 
 right divine and never meet with any one so absurd as to question it . 
 " there , Dauphin , tell I what that be ! " say this magnificent old lady , 
 as she deposit her queen very quietly and fold her arm . " I should be 
 sorry to utter a word disagreeable to your feeling . " 
 " ah , you witch - mother , you sorceress ! how be a christian man to win a 
 game off you ? I should have sprinkle the board with holy water before 
 we begin . you have   not win that game by fair mean , now , so do not   pretend 
 it . " 
 " yes , yes , that be what the beat have always say of great conqueror . 
 but see , there be the sunshine fall on the board , to show you more 
 clearly what a foolish move you make with that pawn . come , shall I give 
 you another chance ? " 
 " no , Mother , I shall leave you to your own conscience , now it be clearing 
 up . we must go and plash up the mud a little , mus'n't   we , Juno ? " this 
 be address to the brown setter , who have jump up at the sound of the 
 voice and lay her nose in an insinuating way on her master 's leg . " but 
 I must go upstairs first and see Anne . I be call away to Tholer 's 
 funeral just when I be go before . " 
 " it be of no use , child ; she can not   speak to you . Kate say she have one of 
 her bad headache this morning . " 
 " oh , she like I to go and see she just the same ; she be never too ill 
 to care about that . " 
 if you know how much of human speech be mere purposeless impulse or 
 habit , you will not wonder when I tell you that this identical objection 
 have be make , and have receive the same kind of answer , many hundred 
 time in the course of the fifteen year that Mr. Irwine 's sister Anne 
 have be an invalid . splendid old lady , who take a long time to dress 
 in the morning , have often slight sympathy with sickly daughter . 
 but while Mr. Irwine be still seat , lean back in his chair and 
 stroke Juno 's head , the servant come to the door and say , " if 
 you please , sir , Joshua Rann wish to speak with you , if you be at 
 liberty . " 
 " let he be show in here , " say Mrs. Irwine , take up her knitting . 
 " I always like to hear what Mr. Rann have get to say . his shoe will be 
 dirty , but see that he wipe they Carroll . " 
 in two minute Mr. Rann appear at the door with very deferential bow , 
 which , however , be far from conciliate Pug , who give a sharp bark 
 and run across the room to reconnoitre the stranger 's leg ; while the 
 two puppy , regard Mr. Rann 's prominent calf and rib worsted 
 stocking from a more sensuous point of view , plunge and growl over 
 they in great enjoyment . meantime , Mr. Irwine turn round his chair and 
 say , " well , Joshua , anything the matter at Hayslope , that you have   come 
 over this damp morning ? sit down , sit down . never mind the dog ; give 
 they a friendly kick . here , Pug , you rascal ! " 
 it be very pleasant to see some man turn round ; pleasant as a sudden 
 rush of warm air in winter , or the flash of firelight in the chill dusk . 
 Mr. Irwine be one of those man . he bear the same sort of resemblance to 
 his mother that our love memory of a friend 's face often bear to the 
 face itself : the line be all more generous , the smile brighter , the 
 expression heartier . if the outline have be less finely cut , his face 
 might have be call jolly ; but that be not the right word for its 
 mixture of bonhomie and distinction . 
 " thank your reverence , " answer Mr. Rann , endeavour to look 
 unconcerned about his leg , but shake they alternately to keep off the 
 puppy ; " I will stand , if you please , as more become . I hope I see you 
 an ' Mrs. Irwine well , an ' Miss Irwine - an ' Miss Anne , I hope 's as well 
 as usual . " 
 " yes , Joshua , thank you . you see how bloom my mother look . she beat 
 we young people hollow . but what be the matter ? " 
 " why , sir , I have to come to Brox'on to deliver some work , and I think 
 it but right to call and let you know the goin - on as there be be I ' 
 the village , such as I hanna see I ' my time , and I have   live in it man 
 and boy sixty year come St. Thomas , and collect th ' Easter due for 
 Mr. Blick before your Reverence come into the parish , and be at the 
 ringin ' o ' every bell , and the diggin ' o ' every grave , and sing I ' the 
 choir long afore Bartle Massey come from nobody know where , wi ' his 
 counter - singin ' and fine anthem , as put everybody out but himself - one 
 takin ' it up after another like sheep a - bleatin ' I ' th ' fold . I know 
 what belong to bein ' a parish clerk , and I know as I should be wantin ' 
 I ' respect to your Reverence , an ' church , an ' king , if I be t ' allow 
 such goin - on wi'out speakin ' . I be take by surprise , an ' know 
 nothing on it beforehand , an ' I be so flustered , I be clean as if I would 
 lose my tool . I hanna slep ' more nor four hour this night as be past 
 an ' go ; an ' then it be nothing but nightmare , as tired I bad nor 
 wakin ' . " 
 " why , what in the world be the matter , Joshua ? have the thief be at 
 the church lead again ? " 
 " thief ! no , sir - an ' yet , as I may say , it be thief , an ' a - thievin ' 
 the church , too . it be the Methodisses as be like to get th ' upper hand 
 I ' th ' parish , if your Reverence an ' his Honour , Squire Donnithorne , 
 doesna think well to say the word an ' forbid it . not as I be a - dictatin ' 
 to you , sir ; I be not forgettin ' myself so far as to be wise above my 
 better . Howiver , whether I be wise or no , that be neither here nor there , 
 but what I have   get to say I say - as the young Methodis woman as be at 
 Mester Poyser 's be a - preachin ' an ' a - prayin ' on the Green last night , 
 as sure as I be a - stannin ' afore your Reverence now . " 
 " preach on the Green ! " say Mr. Irwine , look surprised but quite 
 serene . " what , that pale pretty young woman I have   see at Poyser 's ? I see 
 she be a Methodist , or Quaker , or something of that sort , by her dress , 
 but I do not   know she be a preacher . " 
 " it be a true word as I say , sir , " rejoin Mr. Rann , compress his 
 mouth into a semicircular form and pause long enough to indicate three 
 note of exclamation . " she preach on the Green last night ; an ' she be 
 lay hold of Chad 's Bess , as the girl be be I ' fit welly iver sin ' . " 
 " well , Bessy Cranage be a hearty - look lass ; I daresay she will come 
 round again , Joshua . do anybody else go into fit ? " 
 " no , sir , I canna say as they do . but there be no knowin ' what will come ,

 and Mr. Irwine 's sister , as any person of family within ten mile of

 it be quite a pity handsome , clever Mrs. Irwine should have have such 
 commonplace daughter . that fine old lady herself be worth drive ten

 old - fashioned dignity make she a graceful subject for conversation in 
 turn with the King 's health , the sweet new pattern in cotton dress , 
 the news from Egypt , and Lord Dacey 's lawsuit , which be fret poor 
 Lady Dacey to death . but no one ever think of mention the Miss 
 Irwines , except the poor people in Broxton village , who regard they 
 as deep in the science of medicine , and speak of they vaguely as " the 
 gentlefolk . " if any one have ask old Job Dummilow who give he his 
 flannel jacket , he would have answer , " the gentlefolk , last

 gentlefolk give she for her cough . under this name too , they be use 
 with great effect as a means of tame refractory child , so that at

 terrified sense that she be cognizant of all their bad misdemeanours , 
 and know the precise number of stone with which they have intend to 
 hit Farmer Britton 's duck . but for all who see they through a 
 less mythical medium , the Miss Irwines be quite superfluous 
 existence - inartistic figure crowd the canvas of life without 
 adequate effect . Miss Anne , indeed , if her chronic headache could have 
 be account for by a pathetic story of disappointed love , might have 
 have some romantic interest attach to she : but no such story have either 
 be know or invent concern she , and the general impression be 
 quite in accordance with the fact , that both the sister be old maid 
 for the prosaic reason that they have never receive an eligible offer . 
 nevertheless , to speak paradoxically , the existence of insignificant 
 people have very important consequence in the world . it can be show to 
 affect the price of bread and the rate of wage , to call forth many evil 
 temper from the selfish and many heroism from the sympathetic , and , 
 in other way , to play no small part in the tragedy of life . and if that 
 handsome , generous - blooded clergyman , the Rev. Adolphus Irwine , have not 
 have these two hopelessly maiden sister , his lot would have be shape 
 quite differently : he would very likely have take a comely wife in his 
 youth , and now , when his hair be get grey under the powder , would 
 have have tall son and bloom daughter - such possession , in short , 
 as man commonly think will repay they for all the labour they take under 
 the sun . as it be - have with all his three living no more than seven 
 hundred a - year , and see no way of keep his splendid mother and his 
 sickly sister , not to reckon a second sister , who be usually speak of 
 without any adjective , in such ladylike ease as become their birth 
 and habit , and at the same time provide for a family of his own - he 
 remain , you see , at the age of eight - and - forty , a bachelor , not 
 make any merit of that renunciation , but say laughingly , if any one 
 allude to it , that he make it an excuse for many indulgence which a 
 wife would never have allow he . and perhaps he be the only person in 
 the world who do not think his sister unintereste and superfluous ; 
 for his be one of those large - hearted , sweet - blooded nature that never 
 know a narrow or a grudging thought ; Epicurean , if you will , with no 
 enthusiasm , no self - scourging sense of duty ; but yet , as you have see , 
 of a sufficiently subtle moral fibre to have an unwearye tenderness 
 for obscure and monotonous suffering . it be his large - hearted 
 indulgence that make he ignore his mother 's hardness towards her 
 daughter , which be the more striking from its contrast with her doting 
 fondness towards himself ; he hold it no virtue to frown at irremediable 
 fault . 
 see the difference between the impression a man make on you when you 
 walk by his side in familiar talk , or look at he in his home , and the 
 figure he make when see from a lofty historical level , or even in the 
 eye of a critical neighbour who think of he as an embody system 
 or opinion rather than as a man . Mr. Roe , the " travel preacher " 
 station at Treddleston , have include Mr. Irwine in a general statement 
 concern the Church clergy in the surround district , whom he 
 describe as man give up to the lust of the flesh and the pride of 
 life ; hunt and shooting , and adorn their own house ; ask what 
 shall we eat , and what shall we drink , and wherewithal shall we be 
 clothe ? - careless of dispense the bread of life to their flock , 
 preach at good but a carnal and soul - benumb morality , and 
 trafficking in the soul of man by receive money for discharge the 
 pastoral office in parish where they do not so much as look on the 
 face of the people more than once a - year . the ecclesiastical historian , 
 too , look into parliamentary report of that period , find honourable 
 member zealous for the Church , and untainted with any sympathy for 
 the " tribe of canting Methodists , " make statement scarcely less 
 melancholy than that of Mr. Roe . and it be impossible for I to say that 
 Mr. Irwine be altogether belie by the generic classification assign 
 he . he really have no very lofty aim , no theological enthusiasm : if I 
 be closely question , I should be oblige to confess that he feel 
 no serious alarm about the soul of his parishioner , and would have 
 think it a mere loss of time to talk in a doctrinal and awakening 
 manner to old " Feyther Taft , " or even to Chad Cranage the blacksmith . 
 if he have be in the habit of speak theoretically , he would perhaps 
 have say that the only healthy form religion could take in such mind 
 be that of certain dim but strong emotion , suffuse themselves as a 
 hallow influence over the family affection and neighbourly duty . 
 he think the custom of baptism more important than its doctrine , and 
 that the religious benefit the peasant draw from the church where his 
 father worship and the sacred piece of turf where they lie bury 
 be but slightly dependent on a clear understanding of the Liturgy or 
 the sermon . clearly the rector be not what be call in these day an 
 " earnest " man : he be fonder of church history than of divinity , and have 
 much more insight into man 's character than interest in their opinion ; 
 he be neither laborious , nor obviously self - deny , nor very copious 
 in alm - giving , and his theology , you perceive , be lax . his mental 
 palate , indeed , be rather pagan , and find a savouriness in a quotation 
 from Sophocles or Theocritus that be quite absent from any text in 
 Isaiah or Amos . but if you feed your young setter on raw flesh , how 
 can you wonder at its retain a relish for uncooked partridge in 
 after - life ? and Mr. Irwine 's recollection of young enthusiasm and 
 ambition be all associate with poetry and ethic that lie aloof from 
 the Bible . 
 on the other hand , I must plead , for I have an affectionate partiality 
 towards the rector 's memory , that he be not vindictive - and some 
 philanthropist have be so ; that he be not intolerant - and there be a 
 rumour that some zealous theologian have not be altogether free from 
 that blemish ; that although he would probably have decline to give his 
 body to be burn in any public cause , and be far from bestow all 
 his good to feed the poor , he have that charity which have sometimes 
 be lack to very illustrious virtue - he be tender to other man 's 
 failing , and unwilling to impute evil . he be one of those man , 
 and they be not the commonest , of whom we can know the good only by 
 follow they away from the marketplace , the platform , and the pulpit , 
 enter with they into their own home , hear the voice with which 
 they speak to the young and aged about their own hearthstone , and 
 witness their thoughtful care for the everyday want of everyday 
 companion , who take all their kindness as a matter of course , and not 
 as a subject for panegyric . 
 such man , happily , have live in time when great abuse flourish , and 
 have sometimes even be the live representative of the abuse . 
 that be a thought which might comfort we a little under the opposite 
 fact - that it be well sometimes not to follow great reformer of 
 abuse beyond the threshold of their home . 
 but whatever you may think of Mr. Irwine now , if you have meet he that 
 June afternoon rid on his grey cob , with his dog run beside 
 he - portly , upright , manly , with a good - natured smile on his finely 
 turn lip as he talk to his dash young companion on the bay mare , 
 you must have feel that , however ill he harmonize with sound theory 
 of the clerical office , he somehow harmonize extremely well with that 
 peaceful landscape . 
 see they in the bright sunlight , interrupt every now and then by 
 rolling mass of cloud , ascend the slope from the Broxton side , 
 where the tall gable and elm of the rectory predominate over the tiny 
 whitewash church . they will soon be in the parish of Hayslope ; the 
 grey church - tower and village roof lie before they to the left , and 
 far on , to the right , they can just see the chimney of the Hall 
 Farm . 
 Chapter VI 
 the Hall Farm 
 EVIDENTLY that gate be never open , for the long grass and the great 
 hemlock grow close against it , and if it be open , it be so rusty 
 that the force necessary to turn it on its hinge would be likely to 
 pull down the square stone - build pillar , to the detriment of the two 
 stone lioness which grin with a doubtful carnivorous affability above 
 a coat of arm surmount each of the pillar . it would be easy enough , 
 by the aid of the nick in the stone pillar , to climb over the brick 
 wall with its smooth stone cop ; but by put our eye close to the 
 rusty bar of the gate , we can see the house well enough , and all but 
 the very corner of the grassy enclosure . 
 it be a very fine old place , of red brick , soften by a pale powdery 
 lichen , which have disperse itself with happy irregularity , so as 
 to bring the red brick into term of friendly companionship with the 
 limestone ornament surround the three gable , the window , and the 
 door - place . but the window be patch with wooden pane , and the door , 
 I think , be like the gate - it be never open . how it would groan and 
 grate against the stone floor if it be ! for it be a solid , heavy , 
 handsome door , and must once have be in the habit of shut with a 
 sonorous bang behind a liveried lackey , who have just see his master and 
 mistress off the ground in a carriage and pair . 
 but at present one might fancy the house in the early stage of a 
 chancery suit , and that the fruit from that grand double row of 
 walnut - tree on the right hand of the enclosure would fall and rot among 
 the grass , if it be not that we hear the booming bark of dog echo 
 from great building at the back . and now the half - wean calf that 
 have be shelter themselves in a gorse - build hovel against the 
 leave - hand wall come out and set up a silly answer to that terrible bark , 
 doubtless suppose that it have reference to bucket of milk . 
 yes , the house must be inhabit , and we will see by whom ; for 
 imagination be a licensed trespasser : it have no fear of dog , but may 
 climb over wall and peep in at window with impunity . put your face 
 to one of the glass pane in the right - hand window : what do you see ? a 
 large open fireplace , with rusty dog in it , and a bare board floor ; 
 at the far end , fleece of wool stack up ; in the middle of the floor , 
 some empty corn - bag . that be the furniture of the dining - room . and 
 what through the left - hand window ? several clothe - horse , a pillion , 
 a spinning - wheel , and an old box wide open and stuff full of coloured 
 rag . at the edge of this box there lie a great wooden doll , which , so 
 far as mutilation be concern , bear a strong resemblance to the fine 
 greek sculpture , and especially in the total loss of its nose . near it 
 there be a little chair , and the butt end of a boy 's leather long - lash 
 whip . 
 the history of the house be plain now . it be once the residence of 
 a country squire , whose family , probably dwindle down to mere 
 spinsterhood , get merge in the more territorial name of Donnithorne . it 
 be once the Hall ; it be now the Hall Farm . like the life in some 
 coast town that be once a watering - place , and be now a port , where the 
 genteel street be silent and grass - grow , and the dock and warehouse 
 busy and resonant , the life at the Hall have change its focus , and no 
 long radiate from the parlour , but from the kitchen and the farmyard . 
 Plenty of life there , though this be the drowsy time of the year , 
 just before hay - harvest ; and it be the drowsy time of the day too , 
 for it be close upon three by the sun , and it be half - past three by Mrs. 
 Poyser 's handsome eight - day clock . but there be always a strong sense 
 of life when the sun be brilliant after rain ; and now he be pour 
 down his beam , and make sparkle among the wet straw , and lighting 
 up every patch of vivid green moss on the red tile of the cow - shed , and 
 turn even the muddy water that be hurry along the channel to the 
 drain into a mirror for the yellow - bill duck , who be seize the 
 opportunity of get a drink with as much body in it as possible . 
 there be quite a concert of noise ; the great bull - dog , chain against 
 the stable , be throw into furious exasperation by the unwary approach 
 of a cock too near the mouth of his kennel , and send forth a thunder 
 bark , which be answer by two fox - hound shut up in the opposite 
 cow - house ; the old top - knot hen , scratch with their chick among 
 the straw , set up a sympathetic croaking as the discomfited cock join 
 they ; a sow with her brood , all very muddy as to the leg , and curl as 
 to the tail , throw in some deep staccato note ; our friend the calf 
 be bleat from the home croft ; and , under all , a fine ear discern 
 the continuous hum of human voice . 
 for the great barn - door be throw wide open , and man be busy 
 there mend the harness , under the superintendence of Mr. Goby , 
 the " whittaw , " otherwise saddler , who entertain they with the late 
 Treddleston gossip . it be certainly rather an unfortunate day that 
 Alick , the shepherd , have choose for have the whittaw , since the 
 morning turn out so wet ; and Mrs. Poyser have speak her mind pretty 
 strongly as to the dirt which the extra number of man 's shoe bring 
 into the house at dinnertime . indeed , she have not yet recover she 
 equanimity on the subject , though it be now nearly three hour since 
 dinner , and the house - floor be perfectly clean again ; as clean as 
 everything else in that wonderful house - place , where the only chance of 
 collect a few grain of dust would be to climb on the salt - coffer , 
 and put your finger on the high mantel - shelf on which the glitter 
 brass candlestick be enjoy their summer sinecure ; for at this time 
 of year , of course , every one go to bed while it be yet light , or 
 at least light enough to discern the outline of object after you 
 have bruise your shin against they . surely nowhere else could an 
 oak clock - case and an oak table have get to such a polish by the hand : 
 genuine " elbow polish , " as Mrs. Poyser call it , for she thank God 
 she never have any of your varnished rubbish in her house . Hetty Sorrel 
 often take the opportunity , when her aunt 's back be turn , of look 
 at the pleasing reflection of herself in those polished surface , for 
 the oak table be usually turn up like a screen , and be more for 
 ornament than for use ; and she could see herself sometimes in the great 
 round pewter dish that be range on the shelf above the long 
 deal dinner - table , or in the hob of the grate , which always shine like 
 jasper . 
 everything be look at its bright at this moment , for the sun 
 shine right on the pewter dish , and from their reflect surface 
 pleasant jet of light be throw on mellow oak and bright brass - and 
 on a still pleasanter object than these , for some of the ray fall on 
 Dinah 's finely mould cheek , and light up her pale red hair to auburn , 
 as she bend over the heavy household linen which she be mend for her 
 aunt . no scene could have be more peaceful , if Mrs. Poyser , who be 
 iron a few thing that still remain from the Monday 's wash , have 
 not be make a frequent clinking with her iron and move to and 
 fro whenever she want it to cool ; carry the keen glance of her 
 blue - grey eye from the kitchen to the dairy , where Hetty be make 
 up the butter , and from the dairy to the back kitchen , where Nancy be 
 take the pie out of the oven . do not suppose , however , that Mrs. 
 Poyser be elderly or shrewish in her appearance ; she be a good - look 
 woman , not more than eight - and - thirty , of fair complexion and sandy 
 hair , well - shapen , light - footed . the most conspicuous article in her 
 attire be an ample checker linen apron , which almost cover she 
 skirt ; and nothing could be plainer or less noticeable than her cap 
 and gown , for there be no weakness of which she be less tolerant than 
 feminine vanity , and the preference of ornament to utility . the family 
 likeness between she and her niece Dinah Morris , with the contrast 
 between her keenness and Dinah 's seraphic gentleness of expression , 
 might have serve a painter as an excellent suggestion for a Martha and 
 Mary . their eye be just of the same colour , but a striking test of 
 the difference in their operation be see in the demeanour of Trip , the 
 black - and - tan terrier , whenever that much - suspect dog unwarily expose 
 himself to the freezing arctic ray of Mrs. Poyser 's glance . her tongue 
 be not less keen than her eye , and , whenever a damsel come within 
 earshot , seem to take up an unfinished lecture , as a barrel - organ 
 take up a tune , precisely at the point where it have leave off . 
 the fact that it be churn day be another reason why it be 
 inconvenient to have the whittaw , and why , consequently , Mrs. 
 Poyser should scold molly the housemaid with unusual severity . to all 
 appearance molly have get through her after - dinner work in an exemplary 
 manner , have " clean herself " with great dispatch , and now come to ask , 
 submissively , if she should sit down to her spinning till milk time . 
 but this blameless conduct , accord to Mrs. Poyser , shroud a secret 
 indulgence of unbecoming wish , which she now drag forth and hold up 
 to Molly 's view with cut eloquence . 
 " Spinning , indeed ! it be not   spin as you would be at , I will be bind , and 
 let you have your own way . I never know your equal for gallowsness . to 
 think of a gell o ' your age want to go and sit with half - a - dozen man ! 
 I would ha ' be ashamed to let the word pass over my lip if I have be you . 
 and you , as have be here ever since last Michaelmas , and I hire you 
 at Treddles'on stattit , without a bit o ' character - as I say , you might 
 be grateful to be hire in that way to a respectable place ; and you know 
 no more o ' what belong to work when you come here than the mawkin I ' 
 the field . as poor a two - fiste thing as ever I see , you know you be . 
 who teach you to scrub a floor , I should like to know ? why , you would leave 
 the dirt in heap I ' the corner - anybody ' ud think you would never be 
 bring up among Christians . and as for spinning , why , you have   waste 
 as much as your wage I ' the flax you have   spoil learning to spin . 
 and you have   a right to feel that , and not to go about as gap and as 
 thoughtless as if you be behold to nobody . comb the wool for the 
 whittaw , indeed ! that be what you would like to be do , be it ? that be the 
 way with you - that be the road you would all like to go , headlong to ruin . 
 you be never easy till you have   get some sweetheart as be as big a fool as 
 yourself : you think you will be finely off when you be married , I daresay , 
 and have get a three - legged stool to sit on , and never a blanket to 
 cover you , and a bit o ' oat - cake for your dinner , as three child be 
 a - snatching at . " 
 " I be sure I donna want t ' go wi ' the whittaw , " say Molly , whimpering , 
 and quite overcome by this dantean picture of her future , " on'y we 
 allay use to comb the wool for ' n at Mester Ottley 's ; an ' so I just 
 ax ye . I donna want to set eye on the whittaw again ; I wish I may 
 never stir if I do . " 
 " Mr. Ottley 's , indeed ! it be fine talk o ' what you do at Mr. 
 Ottley 's . your missis there might like her floor dirte wi ' whittaw 
 for what I know . there be no knowing what people wonna like - such way as 
 I have   hear of ! I never have a gell come into my house as seem to know 
 what cleaning be ; I think people live like pig , for my part . and as to 
 that Betty as be dairymaid at Trent 's before she come to I , she would ha ' 
 leave the cheese without turn from week 's end to week 's end , and the 
 dairy thrall , I might ha ' write my name on them , when I come downstairs 
 after my illness , as the doctor say it be inflammation - it be a mercy 
 I get well of it . and to think o ' your know no well , Molly , and 
 be here a - go I ' nine month , and not for want o ' talk to , 
 neither - and what be you stanne there for , like a jack as be run 
 down , instead o ' get your wheel out ? you be a rare un for sit 
 down to your work a little while after it be time to put by . " 
 " Munny , my iron 's twite tell ; pease put it down to warm . " 
 the small chirrup voice that utter this request come from a little 
 sunny - haired girl between three and four , who , seat on a high chair 
 at the end of the iron table , be arduously clutch the handle of 
 a miniature iron with her tiny fat fist , and iron rag with an 
 assiduity that require she to put her little red tongue out as far as 
 anatomy would allow . 
 " Cold , be it , my darling ? bless your sweet face ! " say Mrs. Poyser , who 
 be remarkable for the facility with which she could relapse from her 
 official objurgatory to one of fondness or of friendly converse . " never 
 mind ! Mother 's do she iron now . she be go to put the iron 
 thing away . " 
 " Munny , I tould ' ike to do into de barn to Tommy , to see de whittawd . " 
 " no , no , no ; Totty ' ud get her foot wet , " say Mrs. Poyser , carry 
 away her iron . " run into the dairy and see cousin Hetty make the 
 butter . " 
 " I tould ' ike a bit o ' pum - take , " rejoin Totty , who seem to be 
 provide with several relay of request ; at the same time , take the 
 opportunity of her momentary leisure to put her finger into a bowl 
 of starch , and drag it down so as to empty the content with tolerable 
 completeness on to the iron sheet . 
 " do ever anybody see the like ? " scream Mrs. Poyser , run towards 
 the table when her eye have fall on the blue stream . " the child 's 
 allay I ' mischief if your back be turn a minute . what shall I do to 
 you , you naughty , naughty gell ? " 
 Totty , however , have descend from her chair with great swiftness , and 
 be already in retreat towards the dairy with a sort of waddle run , 
 and an amount of fat on the nape of her neck which make she look like 
 the metamorphosis of a white suckling pig . 
 the starch have be wipe up by Molly 's help , and the iron 
 apparatus put by , Mrs. Poyser take up her knitting which always lay 
 ready at hand , and be the work she like well , because she could carry 
 it on automatically as she walk to and fro . but now she come and sit 
 down opposite Dinah , whom she look at in a meditative way , as she 
 knit her grey worst stocking . 
 " you look th ' image o ' your Aunt Judith , Dinah , when you sit a - sewing . I 
 could almost fancy it be thirty year back , and I be a little gell 
 at home , look at Judith as she sit at her work , after she have do 
 the house up ; only it be a little cottage , Father 's be , and not a big 
 ramble house as get dirty I ' one corner as fast as you clean it in 
 another - but for all that , I could fancy you be your Aunt Judith , only 
 her hair be a deal dark than your , and she be stouter and broad 
 I ' the shoulder . Judith and I allay hang together , though she have 
 such queer way , but your mother and she never could agree . ah , your 
 mother little thought as she would have a daughter just cut out after the 
 very pattern o ' Judith , and leave she an orphan , too , for Judith to 
 take care on , and bring up with a spoon when she be in the graveyard at 
 Stoniton . I allay say that o ' Judith , as she would bear a pound weight 
 any day to save anybody else carry a ounce . and she be just the same 
 from the first o ' my remember she ; it make no difference in she , as 
 I could see , when she take to the Methodists , only she talk a bit 
 different and wear a different sort o ' cap ; but she would never in her life 
 spend a penny on herself more than keep herself decent . " 
 " she be a blessed woman , " say Dinah ; " God have give she a love , 
 self - forget nature , and he perfect it by grace . and she be very 
 fond of you too , Aunt Rachel . I often hear her talk of you in the same 
 sort of way . when she have that bad illness , and I be only eleven 
 year old , she use to say , ' you will have a friend on earth in your Aunt 
 Rachel , if I be take from you , for she have a kind heart , ' and I be sure 
 I have   find it so . " 
 " I do not   know how , child ; anybody ' ud be cunning to do anything for you , 
 I think ; you be like the bird o ' th ' air , and live nobody know how . 
 I would ha ' be glad to behave to you like a mother 's sister , if you would come 
 and live I ' this country where there be some shelter and victual for 
 man and beast , and folk do not   live on the naked hill , like poultry 
 a - scratching on a gravel bank . and then you might get married to some 
 decent man , and there would be plenty ready to have you , if you would only leave 
 off that preaching , as be ten time bad than anything your Aunt Judith 
 ever do . and even if you would marry Seth Bede , as be a poor wool - gathering 
 Methodist and be never like to have a penny beforehand , I know your 
 uncle ' ud help you with a pig , and very like a cow , for he be allay be 
 good - natur'd to my kin , for all they be poor , and make them welcome to 
 the house ; and ' ud do for you , I will be bind , as much as ever he would do 
 for Hetty , though she be his own niece . and there be linen in the house 
 as I could well spare you , for I have   get lot o ' sheet and 
 table - clothing , and towel , as be not   make up . there be a piece o ' 
 sheet I could give you as that squint Kitty spin - she be a rare 
 girl to spin , for all she squint , and the child could not   abide she ; 
 and , you know , the spinning be go on constant , and there be new linen 
 weave twice as fast as the old wear out . but where be the use o ' talking , 
 if ye wonna be persuade , and settle down like any other woman in her 
 sens , i'stead o ' wear yourself out with walk and preaching , 
 and give away every penny you get , so as you have   nothing save against 
 sickness ; and all the thing you have   get I ' the world , I verily believe , 
 ' ud go into a bundle no big nor a double cheese . and all because 
 you have   get notion I ' your head about religion more nor what be I ' the 
 Catechism and the Prayer - book . " 
 " but not more than what be in the Bible , Aunt , " say Dinah . 
 " yes , and the Bible too , for that matter , " Mrs. Poyser rejoin , rather 
 sharply ; " else why should not   they as know good what be in the Bible - the 
 parson and people as have get nothing to do but learn it - do the same 
 as you do ? but , for the matter o ' that , if everybody be to do like 
 you , the world must come to a standstill ; for if everybody try to 
 do without house and home , and with poor eating and drinking , and be 
 allay talk as we must despise the thing o ' the world as you say , I 
 should like to know where the pick o ' the stock , and the corn , and the 
 good new - milk cheese ' ud have to go . everybody ' ud be want bread 
 make o ' tail end and everybody ' ud be run after everybody else 
 to preach to them , istead o ' bring up their family , and lay by 
 against a bad harvest . it stand to sense as that can not   be the right 
 religion . " 
 " Nay , dear aunt , you never hear I say that all people be call to 
 forsake their work and their family . it be quite right the land should 
 be plough and sow , and the precious corn store , and the thing 
 of this life care for , and right that people should rejoice in their 
 family , and provide for they , so that this be do in the fear of the 
 Lord , and that they be not unmindful of the soul 's want while they be 
 care for the body . we can all be servant of God wherever our lot be 
 cast , but he give we different sort of work , accord as he fit we 
 for it and call we to it . I can no more help spend my life in try 
 to do what I can for the soul of other , than you could help run if 
 you hear little Totty cry at the other end of the house ; the voice 
 would go to your heart , you would think the dear child be in trouble or 
 in danger , and you could not   rest without run to help she and comfort 
 she . " 
 " ah , " say Mrs. Poyser , rise and walk towards the door , " I know it 
 ' ud be just the same if I be to talk to you for hour . you would make I 
 the same answer , at th ' end . I might as well talk to the run brook 
 and tell it to stan ' still . " 
 the causeway outside the kitchen door be dry enough now for Mrs. Poyser 
 to stand there quite pleasantly and see what be go on in the yard , 
 the grey worst stocking make a steady progress in her hand all the 
 while . but she have not be stand there more than five minute before 
 she come in again , and say to Dinah , in rather a flurry , awe - stricken 
 tone , " if there be not   Captain Donnithorne and Mr. Irwine a - come into 
 the yard ! I will lay my life they be come to speak about your preaching 
 on the Green , Dinah ; it be you must answer them , for I be dumb . I have   say 
 enough a'ready about your bring such disgrace upo ' your uncle 's 
 family . I would not   ha ' minded if you have be Mr. Poyser 's own 
 niece - folk must put up wi ' their own kin , as they put up wi ' their own 
 nose - it be their own flesh and blood . but to think of a niece o ' mine 
 be cause o ' my husband 's be turn out of his farm , and I bring 
 he no fortin but my savin 's - - " 
 " Nay , dear Aunt Rachel , " say Dinah gently , " you have   no cause for such 
 fear . I have   strong assurance that no evil will happen to you and my 
 uncle and the child from anything I have   do . I do not   preach without 
 direction . " 
 " Direction ! I know very well what you mean by direction , " say Mrs. 
 Poyser , knit in a rapid and agitated manner . " when there be a big 
 maggot than usual in your head you call it ' direction ' ; and then nothing 
 can stir you - you look like the statty o ' the outside o ' Treddles'on 
 church , a - starin ' and a - smilin ' whether it be fair weather or foul . I 
 hanna common patience with you . " 
 by this time the two gentleman have reach the paling and have get 
 down from their horse : it be plain they mean to come in . Mrs. Poyser 
 advance to the door to meet they , curtsy low and tremble between 
 anger with Dinah and anxiety to conduct herself with perfect propriety 
 on the occasion . for in those day the keen of bucolic mind feel a 
 whisper awe at the sight of the gentry , such as of old man feel when 
 they stand on tiptoe to watch the god pass by in tall human shape . 
 " well , Mrs. Poyser , how be you after this stormy morning ? " say Mr. 
 Irwine , with his stately cordiality . " our foot be quite dry ; we shall 
 not soil your beautiful floor . " 
 " oh , sir , do not   mention it , " say Mrs. Poyser . " will you and the captain 
 please to walk into the parlour ? " 
 " no , indeed , thank you , Mrs. Poyser , " say the captain , look eagerly 
 round the kitchen , as if his eye be seek something it could not 
 find . " I delight in your kitchen . I think it be the most charming room 
 I know . I should like every farmer 's wife to come and look at it for a 
 pattern . " 
 " oh , you be pleased to say so , sir . pray take a seat , " say Mrs. 
 Poyser , relieve a little by this compliment and the captain 's evident 
 good - humour , but still glance anxiously at Mr. Irwine , who , she see , 
 be look at Dinah and advance towards she . 
 " Poyser be not at home , be he ? " say Captain Donnithorne , seat 
 himself where he could see along the short passage to the open 
 dairy - door . 
 " no , sir , he be not ; he be go to Rosseter to see Mr. West , the factor , 
 about the wool . but there be Father i ' the barn , sir , if he would be of any 
 use . " 
 " no , thank you ; I will just look at the whelp and leave a message about 
 they with your shepherd . I must come another day and see your husband ; I 
 want to have a consultation with he about horse . do you know when he be 
 likely to be at liberty ? " 
 " why , sir , you can hardly miss he , except it be o ' Treddles'on 
 market - day - that be of a Friday , you know . for if he be anywhere on the 
 farm we can send for he in a minute . if we have get rid o ' the Scantlands , 
 we should have no outlying field ; and I should be glad of it , for if 
 ever anything happen , he be sure to be go to the Scantlands . thing 
 allay happen so contrairy , if they have   a chance ; and it be an unnat'ral 
 thing to have one bit o ' your farm in one county and all the rest in 
 another . " 
 " ah , the Scantlands would go much well with Choyce 's farm , especially 
 as he want dairyland and you have   get plenty . I think yours be the 
 prettiest farm on the estate , though ; and do you know , Mrs. Poyser , if I 
 be go to marry and settle , I should be tempt to turn you out , and 
 do up this fine old house , and turn farmer myself . " 
 " oh , sir , " say Mrs. Poyser , rather alarmed , " you would not   like it at 
 all . as for farming , it be put money into your pocket wi ' your 
 right hand and fetch it out wi ' your left . as fur as I can see , it be 
 raise victual for other folk and just get a mouthful for yourself 
 and your child as you go along . not as you would be like a poor man as 
 want to get his bread - you could afford to lose as much money as you 
 like I ' farming - but it be poor fun lose money , I should think , though 
 I understan ' it be what the great folk I ' London play at more than 
 anything . for my husband hear at market as Lord Dacey 's eld son have 
 lose thousand upo ' thousand to the Prince o ' Wales , and they say 
 my lady be go to pawn her jewel to pay for he . but you know more 
 about that than I do , sir . but , as for farming , sir , I canna think as 
 you would like it ; and this house - the draught in it be enough to cut you 
 through , and it be my opinion the floor upstairs be very rotten , and 
 the rat I ' the cellar be beyond anything . " 
 " why , that be a terrible picture , Mrs. Poyser . I think I should be do 
 you a service to turn you out of such a place . but there be no chance 
 of that . I be not likely to settle for the next twenty year , till I be a 
 stout gentleman of forty ; and my grandfather would never consent to part 
 with such good tenant as you . " 
 " well , sir , if he think so well o ' Mr. Poyser for a tenant I wish you 
 could put in a word for he to allow we some new gate for the five 
 close , for my husband 's be ask and ask till he be tired , and to 
 think o ' what he be do for the farm , and be never have a penny allow 
 he , be the time bad or good . and as I have   say to my husband often and 
 often , I be sure if the captain have anything to do with it , it would not 
 be so . not as I wish to speak disrespectful o ' they as have get the 
 power I ' their hand , but it be more than flesh and blood ' ull bear 
 sometimes , to be toil and strive , and up early and down late , and 
 hardly sleep a wink when you lie down for think as the cheese 
 may swell , or the cow may slip their calf , or the wheat may grow green 
 again I ' the sheaf - and after all , at th ' end o ' the year , it be like 
 as if you have be cook a feast and have get the smell of it for your 
 pain . " 
 Mrs. Poyser , once launch into conversation , always sail along 
 without any check from her preliminary awe of the gentry . the confidence 
 she feel in her own power of exposition be a motive force that 
 overcome all resistance . 
 " I be afraid I should only do harm instead of good , if I be to speak 
 about the gate , Mrs. Poyser , " say the captain , " though I assure you 
 there be no man on the estate I would soon say a word for than your 
 husband . I know his farm be in well order than any other within 
 ten mile of we ; and as for the kitchen , " he add , smile , " I do not 
 believe there be one in the kingdom to beat it . by the by , I have   never 
 see your dairy : I must see your dairy , Mrs. Poyser . " 
 " indeed , sir , it be not fit for you to go in , for Hetty 's in the middle 
 o ' make the butter , for the churning be throw late , and I be quite 
 ashamed . " this Mrs. Poyser say blushing , and believe that the captain 
 be really interested in her milk - pan , and would adjust his opinion of 
 she to the appearance of her dairy . 
 " oh , I have   no doubt it be in capital order . take I in , " say the captain , 
 himself lead the way , while Mrs. Poyser follow . 
 Chapter VII 
 the Dairy 
 the dairy be certainly worth look at : it be a scene to sicken for 
 with a sort of calenture in hot and dusty street - such coolness , such 
 purity , such fresh fragrance of new - press cheese , of firm butter , of 
 wooden vessel perpetually bath in pure water ; such soft colouring of 
 red earthenware and creamy surface , brown wood and polished tin , grey 
 limestone and rich orange - red rust on the iron weight and hook and 
 hinge . but one get only a confused notion of these detail when they 
 surround a distractingly pretty girl of seventeen , stand on little 
 patten and round her dimpled arm to lift a pound of butter out of 
 the scale . 
 Hetty blush a deep rose - colour when Captain Donnithorne enter the 
 dairy and speak to she ; but it be not at all a distressed blush , for 
 it be inwreathe with smile and dimple , and with sparkle from under 
 long , curl , dark eyelash ; and while her aunt be discourse to he 
 about the limited amount of milk that be to be spar for butter and 
 cheese so long as the calf be not all wean , and a large quantity 
 but inferior quality of milk yield by the shorthorn , which have 
 be buy on experiment , together with other matter which must be 
 interesting to a young gentleman who would one day be a landlord , Hetty 
 toss and pat her pound of butter with quite a self - possess , 
 coquettish air , slyly conscious that no turn of her head be lose . 
 there be various order of beauty , cause man to make fool of 
 themselves in various style , from the desperate to the sheepish ; but 
 there be one order of beauty which seem make to turn the head not only 
 of man , but of all intelligent mammal , even of woman . it be a beauty 
 like that of kitten , or very small downy duck make gentle ripple 
 noise with their soft bill , or baby just begin to toddle and 
 to engage in conscious mischief - a beauty with which you can never be 
 angry , but that you feel ready to crush for inability to comprehend the 
 state of mind into which it throw you . Hetty Sorrel 's be that sort 
 of beauty . her aunt , Mrs. Poyser , who profess to despise all personal 
 attraction and intend to be the severest of mentor , continually 
 gaze at Hetty 's charm by the sly , fascinate in spite of herself ; and 
 after administer such a scolding as naturally flow from her anxiety 
 to do well by her husband 's niece - who have no mother of her own to scold 
 she , poor thing ! - she would often confess to her husband , when they be 
 safe out of hearing , that she firmly believe , " the naughty the little 
 huzzy behave , the pretty she look . " 
 it be of little use for I to tell you that Hetty 's cheek be like a 
 rose - petal , that dimple play about her pouting lip , that her large 
 dark eye hide a soft roguishness under their long lash , and that her 
 curly hair , though all push back under her round cap while she be at 
 work , steal back in dark delicate ring on her forehead , and about her 
 white shell - like ear ; it be of little use for I to say how lovely 
 be the contour of her pink - and - white neckerchief , tuck into her low 
 plum - colour stuff bodice , or how the linen butter - make apron , with 
 its bib , seem a thing to be imitate in silk by duchess , since it 
 fall in such charming line , or how her brown stocking and thick - sole 
 buckle shoe lose all that clumsiness which they must certainly have 
 have when empty of her foot and ankle - of little use , unless you have 
 see a woman who affect you as Hetty affect her beholder , for 
 otherwise , though you might conjure up the image of a lovely woman , she 
 would not in the least resemble that distract kittenlike maiden . I 
 might mention all the divine charm of a bright spring day , but if you 
 have never in your life utterly forget yourself in strain your eye 
 after the mount lark , or in wander through the still lane when 
 the fresh - open blossom fill they with a sacred silent beauty like 
 that of fretted aisle , where would be the use of my descriptive 
 catalogue ? I could never make you know what I mean by a bright spring 
 day . Hetty 's be a spring - tide beauty ; it be the beauty of young 
 frisking thing , round - limbe , gambolling , circumvent you by a 
 false air of innocence - the innocence of a young star - browe calf , for 
 example , that , be incline for a promenade out of bound , lead you 
 a severe steeplechase over hedge and ditch , and only come to a stand in 
 the middle of a bog . 
 and they be the pretty attitude and movement into which a pretty 
 girl be throw in make up butter - toss movement that give a 
 charming curve to the arm , and a sideward inclination of the round white 
 neck ; little pat and roll movement with the palm of the hand , 
 and nice adaptation and finishing which can not at all be effect 
 without a great play of the pouting mouth and the dark eye . and then 
 the butter itself seem to communicate a fresh charm - it be so pure , 
 so sweet - scented ; it be turn off the mould with such a beautiful 
 firm surface , like marble in a pale yellow light ! moreover , Hetty be 
 particularly clever at make up the butter ; it be the one performance 
 of hers that her aunt allow to pass without severe criticism ; so she 
 handle it with all the grace that belong to mastery . 
 " I hope you will be ready for a great holiday on the thirtieth of July , 
 Mrs. Poyser , " say Captain Donnithorne , when he have sufficiently admire 
 the dairy and give several improvise opinion on swede turnip and 
 shorthorn . " you know what be to happen then , and I shall expect you 
 to be one of the guest who come early and leave late . will you 
 promise I your hand for two dance , Miss Hetty ? if I do not   get your 
 promise now , I know I shall hardly have a chance , for all the smart 
 young farmer will take care to secure you . " 
 Hetty smile and blush , but before she could answer , Mrs. Poyser 
 interpose , scandalize at the mere suggestion that the young squire 
 could be exclude by any mean partner . 
 " indeed , sir , you be very kind to take that notice of she . and I be 
 sure , whenever you be pleased to dance with she , she will be proud and 
 thankful , if she stand still all the rest o ' th ' evening . " 
 " oh no , no , that would be too cruel to all the other young fellow who 
 can dance . but you will promise I two dance , will not   you ? " the captain 
 continue , determined to make Hetty look at he and speak to he . 
 Hetty drop the prettiest little curtsy , and steal a half - shy , 
 half - coquettish glance at he as she say , " yes , thank you , sir . " 
 " and you must bring all your child , you know , Mrs. Poyser ; your 
 little Totty , as well as the boy . I want all the young child on 
 the estate to be there - all those who will be fine young man and woman 
 when I be a bald old fellow . " 
 " oh dear , sir , that ' ull be a long time first , " say Mrs. Poyser , quite 
 overcome at the young squire 's speak so lightly of himself , and 
 think how her husband would be interested in hear her recount this 
 remarkable speciman of high - bear humour . the captain be think to 
 be " very full of his joke , " and be a great favourite throughout the 
 estate on account of his free manner . every tenant be quite sure 
 thing would be different when the rein get into his hand - there 
 be to be a millennial abundance of new gate , allowance of lime , and 
 return of ten per cent . 
 " but where be Totty to - day ? " he say . " I want to see she . " 
 " where be the little un , Hetty ? " say Mrs. Poyser . " she come in here not 
 long ago . " 
 " I do not   know . she go into the brewhouse to Nancy , I think . " 
 the proud mother , unable to resist the temptation to show her Totty , 
 pass at once into the back kitchen , in search of she , not , however , 
 without misgiving lest something should have happen to render she 
 person and attire unfit for presentation . 
 " and do you carry the butter to market when you have   make it ? " say the 
 Captain to Hetty , meanwhile . 
 " oh no , sir ; not when it be so heavy . I be not strong enough to carry it . 
 Alick take it on horseback . " 
 " no , I be sure your pretty arm be never mean for such heavy weight . 
 but you go out a walk sometimes these pleasant evening , do not   you ? 
 why do not   you have a walk in the Chase sometimes , now it be so green and 
 pleasant ? I hardly ever see you anywhere except at home and at church . " 
 " aunt do not   like I to go a - walk only when I be go somewhere , " 
 say Hetty . " but I go through the Chase sometimes . " 
 " and do not   you ever go to see Mrs. Best , the housekeeper ? I think I see 
 you once in the housekeeper 's room . " 
 " it be not   Mrs. Best , it be Mrs. Pomfret , the lady 's maid , as I go to see . 
 she be teach I tent - stitch and the lace - mending . I be go to tea 
 with she to - morrow afternoon . " 
 the reason why there have be space for this tete - a - tete can only be 
 know by look into the back kitchen , where Totty have be discover 
 rub a stray blue - bag against her nose , and in the same moment 
 allow some liberal indigo drop to fall on her afternoon pinafore . 
 but now she appear hold her mother 's hand - the end of her round 
 nose rather shiny from a recent and hurried application of soap and

 " here she be ! " say the captain , lift she up and set she on the 
 low stone shelf . " here be Totty ! by the by , what be her other name ? she 
 be not   christen Totty . " 
 " oh , sir , we call she sadly out of her name . Charlotte 's her christen 
 name . it be a name I ' Mr. Poyser 's family : his grandmother be name 
 Charlotte . but we begin with call she Lotty , and now it be get to 
 Totty . to be sure it be more like a name for a dog than a Christian 
 child . " 
 " Totty 's a capital name . why , she look like a Totty . have she get a 
 pocket on ? " say the captain , feel in his own waistcoat pocket . 
 Totty immediately with great gravity lift up her frock , and show a 
 tiny pink pocket at present in a state of collapse . 
 " it dot notin ' in it , " she say , as she look down at it very 
 earnestly . 
 " no ! what a pity ! such a pretty pocket . well , I think I have   get some 
 thing in mine that will make a pretty jingle in it . yes ! I declare I have 
 get five little round silver thing , and hear what a pretty noise they 
 make in Totty 's pink pocket . " here he shake the pocket with the five

 be the young , and th ' only gell . " 
 " oh , she be a funny little fatty ; I would not   have she different . but I

 Chapter VIII 
 a Vocation 
 DINAH , who have rise when the gentleman come in , but still keep hold of 
 the sheet she be mend , curtsy respectfully when she see Mr. Irwine 
 look at she and advance towards she . he have never yet speak to 
 she , or stand face to face with she , and her first thought , as her eye 
 meet his , be , " what a well - favour countenance ! oh that the good seed 
 might fall on that soil , for it would surely flourish . " the agreeable 
 impression must have be mutual , for Mr. Irwine bow to she with a 
 benignant deference , which would have be equally in place if she have 
 be the most dignified lady of his acquaintance . 
 " you be only a visitor in this neighbourhood , I think ? " be his first 
 word , as he seat himself opposite to she . 
 " no , sir , I come from Snowfield , in Stonyshire . but my aunt be very 
 kind , want I to have rest from my work there , because I have be ill , 
 and she invite I to come and stay with she for a while . " 
 " ah , I remember Snowfield very well ; I once have occasion to go there . 
 it be a dreary bleak place . they be build a cotton - mill there ; but 
 that be many year ago now . I suppose the place be a good deal change by 
 the employment that mill must have bring . " 
 " it be change so far as the mill have bring people there , who get a 
 livelihood for themselves by work in it , and make it well for the 
 tradesfolk . I work in it myself , and have reason to be grateful , for 
 thereby I have enough and to spare . but it be still a bleak place , as you 
 say , sir - very different from this country . " 
 " you have relation live there , probably , so that you be attach to 
 the place as your home ? " 
 " I have an aunt there once ; she bring I up , for I be an orphan . but 
 she be take away seven year ago , and I have no other kindre that I 
 know of , besides my Aunt Poyser , who be very good to I , and would 
 have I come and live in this country , which to be sure be a good land , 
 wherein they eat bread without scarceness . but I be not free to leave 
 Snowfield , where I be first plant , and have grow deep into it , like 
 the small grass on the hill - top . " 
 " ah , I daresay you have many religious friend and companion there ; you 
 be a Methodist - a Wesleyan , I think ? " 
 " yes , my aunt at Snowfield belong to the Society , and I have cause 
 to be thankful for the privilege I have have thereby from my early 
 childhood . " 
 " and have you be long in the habit of preach ? for I understand you 
 preach at Hayslope last night . " 
 " I first take to the work four year since , when I be twenty - one . " 
 " your Society sanction woman 's preaching , then ? " 
 " it do not   forbid they , sir , when they have   a clear call to the work , 
 and when their ministry be own by the conversion of sinner and the 
 strengthening of God 's people . Mrs. Fletcher , as you may have hear 
 about , be the first woman to preach in the Society , I believe , before 
 she be marry , when she be Miss Bosanquet ; and Mr. Wesley approve 
 of she undertake the work . she have a great gift , and there be many 
 other now live who be precious fellow - helper in the work of the 
 ministry . I understand there be be voice raise against it in the 
 Society of late , but I can not but think their counsel will come to 
 nought . it be not   for man to make channel for God 's Spirit , as they 
 make channel for the watercourse , and say , ' flow here , but flow not 
 there . ' " 
 " but do not   you find some danger among your people - I do not   mean to say 
 that it be so with you , far from it - but do not   you find sometimes that 
 both man and woman fancy themselves channel for God 's Spirit , and be 
 quite mistaken , so that they set about a work for which they be unfit 
 and bring holy thing into contempt ? " 
 " Doubtless it be so sometimes ; for there have be evil - doer among we 
 who have seek to deceive the brother , and some there be who deceive 
 their own self . but we be not without discipline and correction to 
 put a check upon these thing . there be a very strict order keep among 
 we , and the brother and sister watch for each other 's soul as they 
 that must give account . they do not   go every one his own way and say , ' be 
 I my brother 's keeper ? ' " 
 " but tell I - if I may ask , and I be really interested in know 
 it - how you first come to think of preach ? " 
 " indeed , sir , I do not   think of it at all - I have be use from the time 
 I be sixteen to talk to the little child , and teach they , and 
 sometimes I have have my heart enlarge to speak in class , and be much 
 draw out in prayer with the sick . but I have feel no call to preach , for 
 when I be not greatly work upon , I be too much give to sit still and 
 keep by myself . it seem as if I could sit silent all day long with the 
 thought of God overflow my soul - as the pebble lie bath in the 
 Willow Brook . for thought be so great - be not   they , sir ? they seem to 
 lie upon we like a deep flood ; and it be my besetment to forget where 
 I be and everything about I , and lose myself in thought that I could 
 give no account of , for I could neither make a beginning nor end of 
 they in word . that be my way as long as I can remember ; but sometimes 
 it seem as if speech come to I without any will of my own , and word 
 be give to I that come out as the tear come , because our heart 
 be full and we can not   help it . and those be always time of great 
 blessing , though I have never think it could be so with I before 
 a congregation of people . but , sir , we be lead on , like the little 
 child , by a way that we know not . I be call to preach quite 
 suddenly , and since then I have never be leave in doubt about the work 
 that be lay upon I . " 
 " but tell I the circumstance - just how it be , the very day you begin 
 to preach . " 
 " it be one Sunday I walk with brother Marlowe , who be an aged 
 man , one of the local preacher , all the way to Hetton - Deeps - that be a 
 village where the people get their living by work in the lead - mine , 
 and where there be no church nor preacher , but they live like sheep 
 without a shepherd . it be well than twelve mile from Snowfield , so 
 we set out early in the morning , for it be summertime ; and I have a 
 wonderful sense of the divine love as we walk over the hill , where 
 there be no tree , you know , sir , as there be here , to make the sky look 
 small , but you see the heaven stretch out like a tent , and you feel 
 the everlaste arm around you . but before we get to Hetton , brother 
 Marlowe be seize with a dizziness that make he afraid of fall , for 
 he overwork himself sadly , at his year , in watch and pray , 
 and walk so many mile to speak the Word , as well as carry on his 
 trade of linen - weaving . and when we get to the village , the people be 
 expect he , for he have appoint the time and the place when he be 
 there before , and such of they as care to hear the Word of Life be 
 assemble on a spot where the cottage be thick , so as other might 
 be draw to come . but he feel as he could not   stand up to preach , and 
 he be force to lie down in the first of the cottage we come to . so I 
 go to tell the people , think we would go into one of the house , and I 
 would read and pray with they . but as I pass along by the cottage and 
 see the aged and tremble woman at the door , and the hard look of the 
 man , who seem to have their eye no more fill with the sight of the 
 Sabbath morning than if they have be dumb oxen that never look up to 
 the sky , I feel a great movement in my soul , and I tremble as if I 
 be shake by a strong spirit enter into my weak body . and I go to 
 where the little flock of people be gather together , and step on 
 the low wall that be build against the green hillside , and I speak the 
 word that be give to I abundantly . and they all come round I out 
 of all the cottage , and many weep over their sin , and have since be 
 join to the Lord . that be the beginning of my preaching , sir , and 
 I have   preach ever since . " 
 Dinah have let her work fall during this narrative , which she utter in 
 her usual simple way , but with that sincere articulate , thrill treble 
 by which she always master her audience . she stoop now to gather up 
 her sewing , and then go on with it as before . Mr. Irwine be deeply 
 interested . he say to himself , " he must be a miserable prig who would 
 act the pedagogue here : one might as well go and lecture the tree for 
 grow in their own shape . " 
 " and you never feel any embarrassment from the sense of your youth - that 
 you be a lovely young woman on whom man 's eye be fix ? " he say 
 aloud . 
 " no , I have   no room for such feeling , and I do not   believe the people ever 
 take notice about that . I think , sir , when God make his presence feel 
 through we , we be like the burn bush : Moses never take any heed 
 what sort of bush it be - he only see the brightness of the Lord . I have 
 preach to as rough ignorant people as can be in the village about 
 Snowfield - man that look very hard and wild - but they never say an 
 uncivil word to I , and often thank I kindly as they make way for I 
 to pass through the midst of they . " 
 " that I can believe - that I can well believe , " say Mr. Irwine , 
 emphatically . " and what do you think of your hearer last night , now ? 
 do you find they quiet and attentive ? " 
 " very quiet , sir , but I see no sign of any great work upon they , except 
 in a young girl name Bessy Cranage , towards whom my heart yearn 
 greatly , when my eye first fall on her bloom youth , give up 
 to folly and vanity . I have some private talk and prayer with she 
 afterwards , and I trust her heart be touch . but I have   notice that 
 in these village where the people lead a quiet life among the green 
 pasture and the still water , till the ground and tend the 
 cattle , there be a strange deadness to the Word , as different as can 
 be from the great town , like Leeds , where I once go to visit a holy 
 woman who preach there . it be wonderful how rich be the harvest of 
 soul up those high - walled street , where you seem to walk as in a 
 prison - yard , and the ear be deafen with the sound of worldly toil . 
 I think maybe it be because the promise be sweet when this life be so 
 dark and weary , and the soul get more hungry when the body be ill at 
 ease . " 
 " why , yes , our farm - labourer be not easily rouse . they take life 
 almost as slowly as the sheep and cow . but we have some intelligent 
 workman about here . I daresay you know the Bedes ; Seth Bede , by the by , 
 be a Methodist . " 
 " yes , I know Seth well , and his brother Adam a little . Seth be a 
 gracious young man - sincere and without offence ; and Adam be like the 
 patriarch Joseph , for his great skill and knowledge and the kindness he 
 show to his brother and his parent . " 
 " perhaps you do not   know the trouble that have just happen to they ? 
 their father , Matthias Bede , be drown in the Willow Brook last night , 
 not far from his own door . I be go now to see Adam . " 
 " ah , their poor aged mother ! " say Dinah , drop her hand and look 
 before she with pity eye , as if she see the object of her sympathy . 
 " she will mourn heavily , for Seth have tell I she be of an anxious , 
 troubled heart . I must go and see if I can give she any help . " 
 as she rise and be begin to fold up her work , Captain Donnithorne , 
 have exhaust all plausible pretext for remain among the 
 milk - pan , come out of the dairy , follow by Mrs. Poyser . Mr. Irwine 
 now rise also , and , advance towards Dinah , hold out his hand , and 
 say , " good - bye . I hear you be go away soon ; but this will not be 
 the last visit you will pay your aunt - so we shall meet again , I hope . " 
 his cordiality towards Dinah set all Mrs. Poyser 's anxiety at rest , 
 and her face be bright than usual , as she say , " I have   never ask 
 after Mrs. Irwine and the Miss Irwines , sir ; I hope they be as well as 
 usual . " 
 " yes , thank you , Mrs. Poyser , except that Miss Anne have one of her bad 
 headache to - day . by the by , we all like that nice cream - cheese you 
 send we - my mother especially . " 
 " I be very glad , indeed , sir . it be but seldom I make one , but I 
 remember Mrs. Irwine be fond of them . please to give my duty to she , 
 and to Miss Kate and Miss Anne . they have   never be to look at my poultry 
 this long while , and I have   get some beautiful speckled chicken , black 
 and white , as Miss Kate might like to have some of amongst her . " 
 " well , I will tell she ; she must come and see they . good - bye , " say the 
 rector , mount his horse . 
 " just ride slowly on , Irwine , " say Captain Donnithorne , mount also . 
 " I will overtake you in three minute . I be only go to speak to the 
 shepherd about the whelp . good - bye , Mrs. Poyser ; tell your husband I 
 shall come and have a long talk with he soon . " 
 Mrs. Poyser curtsy duly , and watch the two horse until they have 
 disappear from the yard , amidst great excitement on the part of the

 Mrs. Poyser , however , though she notice the bonnet , defer remarking
