 " why , Mr. Irwine be not   angry , then ? what do he say to you , Dinah ? 
 do not   he scold you for preach ? " 
 " no , he be not at all angry ; he be very friendly to I . I be quite 
 draw out to speak to he ; I hardly know how , for I have always think 
 of he as a worldly Sadducee . but his countenance be as pleasant as the 
 morning sunshine . " 
 " Pleasant ! and what else do y ' expect to find he but pleasant ? " say 
 Mrs. Poyser impatiently , resume her knitting . " I should think his 
 countenance be pleasant indeed ! and he a gentleman bear , and be get a 
 mother like a picter . you may go the country round and not find such 
 another woman turn sixty - six . it be summat - like to see such a man as 
 that I ' the desk of a Sunday ! as I say to Poyser , it be like look at 
 a full crop o ' wheat , or a pasture with a fine dairy o ' cow in it ; it 
 make you think the world 's comfortable - like . but as for such creatur 
 as you methodisse run after , I would as soon go to look at a lot o '

 " he only say he would hear of it ; he do not   seem to feel any displeasure

 something that I be sure will cause you sorrow , as it do I . Thias Bede

 " dear heart , dear heart ! but you must have a cup o ' tea first , child , " 
 say Mrs. Poyser , fall at once from the key of b with five sharp to

 she be get never a drop o ' nothing to comfort she inside . sit down , 
 child , and be easy , for you shall not   stir out till you have   have a cup o ' 
 tea , and so I tell you . " 
 during the latter part of this speech , Mrs. Poyser have be reach

 " molly , " she say , rather languidly , " just run out and get I a bunch of 
 dock - leaf : the butter 's ready to pack up now . " 
 " d ' you hear what be happen , Hetty ? " say her aunt . 
 " no ; how should I hear anything ? " be the answer , in a pettish tone .

 chapter IX 
 Hetty 's World 
 while she adjust the broad leaf that set off the pale fragrant 
 butter as the primrose be set off by its nest of green I be afraid Hetty 
 be think a great deal more of the look Captain Donnithorne have cast 
 at she than of Adam and his trouble . bright , admire glance from 
 a handsome young gentleman with white hand , a gold chain , occasional 
 regimental , and wealth and grandeur immeasurable - those be the 
 warm ray that set poor Hetty 's heart vibrate and play its little 
 foolish tune over and over again . we do not hear that Memnon 's statue 
 give forth its melody at all under the rushing of the mighty wind , 
 or in response to any other influence divine or human than certain 
 short - live sunbeam of morning ; and we must learn to accommodate 
 ourselves to the discovery that some of those cunningly fashion 
 instrument call human soul have only a very limited range of music , 
 and will not vibrate in the least under a touch that fill other with 
 tremulous rapture or quivering agony . 
 Hetty be quite use to the thought that people like to look at she . 
 she be not blind to the fact that young Luke Britton of Broxton come to 
 Hayslope Church on a Sunday afternoon on purpose that he might see she ; 
 and that he would have make much more decided advance if her uncle 
 Poyser , thinking but lightly of a young man whose father 's land be so 
 foul as old Luke Britton 's , have not forbid her aunt to encourage he 
 by any civility . she be aware , too , that Mr. Craig , the gardener at 
 the Chase , be over head and ear in love with she , and have lately make 
 unmistakable avowal in luscious strawberry and hyperbolical pea . 
 she know still well , that Adam Bede - tall , upright , clever , brave Adam 
 Bede - who carry such authority with all the people round about , and 
 whom her uncle be always delighted to see of an evening , say that 
 " Adam know a fine sight more o ' the natur o ' thing than those as 
 think themselves his better " - she know that this Adam , who be often 
 rather stern to other people and not much give to run after the lass , 
 could be make to turn pale or red any day by a word or a look from 
 she . Hetty 's sphere of comparison be not large , but she could not   help 
 perceive that Adam be ' something like " a man ; always know what to say 
 about thing , could tell her uncle how to prop the hovel , and have mend 
 the churn in no time ; know , with only look at it , the value of the 
 chestnut - tree that be blow down , and why the damp come in the wall , 
 and what they must do to stop the rat ; and write a beautiful hand 
 that you could read off , and could do figure in his head - a degree 
 of accomplishment totally unknown among the rich farmer of that 
 countryside . not at all like that slouch Luke Britton , who , when 
 she once walk with he all the way from Broxton to Hayslope , have only 
 broken silence to remark that the grey goose have begin to lay . and as 
 for Mr. Craig , the gardener , he be a sensible man enough , to be sure , 
 but he be knock - kneed , and have a queer sort of sing - song in his talk ; 
 moreover , on the most charitable supposition , he must be far on the way 
 to forty . 
 Hetty be quite certain her uncle want she to encourage Adam , and 
 would be pleased for she to marry he . for those be time when there 
 be no rigid demarcation of rank between the farmer and the respectable 
 artisan , and on the home hearth , as well as in the public house , they 
 might be see take their jug of ale together ; the farmer have 
 a latent sense of capital , and of weight in parish affair , which 
 sustain he under his conspicuous inferiority in conversation . Martin 
 Poyser be not a frequenter of public house , but he like a friendly 
 chat over his own home - brew ; and though it be pleasant to lay down 
 the law to a stupid neighbour who have no notion how to make the good 
 of his farm , it be also an agreeable variety to learn something from 
 a clever fellow like Adam Bede . accordingly , for the last three 
 year - ever since he have superintend the building of the new 
 barn - Adam have always be make welcome at the Hall Farm , especially of 
 a winter evening , when the whole family , in patriarchal fashion , master 
 and mistress , child and servant , be assemble in that glorious 
 kitchen , at well - graduate distance from the blaze fire . and for the 
 last two year , at least , Hetty have be in the habit of hear she 
 uncle say , " Adam Bede may be work for wage now , but he will be a 
 master - man some day , as sure as I sit in this chair . Mester Burge be 
 in the right on't   to want he to go partner and marry his daughter , if 
 it be true what they say ; the woman as marry he ' ull have a good take , 
 be't Lady day or Michaelmas , " a remark which Mrs. Poyser always follow 
 up with her cordial assent . " ah , " she would say , " it be all very fine 
 have a ready - make rich man , but mayhappen he will be a ready - make fool ; 
 and it be no use fill your pocket full o ' money if you have   get a hole 
 in the corner . it will do you no good to sit in a spring - cart o ' your own , 
 if you have   get a soft to drive you : he will soon turn you over into the 
 ditch . I allay say I would never marry a man as have get no brain ; for 
 where be the use of a woman have brain of her own if she be tackle 
 to a geck as everybody be a - laugh at ? she might as well dress herself 
 fine to sit back'ard on a donkey . " 
 these expression , though figurative , sufficiently indicate the bent of 
 Mrs. Poyser 's mind with regard to Adam ; and though she and her husband 
 might have view the subject differently if Hetty have be a daughter

 Adam for a penniless niece . for what could Hetty have be but a servant 
 elsewhere , if her uncle have not take she in and bring she up as a 
 domestic help to her aunt , whose health since the birth of Totty have not 
 be equal to more positive labour than the superintendence of servant 
 and child ? but Hetty have never give Adam any steady encouragement . 
 even in the moment when she be most thoroughly conscious of his

 be grateful enough for the most trifling notice from he . " Mary Burge , 
 indeed ! such a sallow - face girl : if she put on a bit of pink ribbon , 
 she look as yellow as a crow - flower and her hair be as straight as a

 subtle fibre of the plant . she see he as he be - a poor man with old

 Hetty 's dream be all of luxury : to sit in a carpet parlour , and 
 always wear white stocking ; to have some large beautiful ear - ring , 
 such as be all the fashion ; to have Nottingham lace round the top of 
 her gown , and something to make her handkerchief smell nice , like 
 Miss Lydia Donnithorne 's when she draw it out at church ; and not to be 
 oblige to get up early or be scold by anybody . she think , if Adam

 enough to marry he . 
 but for the last few week a new influence have come over Hetty - vague , 
 atmospheric , shape itself into no self - confess hope or prospect , 
 but produce a pleasant narcotic effect , make she tread the ground 
 and go about her work in a sort of dream , unconscious of weight or 
 effort , and show she all thing through a soft , liquid veil , as if 
 she be live not in this solid world of brick and stone , but in a 
 beatified world , such as the sun light up for we in the water . Hetty 
 have become aware that Mr. Arthur Donnithorne would take a good deal of 
 trouble for the chance of see she ; that he always place himself at 
 church so as to have the full view of she both sit and stand ; 
 that he be constantly find reason for call at the Hall Farm , and 
 always would contrive to say something for the sake of make she speak 
 to he and look at he . the poor child no more conceive at present the 
 idea that the young squire could ever be her lover than a baker 's pretty 
 daughter in the crowd , whom a young emperor distinguish by an imperial 
 but admire smile , conceive that she shall be make empress . but the 
 baker 's daughter go home and dream of the handsome young emperor , and 
 perhaps weigh the flour amiss while she be think what a heavenly lot 
 it must be to have he for a husband . and so , poor Hetty have get a face 
 and a presence haunt she wake and sleeping dream ; bright , soft 
 glance have penetrate she , and suffuse her life with a strange , happy

 fine as Adam 's , which sometimes look at she with a sad , beseech

 silly imagination , whereas Adam 's could get no entrance through that

 odour like that of a flower - garden borne on the evening breeze . foolish 
 thought ! but all this happen , you must remember , nearly sixty year 
 ago , and Hetty be quite uneducated - a simple farmer 's girl , to whom 
 a gentleman with a white hand be dazzle as an olympian god . until 
 to - day , she have never look far into the future than to the next 
 time Captain Donnithorne would come to the Farm , or the next Sunday when 
 she should see he at church ; but now she think , perhaps he would try 
 to meet she when she go to the Chase to - morrow - and if he should 
 speak to she , and walk a little way , when nobody be by ! that have never 
 happen yet ; and now her imagination , instead of retrace the past , 
 be busy fashion what would happen to - morrow - whereabout in the 
 Chase she should see he come towards she , how she should put her new

 through in her memory , over and over again , all the rest of the day . 
 in this state of mind , how could Hetty give any feeling to Adam 's 
 trouble , or think much about poor old Thias be drown ? young soul , 
 in such pleasant delirium as hers be as unsympathetic as butterfly

 dream - by invisible look and impalpable arm . 
 while Hetty 's hand be busy pack up the butter , and her head fill 
 with these picture of the morrow , Arthur Donnithorne , rid by Mr. 
 Irwine 's side towards the valley of the Willow Brook , have also certain 
 indistinct anticipation , run as an undercurrent in his mind while

 strong enough to make he feel rather conscious when Mr. Irwine suddenly 
 say , " what fascinate you so in Mrs. Poyser 's dairy , Arthur ? have you 
 become an amateur of damp quarry and skim dish ? " 
 Arthur know the rector too well to suppose that a clever invention would 
 be of any use , so he say , with his accustomed frankness , " no , I go to 
 look at the pretty butter - maker Hetty Sorrel . she be a perfect Hebe ; and 
 if I be an artist , I would paint she . it be amazing what pretty girl 
 one see among the farmer ' daughter , when the man be such clown . 
 that common , round , red face one see sometimes in the man - all cheek 
 and no feature , like Martin Poyser 's - come out in the woman of the 
 famuly as the most charming phiz imaginable . " 
 " well , I have no objection to your contemplating Hetty in an artistic 
 light , but I must not have you feed her vanity and fill her little 
 noddle with the notion that she be a great beauty , attractive to fine 
 gentleman , or you will spoil she for a poor man 's wife - honest Craig 's , 
 for example , whom I have see bestow soft glance on she . the little 
 puss seem already to have air enough to make a husband as miserable 
 as it be a law of nature for a quiet man to be when he marry a beauty . 
 Apropos of marry , I hope our friend Adam will get settle , now the 
 poor old man 's go . he will only have his mother to keep in future , and 
 I have   a notion that there be a kindness between he and that nice modest 
 girl , Mary Burge , from something that fall from old Jonathan one day 
 when I be talk to he . but when I mention the subject to Adam he 
 look uneasy and turn the conversation . I suppose the love - making 
 do not   run smooth , or perhaps Adam hang back till he be in a well 
 position . he have independence of spirit enough for two man - rather an 
 excess of pride , if anything . " 
 " that would be a capital match for Adam . he would slip into old Burge 's 
 shoe and make a fine thing of that building business , I will answer for 
 he . I should like to see he well settle in this parish ; he would be 
 ready then to act as my grand - vizier when I want one . we could plan 
 no end of repair and improvement together . I have   never see the girl , 
 though , I think - at least I have   never look at she . " 
 " look at she next Sunday at church - she sit with her father on the 
 left of the reading - desk . you need not   look quite so much at Hetty Sorrel 
 then . when I have   make up my mind that I can not   afford to buy a tempting 
 dog , I take no notice of he , because if he take a strong fancy to 
 I and look lovingly at I , the struggle between arithmetic and

 wisdom there , Arthur , and as an old fellow to whom wisdom have become 
 cheap , I bestow it upon you . " 
 " thank you . it may stand I in good stead some day though I do not 
 know that I have any present use for it . bless I ! how the brook have 
 overflow . suppose we have a canter , now we be at the bottom of the

 that be the great advantage of dialogue on horseback ; it can be merge 
 any minute into a trot or a canter , and one might have escape from 
 Socrates himself in the saddle . the two friend be free from the 
 necessity of further conversation till they pull up in the lane behind 
 Adam 's cottage . 
 Chapter X 
 Dinah Visits Lisbeth 
 AT five o'clock Lisbeth come downstairs with a large key in her hand : 
 it be the key of the chamber where her husband lie dead . throughout the 
 day , except in her occasional outburst of wail grief , she have be 
 in incessant movement , perform the initial duty to she dead with 
 the awe and exactitude that belong to religious rite . she have bring 
 out her little store of bleached linen , which she have for long year 
 keep in reserve for this supreme use . it seem but yesterday - that time 
 so many midsummer ago , when she have tell Thias where this linen lay , 
 that he might be sure and reach it out for she when she die , for she 
 be the elder of the two . then there have be the work of cleanse to 
 the strict purity every object in the sacred chamber , and of remove 
 from it every trace of common daily occupation . the small window , which 
 have hitherto freely let in the frosty moonlight or the warm summer 
 sunrise on the work man 's slumber , must now be darken with a fair 
 white sheet , for this be the sleep which be as sacred under the bare 
 rafter as in ceile house . Lisbeth have even mend a long - neglect 
 and unnoticeable rent in the checkered bit of bed - curtain ; for the 
 moment be few and precious now in which she would be able to do the 
 small office of respect or love for the still corpse , to which in all 
 her thought she attribute some consciousness . our dead be never dead 
 to we until we have forget they : they can be injure by we , they can 
 be wound ; they know all our penitence , all our aching sense that their 
 place be empty , all the kiss we bestow on the small relic of their 
 presence . and the aged peasant woman most of all believe that her dead 
 be conscious . decent burial be what Lisbeth have be think of for 
 herself through year of thrift , with an indistinct expectation that she 
 should know when she be be carry to the churchyard , follow by 
 her husband and her son ; and now she feel as if the great work of 
 her life be to be do in see that Thias be bury decently before 
 she - under the white thorn , where once , in a dream , she have think she 
 lie in the coffin , yet all the while see the sunshine above and smell 
 the white blossom that be so thick upon the thorn the Sunday she go 
 to be church after Adam be bear . 
 but now she have do everything that could be do to - day in the chamber 
 of death - have do it all herself , with some aid from her son in 
 lifting , for she would let no one be fetched to help she from the 
 village , not be fond of female neighbour generally ; and her 
 favourite dolly , the old housekeeper at Mr. Burge 's , who have come to 
 condole with she in the morning as soon as she hear of Thias 's death , 
 be too dim - sighted to be of much use . she have lock the door , and now 
 hold the key in her hand , as she throw herself wearily into a chair 
 that stand out of its place in the middle of the house floor , where in 
 ordinary time she would never have consent to sit . the kitchen have 
 have none of her attention that day ; it be soil with the tread of 
 muddy shoe and untidy with clothe and other object out of place . but 
 what at another time would have be intolerable to Lisbeth 's habit 
 of order and cleanliness seem to she now just what should be : it be 
 right that thing should look strange and disorder and wretche , now 
 the old man have come to his end in that sad way ; the kitchen ought not 
 to look as if nothing have happen . Adam , overcome with the agitation 
 and exertion of the day after his night of hard work , have fall asleep 
 on a bench in the workshop ; and Seth be in the back kitchen make a 
 fire of stick that he might get the kettle to boil , and persuade his 
 mother to have a cup of tea , an indulgence which she rarely allow 
 herself . 
 there be no one in the kitchen when Lisbeth enter and throw herself 
 into the chair . she look round with blank eye at the dirt and 
 confusion on which the bright afternoon 's sun shine dismally ; it be 
 all of a piece with the sad confusion of her mind - that confusion which 
 belong to the first hour of a sudden sorrow , when the poor human soul 
 be like one who have be deposit sleep among the ruin of a vast 
 city , and wake up in dreary amazement , not know whether it be 
 the growing or the die day - not know why and whence come this 
 illimitable scene of desolation , or why he too find himself desolate in 
 the midst of it . 
 at another time Lisbeth 's first thought would have be , " where be 
 Adam ? " but the sudden death of her husband have restore he in 
 these hour to that first place in her affection which he have hold 
 six - and - twenty year ago . she have forget his fault as we forget the 
 sorrow of our depart childhood , and think of nothing but the young 
 husband 's kindness and the old man 's patience . her eye continue 
 to wander blankly until Seth come in and begin to remove some of the 
 scatter thing , and clear the small round deal table that he might set 
 out his mother 's tea upon it . 
 " what art go to do ? " she say , rather peevishly . 
 " I want thee to have a cup of tea , Mother , " answer Seth , tenderly . 
 " it will do thee good ; and I will put two or three of these thing away , and 
 make the house look more comfortable . " 
 " comfortable ! how canst talk o ' ma'in ' thing comfortable ? let a - be , let 
 a - be . there be no comfort for I no more , " she go on , the tear come 
 when she begin to speak , " now thy poor feyther 's gone , as i'n wash for 
 and mend , an ' get 's victual for he for thirty ' ear , an ' him allay 
 so pleased wi ' iverything I do for he , an ' use to be so handy an ' do 
 the job for I when I war ill an ' cumbered wi ' th ' babby , an ' make I 
 the posset an ' bring it upstairs as proud as could be , an ' carry the 
 lad as war as heavy as two child for five mile an ' ne'er grumble , 
 all the way to Warson Wake , 'cause I want to go an ' see my sister , as 
 war dead an ' go the very next Christmas as e'er come . an ' he to be 
 drownde in the brook as we pass o'er the day we war marry an ' 
 come home together , an ' he have make they lot o ' shelf for I to put my 
 plate an ' thing on , an ' show them I as proud as could be , 'cause he 
 know'd I should be please . an ' he war to die an ' I not to know , but to 
 be a - sleepin ' I ' my bed , as if I caredna nought about it . eh ! an ' I to 
 live to see that ! an ' we as war young folk once , an ' thought we should 
 do rarely when we war marry . let a - be , lad , let a - be ! I wonna ha ' 
 no tay . I carena if I ne'er ate nor drink no more . when one end o ' th ' 
 bridge tumble down , where be th ' use o ' th ' other stannin ' ? I may be well 
 die , an ' foller my old man . there be no knowin ' but he will want I . " 
 here Lisbeth break from word into moan , sway herself backwards and 
 forward on her chair . Seth , always timid in his behaviour towards his 
 mother , from the sense that he have no influence over she , feel it be 
 useless to attempt to persuade or soothe she till this passion be past ; 
 so he content himself with tend the back kitchen fire and fold 
 up his father 's clothe , which have be hang out to dry since 
 morning - afraid to move about in the room where his mother be , lest he 
 should irritate she far . 
 but after Lisbeth have be rock herself and moan for some minute , 
 she suddenly pause and say aloud to herself , " I will go an ' see arter 
 Adam , for I canna think where he be get ; an ' I want he to go upstairs 
 wi ' I afore it be dark , for the minute to look at the corpse be like 
 the meltin ' snow . " 
 Seth overhear this , and come into the kitchen again , as his mother 
 rise from her chair , he say , " Adam 's asleep in the workshop , mother . 
 thee'dst well not wake he . he be o'erwrought with work and trouble . " 
 " wake he ? who be a - go to wake he ? I shanna wake he wi ' lookin ' at 
 he . I hanna see the lad this two hour - I would welly forget as he would e'er 
 grow up from a babby when 's feyther carry he . " 
 Adam be seat on a rough bench , his head support by his arm , which 
 rest from the shoulder to the elbow on the long planing - table in 
 the middle of the workshop . it seem as if he have sit down for a few 
 minute ' rest and have fall asleep without slip from his first 
 attitude of sad , fatigued thought . his face , unwashe since yesterday , 
 look pallid and clammy ; his hair be toss shaggily about his 
 forehead , and his closed eye have the sunken look which follow upon 
 watching and sorrow . his brow be knit , and his whole face have an 
 expression of weariness and pain . Gyp be evidently uneasy , for he sit 
 on his haunch , rest his nose on his master 's stretch - out leg , and 
 divide the time between lick the hand that hang listlessly down and 
 glance with a listen air towards the door . the poor dog be 
 hungry and restless , but would not leave his master , and be wait 
 impatiently for some change in the scene . it be owe to this feeling 
 on Gyp 's part that , when Lisbeth come into the workshop and advance 
 towards Adam as noiselessly as she could , her intention not to awaken 
 he be immediately defeat ; for Gyp 's excitement be too great to find 
 vent in anything short of a sharp bark , and in a moment Adam open his 
 eye and see his mother stand before he . it be not very unlike his 
 dream , for his sleep have be little more than live through again , in 
 a fevere delirious way , all that have happen since daybreak , and his 
 mother with her fretful grief be present to he through it all . the 
 chief difference between the reality and the vision be that in 
 his dream Hetty be continually come before he in bodily 
 presence - strangely mingle herself as an actor in scene with which 
 she have nothing to do . she be even by the Willow Brook ; she make his 
 mother angry by come into the house ; and he meet she with her smart 
 clothe quite wet through , as he walk in the rain to Treddleston , to 
 tell the coroner . but wherever Hetty come , his mother be sure to follow 
 soon ; and when he open his eye , it be not at all startling to see 
 her stand near he . 
 " eh , my lad , my lad ! " Lisbeth burst out immediately , her wail impulse 
 return , for grief in its freshness feel the need of associate its 
 loss and its lament with every change of scene and incident , " thee'st 
 get nobody now but thy old mother to torment thee and be a burden to 
 thee . Thy poor feyther ' ull ne'er anger thee no more ; an ' thy mother 
 may be well go arter he - the soon the well - for I be no good to 
 nobody now . one old coat ' ull do to patch another , but it be good for 
 nought else . thee'dst like to ha ' a wife to mend thy clothe an ' get thy 
 victual , well nor thy old mother . an ' I shall be nought but cumber , 
 a - sittin ' I ' th ' chimney - corner .   Adam wince and move uneasily ; he 
 dread , of all thing , to hear his mother speak of Hetty .   but if 
 thy feyther have live , he would ne'er ha ' want I to go to make room for 
 another , for he could no more ha ' do wi'out I nor one side o ' the 
 scissar can do wi'out th ' other . eh , we should ha ' be both fling away 
 together , an ' then I shouldna ha ' see this day , an ' one buryin ' ' ud ha ' 
 do for we both . " 
 here Lisbeth pause , but Adam sit in pained silence - he could not speak 
 otherwise than tenderly to his mother to - day , but he could not help 
 be irritate by this plaint . it be not possible for poor Lisbeth to 
 know how it affect Adam any more than it be possible for a wound 
 dog to know how his moan affect the nerve of his master . like all 
 complain woman , she complain in the expectation of be soothe , 
 and when Adam say nothing , she be only prompt to complain more 
 bitterly . 
 " I know thee couldst do well wi'out I , for thee couldst go where thee 
 likedst an ' marry they as thee likedst . but I donna want to say thee 
 nay , let thee bring home who thee wut ; I would ne'er open my lip to find 
 faut , for when folk be old an ' o ' no use , they may think theirsen well 
 off to get the bit an ' the sup , though they'n to swallow ill word wi't . 
 an ' if thee'st set thy heart on a lass as'll bring thee nought and waste 
 all , when thee mightst ha ' they as ' ud make a man on thee , I will say 
 nought , now thy feyther 's dead an ' drownde , for I be no well nor an 
 old haft when the blade 's go . " 
 Adam , unable to bear this any long , rise silently from the bench and 
 walk out of the workshop into the kitchen . but Lisbeth follow he . 
 " Thee wutna go upstairs an ' see thy feyther then ? I'n do everythin ' 
 now , an ' he would like thee to go an ' look at he , for he war allay so 
 please when thee wast mild to he . " 
 Adam turn round at once and say , " yes , mother ; let we go upstairs . 
 Come , Seth , let we go together . " 
 they go upstairs , and for five minute all be silence . then the key 
 be turn again , and there be a sound of footstep on the stair . but 
 Adam do not come down again ; he be too weary and wear - out to encounter 
 more of his mother 's querulous grief , and he go to rest on his bed . 
 Lisbeth no soon enter the kitchen and sit down than she throw she 
 apron over her head , and begin to cry and moan and rock herself as 
 before . Seth think , " she will be quieter by and by , now we have be 
 upstairs " ; and he go into the back kitchen again , to tend his little 
 fire , hop that he should presently induce she to have some tea . 
 Lisbeth have be rock herself in this way for more than five minute , 
 give a low moan with every forward movement of her body , when she 
 suddenly feel a hand place gently on her , and a sweet treble voice 
 say to she , " dear sister , the Lord have send I to see if I can be a 
 comfort to you . " 
 Lisbeth pause , in a listening attitude , without remove her apron from 
 her face . the voice be strange to she . could it be her sister 's spirit 
 come back to she from the dead after all those year ? she tremble and 
 dare not look . 
 Dinah , believe that this pause of wonder be in itself a relief for 
 the sorrow woman , say no more just yet , but quietly take off her 
 bonnet , and then , motion silence to Seth , who , on hear her voice , 
 have come in with a beat heart , lay one hand on the back of Lisbeth 's 
 chair and lean over she , that she might be aware of a friendly 
 presence . 
 slowly Lisbeth draw down her apron , and timidly she open her dim 
 dark eye . she see nothing at first but a face - a pure , pale face , with 
 love grey eye , and it be quite unknown to she . her wonder increase ; 
 perhaps it be an angel . but in the same instant Dinah have lay her hand 
 on Lisbeth 's again , and the old woman look down at it . it be a much 
 small hand than her own , but it be not white and delicate , for Dinah 
 have never wear a glove in her life , and her hand bear the trace of 
 labour from her childhood upwards . Lisbeth look earnestly at the hand 
 for a moment , and then , fix her eye again on Dinah 's face , say , 
 with something of restore courage , but in a tone of surprise , " why , 
 ye're a workin ' woman ! " 
 " yes , I be Dinah Morris , and I work in the cotton - mill when I be at 
 home . " 
 " ah ! " say Lisbeth slowly , still wonder ; " ye come in so light , like 
 the shadow on the wall , an ' speak I ' my ear , as I think ye might be a 
 sperrit . Ye've   get a'most the face o ' one as be a - sittin ' on the grave 
 I ' Adam 's new Bible . " 
 " I come from the Hall Farm now . you know Mrs. Poyser - she be my aunt , and 
 she have hear of your great affliction , and be very sorry ; and I be come 
 to see if I can be any help to you in your trouble ; for I know your son 
 Adam and Seth , and I know you have no daughter ; and when the clergyman 
 tell I how the hand of God be heavy upon you , my heart go out 
 towards you , and I feel a command to come and be to you in the place of 
 a daughter in this grief , if you will let I . " 
 " ah ! I know who y ' be now ; y ' be a Methody , like Seth ; he be tould 
 I on you , " say Lisbeth fretfully , her overpowering sense of pain 
 returning , now her wonder be go . " Ye'll make it out as trouble 's a 
 good thing , like he allay do . but where be the use o ' talkin ' to I 
 a - that'n ? ye canna make the smart less wi ' talkin ' . Ye'll ne'er make I 
 believe as it be well for I not to ha ' my old man die in 's bed , if he 
 must die , an ' ha ' the parson to pray by he , an ' I to sit by he , an ' 
 tell he ne'er to mind th ' ill word I have   gi'en he sometimes when I war 
 anger , an ' to gi ' he a bit an ' a sup , as long as a bit an ' a sup 
 he would swallow . but eh ! to die I ' the cold water , an ' us close to he , an ' 
 ne'er to know ; an ' I a - sleepin ' , as if I ne'er belong to he no more 
 nor if he have be a journeyman tramp from nobody know where ! " 
 here Lisbeth begin to cry and rock herself again ; and Dinah say , " yes , 
 dear friend , your affliction be great . it would be hardness of heart to 
 say that your trouble be not heavy to bear . God do not   send I to you 
 to make light of your sorrow , but to mourn with you , if you will let I . 
 if you have a table spread for a feast , and be make merry with your 
 friend , you would think it be kind to let I come and sit down and 
 rejoice with you , because you would think I should like to share those 
 good thing ; but I should like well to share in your trouble and your 
 labour , and it would seem hard to I if you deny I that . you will not 
 send I away ? you be not angry with I for come ? " 
 " Nay , nay ; anger ! who say I war anger ? it war good on you to come . 
 an ' Seth , why donna ye get she some tay ? ye war in a hurry to get some 
 for I , as have no need , but ye donna think o ' gettin ' ' t for they as 
 want it . sit ye down ; sit ye down . I thank you kindly for comin ' , for 
 it be little wage ye get by walkin ' through the wet field to see an old 
 woman like I .... nay , I'n get no daughter o ' my own - ne'er have one - an ' 
 I warna sorry , for they be poor queechy thing , gell be ; I allay 
 want to ha ' lad , as could fend for theirsen . an ' the lad ' ull be 
 marryin ' - I shall ha ' daughter eno ' , an ' too many . but now , do ye make 
 the tay as ye like it , for I'n get no taste I ' my mouth this day - it be 
 all one what I swaller - it be all get the taste o ' sorrow wi't . " 
 Dinah take care not to betray that she have have her tea , and accept 
 Lisbeth 's invitation very readily , for the sake of persuade the old 
 woman herself to take the food and drink she so much need after a day 
 of hard work and fast . 
 Seth be so happy now Dinah be in the house that he could not help 
 think her presence be worth purchase with a life in which grief 
 incessantly follow upon grief ; but the next moment he reproach 
 himself - it be almost as if he be rejoice in his father 's sad 
 death . nevertheless the joy of be with Dinah would triumph - it be 
 like the influence of climate , which no resistance can overcome . and the 
 feeling even suffuse itself over his face so as to attract his mother 's

 " Thee may'st well talk o ' trouble bein ' a good thing , Seth , for thee 
 thriv'st on't . Thee look'st as if thee know'dst no more o ' care an ' 
 cumber nor when thee wast a babby a - lyin ' awake I ' th ' cradle . for 
 thee'dst allay lie still wi ' thy eye open , an ' Adam ne'er ' ud lie 
 still a minute when he waken . Thee wast allay like a bag o ' meal as 
 can ne'er be bruise - though , for the matter o ' that , thy poor feyther 
 war just such another . but ye've   get the same look too "   here Lisbeth 
 turn to Dinah . " I reckon it be wi ' bein ' a Methody . not as I be 
 a - findin ' faut wi ' ye for't , for ye've   no call to be frettin ' , an ' 
 somehow ye looken sorry too . eh ! well , if the Methodies be fond o ' 
 trouble , they be like to thrive : it be a pity they canna ha't all , an ' 
 take it away from they as donna like it . I could ha ' gi'en them plenty ; 
 for when I have get my old man I war worrete from morn till night ; and 
 now he be go , I would be glad for the bad o'er again . " 
 " yes , " say Dinah , careful not to oppose any feeling of Lisbeth 's , for 
 her reliance , in her small word and deed , on a divine guidance , 
 always issue in that fine woman 's tact which proceed from acute and 
 ready sympathy ; " yes , I remember too , when my dear aunt die , I long 
 for the sound of her bad cough in the night , instead of the silence 
 that come when she be go . but now , dear friend , drink this other cup 
 of tea and eat a little more . " 
 " what ! " say Lisbeth , take the cup and speak in a less querulous 
 tone , " have ye get no feyther and mother , then , as ye war so sorry about 
 your aunt ? " 
 " no , I never know a father or mother ; my aunt bring I up from a baby . 
 she have no child , for she be never marry and she bring I up as 
 tenderly as if I have be her own child . " 
 " eh , she would fine work wi ' ye , I will warrant , bringin ' ye up from a babby , 
 an ' she a lone woman - it be ill bringin ' up a cade lamb . but I daresay 
 ye warna franzy , for ye look as if ye'd ne'er be anger I ' your life . 
 but what do ye do when your aunt die , an ' why didna ye come to live in 
 this country , bein ' as Mrs. Poyser 's your aunt too ? "

 would like to be leave alone with his mother . 
 Lisbeth sit watch she as she move about in she still quick way , and 
 say at last , " Ye've   get a notion o ' cleanin ' up . I wouldna mind ha'in 
 ye for a daughter , for ye wouldna spend the lad 's wage I ' fine clothe 
 an ' waste . Ye're not like the lass o ' this countryside . I reckon folk 
 be different at Snowfield from what they be here . " 
 " they have a different sort of life , many of them , " say Dinah ; " they 
 work at different thing - some in the mill , and many in the mine , in 
 the village round about . but the heart of man be the same everywhere , 
 and there be the child of this world and the child of light there 
 as well as elsewhere . but we have   many more Methodists there than in this 
 country . " 
 " well , I didna know as the Methody woman war like ye , for there be will 
 Maskery 's wife , as they say be a big Methody , isna pleasant to look at , 
 at all . I would as lief look at a tooad . an ' I be thinkin ' I wouldna mind if 
 ye'd stay an ' sleep here , for I should like to see ye I ' th ' house I ' 
 th ' mornin ' . but mayhappen they will be lookin for ye at Mester Poyser 's . " 
 " no , " say Dinah , " they do not   expect I , and I should like to stay , if 
 you will let I . " 
 " well , there 's room ; I'n get my bed lay I ' th ' little room o'er the 
 back kitchen , an ' ye can lie beside I . I would be glad to ha ' ye wi ' I to 
 speak to I ' th ' night , for ye've   get a nice way o ' talkin ' . it put I 
 I ' mind o ' the swallow as be under the thack last ' ear when they fust 
 begin to sing low an ' soft - like I ' th ' mornin ' . eh , but my old man war 
 fond o ' they bird ! an ' so war Adam , but they'n ne'er come again this 
 ' ear . Happen THEY'RE dead too . " 
 " there , " say Dinah , " now the kitchen look tidy , and now , dear 
 Mother - for I be your daughter to - night , you know - I should like you to 
 wash your face and have a clean cap on . do you remember what David do , 
 when God take away his child from he ? while the child be yet alive 
 he fast and pray to God to spare it , and he would neither eat nor 
 drink , but lie on the ground all night , beseech God for the child . 
 but when he know it be dead , he rise up from the ground and wash and 
 anoint himself , and change his clothe , and eat and drank ; and when 
 they ask he how it be that he seem to have leave off grieve now 
 the child be dead , he say , ' while the child be yet alive , I fast 
 and weep ; for I say , who can tell whether God will be gracious to I , 
 that the child may live ? but now he be dead , wherefore should I fast ? 
 can I bring he back again ? I shall go to he , but he shall not return 
 to I . ' " 
 " eh , that be a true word , " say Lisbeth . " yea , my old man wonna come back 
 to I , but I shall go to he - the soon the well . well , ye may do as 
 ye like wi ' me : there be a clean cap I ' that drawer , an ' I will go I ' the 
 back kitchen an ' wash my face . an ' Seth , thee may'st reach down Adam 's 
 new Bible wi ' th ' picter in , an ' she shall read we a chapter . eh , I 
 like they word - ' I shall go to he , but he wonna come back to I . ' " 
 Dinah and Seth be both inwardly offer thank for the great 
 quietness of spirit that have come over Lisbeth . this be what Dinah have 
 be try to bring about , through all her still sympathy and absence 
 from exhortation . from her girlhood upwards she have have experience among 
 the sick and the mourning , among mind harden and shrivel through 
 poverty and ignorance , and have gain the subtle perception of the 
 mode in which they could well be touch and soften into willingness

 it , ' she be never leave to herself ; but it be always give she when to 
 keep silence and when to speak . " and do we not all agree to call rapid 
 thought and noble impulse by the name of inspiration ? after our subtle 
 analysis of the mental process , we must still say , as Dinah do , that 
 our high thought and our good deed be all give to we . 
 and so there be earnest prayer - there be faith , love , and hope pour 
 forth that evening in the little kitchen . and poor , aged , fretful 
 Lisbeth , without grasp any distinct idea , without go through any 
 course of religious emotion , feel a vague sense of goodness and love , 
 and of something right lie underneath and beyond all this sorrow 
 life . she could not   understand the sorrow ; but , for these moment , under 
 the subduing influence of Dinah 's spirit , she feel that she must be 
 patient and still . 
 chapter XI 
 in the cottage

 Chapter XII 
 in the Wood 
 that same Thursday morning , as Arthur Donnithorne be move about in 
 his dressing - room see his well - look british person reflect in 
 the old - fashioned mirror , and star at , from a dingy olive - green piece 
 of tapestry , by Pharaoh 's daughter and her maiden , who ought to have 
 be mind the infant Moses , he be hold a discussion with himself , 
 which , by the time his valet be tie the black silk sling over his 
 shoulder , have issue in a distinct practical resolution . 
 " I mean to go to Eagledale and fish for a week or so , " he say aloud . 
 " I shall take you with I , Pym , and set off this morning ; so be ready by 
 half - past eleven . " 
 the low whistle , which have assist he in arrive at this resolution , 
 here break out into his loud ringing tenor , and the corridor , as he 
 hurry along it , echo to his favourite song from the Beggar 's Opera , 
 " when the heart of a man be oppress with care . " not an heroic strain ; 
 nevertheless Arthur feel himself very heroic as he stride towards the 
 stable to give his order about the horse . his own approbation be 
 necessary to he , and it be not an approbation to be enjoy quite 
 gratuitously ; it must be win by a fair amount of merit . he have never yet 
 forfeit that approbation , and he have considerable reliance on his own 
 virtue . no young man could confess his fault more candidly ; candour 
 be one of his favourite virtue ; and how can a man 's candour be see 
 in all its lustre unless he have a few failing to talk of ? but he have 
 an agreeable confidence that his fault be all of a generous 
 kind - impetuous , warm - blooded , leonine ; never crawl , crafty , 
 reptilian . it be not possible for Arthur Donnithorne to do anything 
 mean , dastardly , or cruel . " no ! I be a devil of a fellow for get 
 myself into a hobble , but I always take care the load shall fall on 
 my own shoulder . " unhappily , there be no inherent poetical justice in 
 hobble , and they will sometimes obstinately refuse to inflict their 
 bad consequence on the prime offender , in spite of his loudly 
 express wish . it be entirely owe to this deficiency in the scheme 
 of thing that Arthur have ever bring any one into trouble besides 
 himself . he be nothing if not good - natured ; and all his picture of 
 the future , when he should come into the estate , be make up of a 
 prosperous , contented tenantry , adore their landlord , who would be the 
 model of an english gentleman - mansion in first - rate order , all elegance 
 and high taste - jolly housekeeping , fine stud in Loamshire - purse open 
 to all public object - in short , everything as different as possible 
 from what be now associate with the name of Donnithorne . and one of 
 the first good action he would perform in that future should be to 
 increase Irwine 's income for the vicarage of Hayslope , so that he might 
 keep a carriage for his mother and sister . his hearty affection for the 
 rector date from the age of frock and trouser . it be an affection 
 partly filial , partly fraternal - fraternal enough to make he like 
 Irwine 's company well than that of most young man , and filial enough 
 to make he shrink strongly from incur Irwine 's disapprobation . 
 you perceive that Arthur Donnithorne be " a good fellow " - all his 
 college friend think he such . he could not   bear to see any one 
 uncomfortable ; he would have be sorry even in his angriest mood for

 chapter XIII 
 evening in the Wood

 the rest of his life . he could not   imagine himself in that position ; it 
 be too odious , too unlike he . 
 and even if no one know anything about it , they might get too fond of 
 each other , and then there could be nothing but the misery of parting , 
 after all . no gentleman , out of a ballad , could marry a farmer 's niece . 
 there must be an end to the whole thing at once . it be too foolish . 
 and yet he have be so determined this morning , before he go to 
 Gawaine 's ; and while he be there something have take hold of he and 
 make he gallop back . it seem he could not   quite depend on his own 
 resolution , as he have think he could ; he almost wish his arm would 
 get painful again , and then he should think of nothing but the comfort 
 it would be to get rid of the pain . there be no know what impulse 
 might seize he to - morrow , in this confounded place , where there be 
 nothing to occupy he imperiously through the livelong day . what could 
 he do to secure himself from any more of this folly ? 
 there be but one resource . he would go and tell Irwine - tell he 
 everything . the mere act of tell it would make it seem trivial ; the 
 temptation would vanish , as the charm of fond word vanish when one 
 repeat they to the indifferent . in every way it would help he to tell 
 Irwine . he would ride to Broxton Rectory the first thing after breakfast 
 to - morrow . 
 Arthur have no soon come to this determination than he begin to think 
 which of the path would lead he home , and make as short a walk thither 
 as he could . he feel sure he should sleep now : he have have enough to tire 
 he , and there be no more need for he to think .

 the Return Home

 Chapter XV 
 the Two Bed - Chambers 
 HETTY and Dinah both sleep in the second story , in room adjoin each

 oh yes ! she be very pretty . Captain Donnithorne think so . pretty

 how pretty the little puss look in that odd dress ! it would be the 
 easy folly in the world to fall in love with she : there be such a 
 sweet babylike roundness about her face and figure ; the delicate dark

 ARTHUR DONNITHORNE , you remember , be under an engagement with himself to 
 go and see Mr. Irwine this Friday morning , and he be awake and dress 
 so early that he determine to go before breakfast , instead of after . 
 the rector , he know , breakfast alone at half - past nine , the lady of 
 the family have a different breakfast - hour ; Arthur will have an early 
 ride over the hill and breakfast with he . one can say everything good 
 over a meal . 
 the progress of civilization have make a breakfast or a dinner an 
 easy and cheerful substitute for more troublesome and disagreeable 
 ceremony . we take a less gloomy view of our error now our father 
 confessor listen to we over his egg and coffee . we be more distinctly 
 conscious that rude penance be out of the question for gentleman in 
 an enlightened age , and that mortal sin be not incompatible with an 
 appetite for muffin . an assault on our pocket , which in more barbarous 
 time would have be make in the brusque form of a pistol - shot , be 
 quite a well - breed and smile procedure now it have become a request for 
 a loan throw in as an easy parenthesis between the second and third 
 glass of claret . 
 still , there be this advantage in the old rigid form , that they 
 commit you to the fulfilment of a resolution by some outward deed : 
 when you have put your mouth to one end of a hole in a stone wall and 
 be aware that there be an expectant ear at the other end , you be more 
 likely to say what you come out with the intention of say than if you 
 be seat with your leg in an easy attitude under the mahogany with 
 a companion who will have no reason to be surprised if you have nothing 
 particular to say . 
 however , Arthur Donnithorne , as he wind among the pleasant lane on

 influence of rank , and quite ready to give an extra amount of respect to

 carpenter with a large fund of reverence in his nature , which incline

 towards the young squire this instinctive reverence of Adam 's be

 " on Sunday , sir ; Mr. Irwine be come early on purpose . I shall be glad

 it off , and the new spring bring no new shoot out on the wither

 Book two 
 chapter XVII 
 in which the Story pause a little 
 " this rector of Broxton be little well than a pagan ! " I hear one of my 
 reader exclaim . " how much more edify it would have be if you have 
 make he give Arthur some truly spiritual advice ! you might have put 
 into his mouth the most beautiful thing - quite as good as read a 
 sermon . " 
 certainly I could , if I hold it the high vocation of the novelist 
 to represent thing as they never have be and never will be . then , 
 of course , I might refashion life and character entirely after my own 
 liking ; I might select the most unexceptionable type of clergyman and 
 put my own admirable opinion into his mouth on all occasion . but it 
 happen , on the contrary , that my strong effort be to avoid any such 
 arbitrary picture , and to give a faithful account of man and thing 
 as they have mirror themselves in my mind . the mirror be doubtless 
 defective , the outline will sometimes be disturb , the reflection 
 faint or confused ; but I feel as much bind to tell you as precisely 
 as I can what that reflection be , as if I be in the witness - box , 
 narrate my experience on oath . 
 sixty year ago - it be a long time , so no wonder thing have 
 change - all clergyman be not zealous ; indeed , there be reason to 
 believe that the number of zealous clergyman be small , and it be 
 probable that if one among the small minority have own the living 
 of Broxton and Hayslope in the year 1799 , you would have like he no 
 well than you like Mr. Irwine . ten to one , you would have think he 
 a tasteless , indiscreet , methodistical man . it be so very rarely that 
 fact hit that nice medium require by our own enlightened opinion and 
 refine taste ! perhaps you will say , " do improve the fact a little , 
 then ; make they more accordant with those correct view which it be our 
 privilege to possess . the world be not just what we like ; do touch it 
 up with a tasteful pencil , and make believe it be not quite such a mixed 
 entangle affair . let all people who hold unexceptionable opinion act 
 unexceptionably . let your most faulty character always be on the wrong 
 side , and your virtuous one on the right . then we shall see at a glance 
 whom we be to condemn and whom we be to approve . then we shall be able 
 to admire , without the slight disturbance of our prepossession : we 
 shall hate and despise with that true ruminant relish which belong to 
 undoubting confidence . " 
 but , my good friend , what will you do then with your fellow - parishioner 
 who oppose your husband in the vestry ? with your newly appoint vicar , 
 whose style of preach you find painfully below that of his regretted 
 predecessor ? with the honest servant who worry your soul with her one 
 fail ? with your neighbour , Mrs. Green , who be really kind to you 
 in your last illness , but have say several ill - natured thing about you 
 since your convalescence ? Nay , with your excellent husband himself , who 
 have other irritate habit besides that of not wipe his shoe ? these 
 fellow - mortal , every one , must be accept as they be : you can neither 
 straighten their nose , nor brighten their wit , nor rectify their 
 disposition ; and it be these people - amongst whom your life be 
 pass - that it be needful you should tolerate , pity , and love : it be 
 these more or less ugly , stupid , inconsistent people whose movement of 
 goodness you should be able to admire - for whom you should cherish all 
 possible hope , all possible patience . and I would not , even if I have 
 the choice , be the clever novelist who could create a world so much 
 well than this , in which we get up in the morning to do our daily 
 work , that you would be likely to turn a hard , cold eye on the 
 dusty street and the common green field - on the real breathing man 
 and woman , who can be chill by your indifference or injure by your 
 prejudice ; who can be cheer and help onward by your fellow - feeling , 
 your forbearance , your outspoken , brave justice . 
 so I be content to tell my simple story , without try to make thing 
 seem well than they be ; dread nothing , indeed , but falsity , 
 which , in spite of one 's good effort , there be reason to dread . 
 falsehood be so easy , truth so difficult . the pencil be conscious of a 
 delightful facility in draw a griffin - the long the claw , and 
 the large the wing , the well ; but that marvellous facility which 
 we mistook for genius be apt to forsake we when we want to draw a real 
 unexaggerated lion . examine your word well , and you will find that even 
 when you have no motive to be false , it be a very hard thing to say the 
 exact truth , even about your own immediate feeling - much hard than to 
 say something fine about they which be not the exact truth . 
 it be for this rare , precious quality of truthfulness that I delight in 
 many dutch painting , which lofty - minded people despise . I find a source 
 of delicious sympathy in these faithful picture of a monotonous 
 homely existence , which have be the fate of so many more among my 
 fellow - mortal than a life of pomp or of absolute indigence , of tragic 
 suffering or of world - stir action . I turn , without shrink , from 
 cloud - bear angel , from prophet , sibyls , and heroic warrior , to an 
 old woman bend over her flower - pot , or eat her solitary dinner , 
 while the noonday light , soften perhaps by a screen of leaf , fall 
 on her mob - cap , and just touch the rim of her spinning - wheel , and 
 her stone jug , and all those cheap common thing which be the precious 
 necessary of life to she - or I turn to that village wedding , keep 
 between four brown wall , where an awkward bridegroom open the dance 
 with a high - shoulder , broad - faced bride , while elderly and middle - aged 
 friend look on , with very irregular nose and lip , and probably 
 with quart - pot in their hand , but with an expression of unmistakable 
 contentment and goodwill . " Foh ! " say my idealistic friend , " what vulgar 
 detail ! what good be there in take all these pain to give an exact 
 likeness of old woman and clown ? what a low phase of life ! what clumsy , 
 ugly people ! " 
 but bless we , thing may be lovable that be not altogether handsome , I 
 hope ? I be not at all sure that the majority of the human race have 
 not be ugly , and even among those " lord of their kind , " the british , 
 squat figure , ill - shapen nostril , and dingy complexion be not 
 startling exception . yet there be a great deal of family love amongst 
 we . I have a friend or two whose class of feature be such that the 
 Apollo curl on the summit of their brow would be decidedly try ; yet 
 to my certain knowledge tender heart have beat for they , and their 
 miniature - flatter , but still not lovely - be kiss in secret by 
 motherly lip . I have see many an excellent matron , who could have 
 never in her good day have be handsome , and yet she have a packet of 
 yellow love - letter in a private drawer , and sweet child shower 
 kiss on her sallow cheek . and I believe there have be plenty of 
 young hero , of middle stature and feeble beard , who have feel quite 
 sure they could never love anything more insignificant than a Diana , and 
 yet have find themselves in middle life happily settle with a wife who 
 waddle . yes ! thank God ; human feeling be like the mighty river that 
 bless the earth : it do not wait for beauty - it flow with resistless 
 force and bring beauty with it . 
 all honour and reverence to the divine beauty of form ! let we cultivate 
 it to the utmost in man , woman , and child - in our garden and in our 
 house . but let we love that other beauty too , which lie in no secret 
 of proportion , but in the secret of deep human sympathy . paint we an 
 angel , if you can , with a float violet robe , and a face pal by the 
 celestial light ; paint we yet oftener a Madonna , turn her mild face 
 upward and open her arm to welcome the divine glory ; but do not 
 impose on we any aesthetic rule which shall banish from the region of 
 art those old woman scrap carrot with their work - wear hand , those 
 heavy clown take holiday in a dingy pot - house , those rounded back 
 and stupid weather - beat face that have bend over the spade and do 
 the rough work of the world - those home with their tin pan , their 
 brown pitcher , their rough cur , and their cluster of onion . in 
 this world there be so many of these common coarse people , who have 
 no picturesque sentimental wretchedness ! it be so needful we should 
 remember their existence , else we may happen to leave they quite out of 
 our religion and philosophy and frame lofty theory which only fit 
 a world of extreme . therefore , let art always remind we of they ; 
 therefore let we always have man ready to give the love pain of a 
 life to the faithful representing of commonplace thing - man who see 
 beauty in these commonplace thing , and delight in show how kindly 
 the light of heaven fall on they . there be few prophet in the world ; 
 few sublimely beautiful woman ; few hero . I can not   afford to give all 
 my love and reverence to such rarity : I want a great deal of those 
 feeling for my every - day fellow - man , especially for the few in the 
 foreground of the great multitude , whose face I know , whose hand I 
 touch for whom I have to make way with kindly courtesy . neither be 
 picturesque lazzaroni or romantic criminal half so frequent as your 
 common labourer , who get his own bread and eat it vulgarly but 
 creditably with his own pocket - knife . it be more needful that I should 
 have a fibre of sympathy connect I with that vulgar citizen who 
 weigh out my sugar in a vilely assorted cravat and waistcoat , than with 
 the handsome rascal in red scarf and green feather - more needful that 
 my heart should swell with love admiration at some trait of gentle 
 goodness in the faulty people who sit at the same hearth with I , or in 
 the clergyman of my own parish , who be perhaps rather too corpulent and 
 in other respect be not an Oberlin or a Tillotson , than at the deed 
 of hero whom I shall never know except by hearsay , or at the sublime 
 abstract of all clerical grace that be ever conceive by an able 
 novelist . 
 and so I come back to Mr. Irwine , with whom I desire you to be in 
 perfect charity , far as he may be from satisfy your demand on the 
 clerical character . perhaps you think he be not - as he ought to have 
 be - a live demonstration of the benefit attach to a national 
 church ? but I be not sure of that ; at least I know that the people 
 in Broxton and Hayslope would have be very sorry to part with their 
 clergyman , and that most face brighten at his approach ; and until it 
 can be prove that hatred be a well thing for the soul than love , 
 I must believe that Mr. Irwine 's influence in his parish be a more 
 wholesome one than that of the zealous Mr. Ryde , who come there twenty 
 year afterwards , when Mr. Irwine have be gather to his father . it 
 be true , Mr. Ryde insist strongly on the doctrine of the Reformation , 
 visit his flock a great deal in their own home , and be severe 
 in rebuke the aberration of the flesh - put a stop , indeed , to the 
 Christmas round of the church singer , as promote drunkenness and 
 too light a handling of sacred thing . but I gather from Adam Bede , to 
 whom I talk of these matter in his old age , that few clergyman could 
 be less successful in win the heart of their parishioner than Mr. 
 Ryde . they learn a great many notion about doctrine from he , so 
 that almost every church - goer under fifty begin to distinguish as well 
 between the genuine gospel and what do not come precisely up to that 
 standard , as if he have be bear and breed a Dissenter ; and for some time 
 after his arrival there seem to be quite a religious movement in that 
 quiet rural district . " but , " say Adam , " I have   see pretty clear , ever 
 since I be a young un , as religion 's something else besides notion . it 
 be not   notion set people do the right thing - it be feeling . it be the 
 same with the notion in religion as it be with math'matics - a man may 
 be able to work problem straight off in 's head as he sit by the fire 
 and smoke his pipe , but if he have to make a machine or a building , he 
 must have a will and a resolution and love something else well than 
 his own ease . somehow , the congregation begin to fall off , and people 
 begin to speak light o ' Mr. Ryde . I believe he mean right at bottom ; 
 but , you see , he be sourish - temper , and be for beat down price 
 with the people as work for he ; and his preaching would not   go down 
 well with that sauce . and he want to be like my lord judge I ' the 
 parish , punish folk for do wrong ; and he scold them from 
 the pulpit as if he have be a Ranter , and yet he could not   abide the 
 dissenter , and be a deal more set against them than Mr. Irwine be . and 
 then he do not   keep within his income , for he seem to think at first 
 go - off that six hundred a - year be to make he as big a man as Mr. 
 Donnithorne . that be a sore mischief I have   often see with the poor 
 curate jump into a bit of a living all of a sudden . Mr. Ryde be a 
 deal think on at a distance , I believe , and he write book , but as for 
 math'matic and the natur o ' thing , he be as ignorant as a woman . he 
 be very know about doctrine , and use to call them the bulwark of 
 the Reformation ; but I have   always mistrust that sort o ' learning as 
 leave folk foolish and unreasonable about business . now Mester Irwine 
 be as different as could be : as quick ! - he understand what you mean in 
 a minute , and he know all about building , and could see when you have make 
 a good job . and he behave as much like a gentleman to the farmer , and 
 th ' old woman , and the labourer , as he do to the gentry . you never see 
 HIM interfere and scolding , and try to play th ' emperor . ah , he be 
 a fine man as ever you set eye on ; and so kind to 's mother and sister . 
 that poor sickly Miss Anne - he seem to think more of she than of 
 anybody else in the world . there be not   a soul in the parish have a word 
 to say against he ; and his servant stay with he till they be so 
 old and potter , he have to hire other folk to do their work . " 
 " well , " I say , " that be an excellent way of preach in the weekday ; 
 but I daresay , if your old friend Mr. Irwine be to come to life again , 
 and get into the pulpit next Sunday , you would be rather ashamed that he 
 do not   preach well after all your praise of he . " 
 " Nay , nay , " say Adam , broaden his chest and throw himself back in 
 his chair , as if he be ready to meet all inference , " nobody have ever 
 hear I say Mr. Irwine be much of a preacher . he do not   go into deep 
 speritial experience ; and I know there s a deal in a man 's inward life 
 as you can not   measure by the square , and say , ' do this and that will 
 follow , ' and , ' do that and this will follow . ' there 's thing go on in the 
 soul , and time when feeling come into you like a rush mighty wind , 
 as the scripture say , and part your life in two a'most , so you look 
 back on yourself as if you be somebody else . those be thing as you 
 can not   bottle up in a ' do this ' and ' do that ' ; and I will go so far with 
 the strong Methodist ever you will find . that show I there be deep 
 speritial thing in religion . you can not   make much out wi ' talk about 
 it , but you feel it . Mr. Irwine do not   go into those thing - he preach 
 short moral sermon , and that be all . but then he act pretty much 
 up to what he say ; he do not   set up for be so different from other 
 folk one day , and then be as like them as two pea the next . and he 
 make folk love he and respect he , and that be well nor stir 
 up their gall wi ' be overbusy . Mrs. Poyser use to say - you know she 
 would have her word about everything - she say , Mr. Irwine be like a 
 good meal o ' victual , you be the well for he without think on 
 it , and Mr. Ryde be like a dose o ' physic , he grip you and worrete

 " but do not   Mr. Ryde preach a great deal more about that spiritual part 
 of religion that you talk of , Adam ? could not   you get more out of his 
 sermon than out of Mr. Irwine 's ? " 
 " eh , I knowna . he preach a deal about doctrine . but I have   see pretty 
 clear , ever since I be a young un , as religion 's something else besides 
 doctrine and notion . I look at it as if the doctrine be like find 
 name for your feeling , so as you can talk of them when you have   never 
 know them , just as a man may talk o ' tool when he know their name , 
 though he be never so much as see them , still less handle them . I have 
 hear a deal o ' doctrine I ' my time , for I use to go after the 
 dissent preacher along wi ' Seth , when I be a lad o ' seventeen , and 
 get puzzle myself a deal about th ' Arminians and the Calvinists . the 
 Wesleyans , you know , be strong Arminians ; and Seth , who could never 
 abide anything harsh and be always for hop the good , hold fast by 
 the Wesleyans from the very first ; but I think I could pick a hole or 
 two in their notion , and I get dispute wi ' one o ' the class leader 
 down at Treddles'on , and harass he so , first o ' this side and then 
 o ' that , till at last he say , ' young man , it be the devil make use o ' 
 your pride and conceit as a weapon to war against the simplicity o ' 
 the truth . ' I could not   help laugh then , but as I be go home , I 
 think the man be not   far wrong . I begin to see as all this weighing 
 and sift what this text mean and that text mean , and whether folk 
 be save all by God 's grace , or whether there go an ounce o ' their 
 own will to't , be no part o ' real religion at all . you may talk o ' 
 these thing for hour on end , and you will only be all the more coxy and 
 conceite for't . so I take to go nowhere but to church , and hear 
 nobody but Mr. Irwine , for he say nothing but what be good and what 
 you would be the wiser for remembering . and I find it well for my soul 
 to be humble before the mystery o ' God 's dealing , and not be make 
 a clatter about what I could never understand . and they be poor foolish 
 question after all ; for what have we get either inside or outside of we 
 but what come from God ? if we have   get a resolution to do right , he give 
 it we , I reckon , first or last ; but I see plain enough we shall never do 
 it without a resolution , and that be enough for I . " 
 Adam , you perceive , be a warm admirer , perhaps a partial judge , of Mr. 
 Irwine , as , happily , some of we still be of the people we have know 
 familiarly . doubtless it will be despise as a weakness by that lofty 
 order of mind who pant after the ideal , and be oppress by a general 
 sense that their emotion be of too exquisite a character to find fit 
 object among their everyday fellowman . I have often be favour with 
 the confidence of these select nature , and find they to concur in 
 the experience that great man be overestimated and small man be 
 insupportable ; that if you would love a woman without ever look back 
 on your love as a folly , she must die while you be court she ; and if 
 you would maintain the slight belief in human heroism , you must never 
 make a pilgrimage to see the hero . I confess I have often meanly shrink 
 from confess to these accomplished and acute gentleman what my own 
 experience have be . I be afraid I have often smile with hypocritical 
 assent , and gratify they with an epigram on the fleeting nature of our 
 illusion , which any one moderately acquainted with french literature 
 can command at a moment 's notice . human converse , I think some wise 
 man have remark , be not rigidly sincere . but I herewith discharge my 
 conscience , and declare that I have have quite enthusiastic movement of 
 admiration towards old gentleman who speak the bad English , who be 
 occasionally fretful in their temper , and who have never move in a 
 high sphere of influence than that of parish overseer ; and that 
 the way in which I have come to the conclusion that human nature be 
 lovable - the way I have learn something of its deep pathos , its sublime 
 mystery - have be by live a great deal among people more or less 
 commonplace and vulgar , of whom you would perhaps hear nothing very 
 surprising if you be to inquire about they in the neighbourhood where 
 they dwelt . ten to one most of the small shopkeeper in their vicinity 
 see nothing at all in they . for I have observe this remarkable 
 coincidence , that the select nature who pant after the ideal , and 
 find nothing in pantaloon or petticoat great enough to command their 
 reverence and love , be curiously in unison with the narrow and 
 pettiest . for example , I have often hear Mr. Gedge , the landlord of 
 the Royal Oak , who use to turn a bloodshot eye on his neighbour in 
 the village of Shepperton , sum up his opinion of the people in his own 
 parish - and they be all the people he know - in these emphatic word : 
 " Aye , sir , I have   say it often , and I will say it again , they be a poor lot 
 I ' this parish - a poor lot , sir , big and little . " I think he have a 
 dim idea that if he could migrate to a distant parish , he might find 
 neighbour worthy of he ; and indeed he do subsequently transfer 
 himself to the saracen 's Head , which be do a thriving business in 
 the back street of a neighbouring market - town . but , oddly enough , he have 
 find the people up that back street of precisely the same stamp as the 
 inhabitant of Shepperton - " a poor lot , sir , big and little , and they 
 as come for a go o ' gin be no well than they as come for a pint o ' 
 twopenny - a poor lot . " 
 Chapter xviii 
 Church 
 " HETTY , Hetty , do not   you know church begin at two , and it be go half

 Hetty be come downstairs , and Mrs. Poyser , in her plain bonnet and

 and now the little procession set off . Mr. Poyser be in his Sunday suit

 the " little uns " address be Marty and Tommy , boy of nine and seven , 
 in little fustian tail coat and knee - breech , relieve by rosy

 you might have know it be Sunday if you have only wake up in the

 have be satisfied with a small bite than usual . the sunshine seem

 with their bill tuck under their wing ; on the old black sow

 excellent spring - bed on his mother 's fat rib ; on Alick , the shepherd , 
 in his new smock - frock , take an uneasy siesta , half - sit , 
 half - stand on the granary step . Alick be of opinion that church , 
 like other luxury , be not to be indulge in often by a foreman who 
 have the weather and the ewe on his mind . " Church ! Nay - I'n get 
 summat else to think on , " be an answer which he often utter in a tone 
 of bitter significance that silence further question . I feel sure 
 Alick mean no irreverence ; indeed , I know that his mind be not of a 
 speculative , negative cast , and he would on no account have miss go 
 to church on Christmas Day , Easter Sunday , and " whissuntide . " but he have 
 a general impression that public worship and religious ceremony , 
 like other non - productive employment , be intend for people who have 
 leisure . 
 " there be Father a - standing at the yard - gate , " say Martin Poyser . " I 
 reckon he want to watch we down the field . it be wonderful what sight he 
 have , and he turn seventy - five . " 
 " ah , I often think it be wi ' th ' old folk as it be wi ' the babbie , "

 Old Martin open the gate as he see the family procession approach , 
 and hold it wide open , lean on his stick - pleased to do this bit 
 of work ; for , like all old man whose life have be spend in labour , he 
 like to feel that he be still useful - that there be a well crop of 
 onion in the garden because he be by at the sowing - and that the cow

 the tall pew , about their illness and the total failure of doctor 's

 far preferable - about the servant , and their grow exorbitance as to 
 wage , whereas the quality of their service decline from year to year ,

 his solvency , notwithstanding that Mrs. Dingall be a sensible woman ,

 until Mr. Irwine be in the desk . they see no reason for that premature 
 entrance - what could they do in church if they be there before service 
 begin ? - and they do not conceive that any power in the universe 
 could take it ill of they if they stay out and talk a little about 
 " bus'ness . " 
 Chad Cranage look like quite a new acquaintance to - day , for he have get 
 his clean Sunday face , which always make his little granddaughter cry 
 at he as a stranger . but an experienced eye would have fix on he at

 horse to be shod must be treat with respect . Chad and the rougher 
 sort of workman keep aloof from the grave under the white thorn , 
 where the burial be go forward ; but Sandy Jim , and several of the 
 farm - labourer , make a group round it , and stand with their hat off , as 
 fellow - mourner with the mother and son . other hold a midway position , 
 sometimes watch the group at the grave , sometimes listen to the

 discern his fitness for the lead business ; curiously in contrast with

 could not be turn into cash . the talk be in rather a low tone than 
 usual to - day , hush a little by the sound of Mr. Irwine 's voice read

 Donnithorne himself , for that gentleman have the meanness to receive

 conversation be an additional reason for not be loud , since Satchell 
 himself might presently be walk up the paved road to the church door . 
 and soon they become suddenly silent ; for Mr. Irwine 's voice have cease , 
 and the group round the white thorn be disperse itself towards the 
 church . 
 they all move aside , and stand with their hat off , while Mr. Irwine 
 pass . Adam and Seth be come next , with their mother between they ; 
 for Joshua Rann officiate as head sexton as well as clerk , and be not

 the mother and son pass into the church , and one by one the 
 loiterer follow , though some still linger without ; the sight of Mr. 
 Donnithorne 's carriage , which be wind slowly up the hill , perhaps

 but presently the sound of the bassoon and the key - bugle burst forth ; 
 the evening hymn , which always open the service , have begin , and every 
 one must now enter and take his place . 
 I can not say that the interior of Hayslope Church be remarkable for 
 anything except for the grey age of its oaken pew - great square pew 
 mostly , range on each side of a narrow aisle . it be free , indeed , 
 from the modern blemish of gallery . the choir have two narrow pew to 
 themselves in the middle of the right - hand row , so that it be a short 
 process for Joshua Rann to take his place among they as principal bass , 
 and return to his desk after the singing be over . the pulpit and desk , 
 grey and old as the pew , stand on one side of the arch lead into 
 the chancel , which also have its grey square pew for Mr. Donnithorne 's 
 family and servant . yet I assure you these grey pew , with the 
 buff - wash wall , give a very pleasing tone to this shabby interior , 
 and agree extremely well with the ruddy face and bright waistcoat . 
 and there be liberal touch of crimson toward the chancel , for 
 the pulpit and Mr. Donnithorne 's own pew have handsome crimson cloth 
 cushion ; and , to close the vista , there be a crimson altar - cloth , 
 embroider with golden ray by Miss Lydia 's own hand . 
 but even without the crimson cloth , the effect must have be warm and 
 cheer when Mr. Irwine be in the desk , look benignly round on 
 that simple congregation - on the hardy old man , with bent knee and 
 shoulder , perhaps , but with vigour leave for much hedge - clip and 
 thatch ; on the tall stalwart frame and roughly cut bronzed face of 
 the stone - cutter and carpenter ; on the half - dozen well - to - do farmer , 
 with their apple - cheek family ; and on the clean old woman , mostly 
 farm - labourer ' wife , with their bit of snow - white cap - border under 
 their black bonnet , and with their wither arm , bare from the elbow , 
 fold passively over their chest . for none of the old people hold 
 book - why should they ? not one of they could read . but they know a 
 few " good word " by heart , and their withered lip now and then move 
 silently , follow the service without any very clear comprehension 
 indeed , but with a simple faith in its efficacy to ward off harm and 
 bring blessing . and now all face be visible , for all be stand 
 up - the little child on the seat peep over the edge of the grey 
 pew , while good Bishop Ken 's evening hymn be be sing to one of 
 those lively psalm - tune which die out with the last generation of 
 rector and choral parish clerk . melody die out , like the pipe of 
 Pan , with the ear that love they and listen for they . Adam be not in 
 his usual place among the singer to - day , for he sit with his mother 
 and Seth , and he notice with surprise that Bartle Massey be absent 
 too - all the more agreeable for Mr. Joshua Rann , who give out his bass 
 note with unusual complacency and throw an extra ray of severity into 
 the glance he send over his spectacle at the recusant will Maskery . 
 I beseech you to imagine Mr. Irwine look round on this scene , in his 
 ample white surplice that become he so well , with his powdered hair 
 throw back , his rich brown complexion , and his finely cut nostril and 
 upper lip ; for there be a certain virtue in that benignant yet keen 
 countenance as there be in all human face from which a generous soul 
 beam out . and over all stream the delicious June sunshine through the 
 old window , with their desultory patch of yellow , red , and blue , that 
 throw pleasant touch of colour on the opposite wall . 
 I think , as Mr. Irwine look round to - day , his eye rest an instant

 not to wander thither , and rest on that round pink - and - white figure . but 
 Hetty be at that moment quite careless of any glance - she be absorb
