 for the carriage must surely be at the church - gate by this time . she 
 have never see he since she part with he in the wood on Thursday 
 evening , and oh , how long the time have seem ! thing have go on just 
 the same as ever since that evening ; the wonder that have happen then 
 have bring no change after they ; they be already like a dream . when 
 she hear the church door swing , her heart beat so , she dare not 
 look up . she feel that her aunt be curtsy ; she curtsy herself . 
 that must be old Mr. Donnithorne - he always come first , the wrinkle 
 small old man , peer round with short - sighted glance at the bowing 
 and curtsying congregation ; then she know Miss Lydia be pass , 
 and though Hetty like so much to look at her fashionable little 
 coal - scuttle bonnet , with the wreath of small rose round it , she do not 
 mind it to - day . but there be no more curtsy - no , he be not come ; 
 she feel sure there be nothing else pass the pew door but the 
 house - keeper 's black bonnet and the lady 's maid 's beautiful straw hat 
 that have once be Miss Lydia 's , and then the powdered head of the 
 butler and footman . no , he be not there ; yet she would look now - she 
 might be mistake - for , after all , she have not look . so she lift

 chancel - there be no one but old Mr. Donnithorne rub his spectacle 
 with his white handkerchief , and Miss Lydia open the large gilt - edge 
 prayer - book . the chill disappointment be too hard to bear . she feel

 what fluctuation there be in her busy thought and feeling , while

 but Adam 's thought of Hetty do not deafen he to the service ; they 
 rather blend with all the other deep feeling for which the church 
 service be a channel to he this afternoon , as a certain consciousness 
 of our entire past and our imagined future blend itself with all our 
 moment of keen sensibility . and to Adam the church service be the 
 good channel he could have find for his mingled regret , yearning , and 
 resignation ; its interchange of beseech cry for help with outburst 
 of faith and praise , its recurrent response and the familiar rhythm of 
 its collect , seem to speak for he as no other form of worship could 
 have do ; as , to those early Christians who have worship from their 
 childhood upwards in catacomb , the torch - light and shadow must have 
 seem near the divine presence than the heathenish daylight of the 
 street . the secret of our emotion never lie in the bare object , but 
 in its subtle relation to our own past : no wonder the secret escape 
 the unsympathize observer , who might as well put on his spectacle to 
 discern odour . 
 but there be one reason why even a chance comer would have find the 
 service in Hayslope Church more impressive than in most other village 
 nook in the kingdom - a reason of which I be sure you have not the 
 slight suspicion . it be the reading of our friend Joshua Rann . where 
 that good shoemaker get his notion of reading from remain a mystery 
 even to his most intimate acquaintance . I believe , after all , he get it 
 chiefly from Nature , who have pour some of her music into this honest 
 conceited soul , as she have be know to do into other narrow soul 
 before his . she have give he , at least , a fine bass voice and a musical 
 ear ; but I can not positively say whether these alone have suffice to 
 inspire he with the rich chant in which he deliver the response . 
 the way he roll from a rich deep forte into a melancholy cadence , 
 subsiding , at the end of the last word , into a sort of faint resonance , 
 like the linger vibration of a fine violoncello , I can compare to 
 nothing for its strong calm melancholy but the rush and cadence of the 
 wind among the autumn bough . this may seem a strange mode of speak 
 about the reading of a parish clerk - a man in rusty spectacle , with 
 stubbly hair , a large occiput , and a prominent crown . but that be 
 Nature 's way : she will allow a gentleman of splendid physiognomy and 
 poetic aspiration to sing woefully out of tune , and not give he the 
 slight hint of it ; and take care that some narrow - browed fellow , 
 troll a ballad in the corner of a pot - house , shall be as true to his 
 interval as a bird . 
 Joshua himself be less proud of his reading than of his singing , and it 
 be always with a sense of heighten importance that he pass from the 
 desk to the choir . still more to - day : it be a special occasion , for an 
 old man , familiar to all the parish , have die a sad death - not in his 
 bed , a circumstance the most painful to the mind of the peasant - and 
 now the funeral psalm be to be sing in memory of his sudden departure . 
 moreover , Bartle Massey be not at church , and Joshua 's importance in 
 the choir suffer no eclipse . it be a solemn minor strain they sing . 
 the old psalm - tune have many a wail among they , and the word - 
 Thou sweep'st we off as with a flood ; 
 we vanish hence like dream - 
 seem to have a close application than usual in the death of poor 
 Thias . the mother and son listen , each with peculiar feeling . 
 Lisbeth have a vague belief that the psalm be do her husband good ; it 
 be part of that decent burial which she would have think it a great 
 wrong to withhold from he than to have cause he many unhappy day

 try to recall , as he have do continually since his father 's death ,

 consciousness at the last might be a moment of pardon and reconcilement ;

 divine dealing be not measure and circumscribe by time ? Adam have 
 never be unable to join in a psalm before . he have know plenty of

 because the chief source of his past trouble and vexation be for ever

 between we ; I never forget what I owe you when I be a lad ; you forgive

 father : his thought run constantly on what the old man 's feeling have 
 be in moment of humiliation , when he have hold down his head before

 silence , we be apt to feel twinge of doubt afterwards as to our own 
 generosity , if not justice ; how much more when the object of our anger 
 have go into everlasting silence , and we have see his face for the 
 last time in the meekness of death ! 
 " ah ! I be always too hard , " Adam say to himself . " it be a sore fault in 
 I as I be so hot and out o ' patience with people when they do wrong , and 
 my heart get shut up against them , so as I can not   bring myself to forgive 
 them . I see clear enough there be more pride nor love in my soul , for I 
 could soon make a thousand stroke with th ' hammer for my father than 
 bring myself to say a kind word to he . and there go plenty o ' pride 
 and temper to the stroke , as the devil will be have his finger in 
 what we call our duty as well as our sin . mayhap the good thing I

 allay be easy for I to work nor to sit still , but the real tough 
 job for I ' ud be to master my own will and temper and go right against 
 my own pride . it seem to I now , if I be to find Father at home

 nothing ' ud be a lesson to we if it do not   come too late . it be well we 
 should feel as life be a reckoning we can not   make twice over ; there be 
 no real making amend in this world , any more nor you can mend a wrong 
 subtraction by do your addition right . " 
 this be the key - note to which Adam 's thought have perpetually return 
 since his father 's death , and the solemn wail of the funeral psalm 
 be only an influence that bring back the old thought with strong 
 emphasis . so be the sermon , which Mr. Irwine have choose with reference

 midst of life we be in death " - how the present moment be all we can 
 call our own for work of mercy , of righteous dealing , and of family 
 tenderness . all very old truth - but what we think the old truth

 dead face of one who have make a part of our own life . for when man want 
 to impress we with the effect of a new and wonderfully vivid light , do 
 they not let it fall on the most familiar object , that we may measure 
 its intensity by remember the former dimness ? 
 then come the moment of the final blessing , when the forever sublime 
 word , " the peace of God , which passeth all understanding , " seem to

 th ' country ; and well you may , for I remember poor Thias as fine a

 Mr. Craig be never aware that his conversation and advance be

 " they look pretty fur , though , " say Mr. Poyser , turn his head on one

 come truer nor that pictur o ' the cock wi ' the big spur , as have get its

 as th ' Bible . why , th ' cock 's France , an ' th ' anchor 's Nelson - an ' they

 " Pee - ee - eh ! " say Mr. Craig . " a man doesna want to see fur to know as 
 th ' English ' ull beat the French . why , I know upo ' good authority as

 Mr. Craig smile and wink slowly as he make this last observation ,

 so Adam and Seth , with their mother between they , wound their way down 
 to the valley and up again to the old house , where a sadden memory have 
 take the place of a long , long anxiety - where Adam would never have to 
 ask again as he enter , " where be Father ? " 
 and the other family party , with Mr. Craig for company , go back to 
 the pleasant bright house - place at the Hall Farm - all with quiet mind ,

 be pleasant to she again if her Thursday night 's vision be not to be 
 fulfil ; and in this moment of chill , bare , wintry disappointment and 
 doubt , she look towards the possibility of be with Arthur again , 
 of meet his love glance , and hear his soft word with that eager 
 yearning which one may call the " grow pain " of passion . 
 Chapter XIX 
 Adam on a Working Day 
 notwithstanding Mr. Craig 's prophecy , the dark - blue cloud disperse 
 itself without have produce the threaten consequence . " the 
 weather " - as he observe the next morning - " the weather , you see , be 
 a ticklish thing , an ' a fool ' ull hit on't   sometimes when a wise man 
 miss ; that be why the almaneck get so much credit . it be one o ' they 
 chancy thing as fool thrive on . " 
 this unreasonable behaviour of the weather , however , could displease no 
 one else in Hayslope besides Mr. Craig . all hand be to be out in 
 the meadow this morning as soon as the dew have rise ; the wife and 
 daughter do double work in every farmhouse , that the maid might give 
 their help in toss the hay ; and when Adam be march along the 
 lane , with his basket of tool over his shoulder , he catch the sound 
 of jocose talk and ringing laughter from behind the hedge . the jocose 
 talk of hay - maker be good at a distance ; like those clumsy bell round 
 the cow ' neck , it have rather a coarse sound when it come close , 
 and may even grate on your ear painfully ; but hear from far off , it 
 mingle very prettily with the other joyous sound of nature . man 's 
 muscle move well when their soul be make merry music , though 
 their merriment be of a poor blundering sort , not at all like the 
 merriment of bird . 
 and perhaps there be no time in a summer 's day more cheering than when 
 the warmth of the sun be just begin to triumph over the freshness 
 of the morning - when there be just a lingering hint of early coolness 
 to keep off languor under the delicious influence of warmth . the reason 
 Adam be walk along the lane at this time be because his work for 
 the rest of the day lie at a country - house about three mile off , which 
 be be put in repair for the son of a neighbouring squire ; and he 
 have be busy since early morning with the packing of panel , door , 
 and chimney - piece , in a waggon which be now go on before he , while 
 Jonathan Burge himself have ride to the spot on horseback , to await its 
 arrival and direct the workman . 
 this little walk be a rest to Adam , and he be unconsciously under 
 the charm of the moment . it be summer morning in his heart , and he see 
 Hetty in the sunshine - a sunshine without glare , with slant ray 
 that tremble between the delicate shadow of the leaf . he think , 
 yesterday when he put out his hand to she as they come out of church , 
 that there be a touch of melancholy kindness in her face , such as he 
 have not see before , and he take it as a sign that she have some sympathy 
 with his family trouble . poor fellow ! that touch of melancholy come from 
 quite another source , but how be he to know ? we look at the one little 
 woman 's face we love as we look at the face of our mother earth , and see 
 all sort of answer to our own yearning . it be impossible for Adam 
 not to feel that what have happen in the last week have bring the 
 prospect of marriage near to he . Hitherto he have feel keenly the 
 danger that some other man might step in and get possession of Hetty 's 
 heart and hand , while he himself be still in a position that make he 
 shrink from ask she to accept he . even if he have have a strong hope 
 that she be fond of he - and his hope be far from be strong - he 
 have be too heavily burden with other claim to provide a home for 
 himself and Hetty - a home such as he could expect she to be content with 
 after the comfort and plenty of the Farm . like all strong nature , Adam 
 have confidence in his ability to achieve something in the future ; he 
 feel sure he should some day , if he live , be able to maintain a family 
 and make a good broad path for himself ; but he have too cool a head not 
 to estimate to the full the obstacle that be to be overcome . and the 
 time would be so long ! and there be Hetty , like a bright - cheek apple 
 hang over the orchard wall , within sight of everybody , and everybody 
 must long for she ! to be sure , if she love he very much , she would be 
 content to wait for he : but do she love he ? his hope have never rise

 go to the Farm ; but it be impossible to come to any but fluctuate 
 conclusion about Hetty 's feeling . she be like a kitten , and have the 
 same distractingly pretty look , that mean nothing , for everybody that 
 come near she . 
 but now he could not help say to himself that the heavy part of 
 his burden be remove , and that even before the end of another year 
 his circumstance might be bring into a shape that would allow he to 
 think of marry . it would always be a hard struggle with his mother , 
 he know : she would be jealous of any wife he might choose , and she have 
 set her mind especially against Hetty - perhaps for no other reason than 
 that she suspect Hetty to be the woman he have choose . it would never 
 do , he fear , for his mother to live in the same house with he when 
 he be marry ; and yet how hard she would think it if he ask she to 
 leave he ! yes , there be a great deal of pain to be go through with 
 his mother , but it be a case in which he must make she feel that his 
 will be strong - it would be well for she in the end . for himself , 
 he would have like that they should all live together till Seth be 
 married , and they might have build a bit themselves to the old house , 
 and make more room . he do not like " to part wi ' th ' lad " : they have 
 hardly every be separate for more than a day since they be bear . 
 but Adam have no soon catch his imagination leap forward in this 
 way - make arrangement for an uncertain future - than he check 
 himself . " a pretty building I be make , without either brick or 
 timber . I be up I ' the garret a'ready , and have not   so much as dig the 
 foundation . " whenever Adam be strongly convinced of any proposition , it 
 take the form of a principle in his mind : it be knowledge to be act 
 on , as much as the knowledge that damp will cause rust . perhaps here lay 
 the secret of the hardness he have accuse himself of : he have too 
 little fellow - feeling with the weakness that err in spite of foreseen 
 consequence . without this fellow - feeling , how be we to get enough 
 patience and charity towards our stumbling , fall companion in the 
 long and changeful journey ? and there be but one way in which a strong 
 determined soul can learn it - by get his heart - string bind 
 round the weak and err , so that he must share not only the outward 
 consequence of their error , but their inward suffering . that be a long 
 and hard lesson , and Adam have at present only learn the alphabet of it 
 in his father 's sudden death , which , by annihilate in an instant all 
 that have stimulate his indignation , have send a sudden rush of thought 
 and memory over what have claim his pity and tenderness . 
 but it be Adam 's strength , not its correlative hardness , that 
 influence his meditation this morning . he have long make up his mind 
 that it would be wrong as well as foolish for he to marry a blooming 
 young girl , so long as he have no other prospect than that of grow 
 poverty with a grow family . and his saving have be so constantly 
 draw upon   besides the terrible sweep of pay for Seth 's substitute 
 in the militia   that he have not enough money beforehand to furnish even 
 a small cottage , and keep something in reserve against a rainy day . he 
 have good hope that he should be " firm on his leg " by and by ; but he 
 could not be satisfied with a vague confidence in his arm and brain ; he 
 must have definite plan , and set about they at once . the partnership 
 with Jonathan Burge be not to be think of at present - there be 
 thing implicitly tack to it that he could not accept ; but Adam 
 think that he and Seth might carry on a little business for themselves 
 in addition to their journeyman 's work , by buy a small stock of 
 superior wood and make article of household furniture , for which Adam 
 have no end of contrivance . Seth might gain more by work at separate 
 job under Adam 's direction than by his journeyman 's work , and Adam , 
 in his overhour , could do all the " nice " work that require peculiar 
 skill . the money gain in this way , with the good wage he receive 
 as foreman , would soon enable they to get beforehand with the world , 
 so sparingly as they would all live now . no soon have this little 
 plan shape itself in his mind than he begin to be busy with exact 
 calculation about the wood to be buy and the particular article of 
 furniture that should be undertake first - a kitchen cupboard of his 
 own contrivance , with such an ingenious arrangement of slide - door and 
 bolt , such convenient nook for stow household provender , and such 
 a symmetrical result to the eye , that every good housewife would be 
 in rapture with it , and fall through all the gradation of melancholy 
 longing till her husband promise to buy it for she . Adam picture to 
 himself Mrs. Poyser examine it with her keen eye and try in vain to 
 find out a deficiency ; and , of course , close to Mrs. Poyser stand Hetty ,

 dream and hope . yes , he would go and see she this evening - it be so 
 long since he have be at the Hall Farm . he would have like to go

 yesterday , for he fear his old friend be ill ; but , unless he could 
 manage both visit , this last must be put off till to - morrow - the desire

 as he make up his mind to this , he be come very near to the end of 
 his walk , within the sound of the hammer at work on the refitting of 
 the old house . the sound of tool to a clever workman who love his work 
 be like the tentative sound of the orchestra to the violinist who 
 have to bear his part in the overture : the strong fibre begin their 
 accustomed thrill , and what be a moment before joy , vexation , or 
 ambition , begin its change into energy . all passion become strength 
 when it have an outlet from the narrow limit of our personal lot in the 
 labour of our right arm , the cunning of our right hand , or the still , 
 creative activity of our thought . look at Adam through the rest of the 
 day , as he stand on the scaffolding with the two - foot ruler in 
 his hand , whistle low while he consider how a difficulty about a 
 floor - joist or a window - frame be to be overcome ; or as he push one of 
 the young workman aside and take his place in upheave a weight of 
 timber , say , " let alone , lad ! Thee'st get too much gristle I ' thy 
 bone yet " ; or as he fix his keen black eye on the motion of a 
 workman on the other side of the room and warn he that his distance 
 be not right . look at this broad - shoulder man with the bare muscular 
 arm , and the thick , firm , black hair toss about like trodden 
 meadow - grass whenever he take off his paper cap , and with the strong 
 barytone voice burst every now and then into loud and solemn 
 psalm - tune , as if seek an outlet for superfluous strength , yet 
 presently check himself , apparently cross by some thought which 
 jar with the singing . perhaps , if you have not be already in 
 the secret , you might not have guess what sad memory what warm 
 affection , what tender flutter hope , have their home in this athletic 
 body with the broken finger - nail - in this rough man , who know no well 
 lyric than he could find in the Old and New Version and an occasional 
 hymn ; who know the small possible amount of profane history ; and for 
 whom the motion and shape of the earth , the course of the sun , and the 
 change of the season lie in the region of mystery just make visible by 
 fragmentary knowledge . it have cost Adam a great deal of trouble and 
 work in overhour to know what he know over and above the secret of his 
 handicraft , and that acquaintance with mechanic and figure , and the 
 nature of the material he work with , which be make easy to he by 
 inborn inherit faculty - to get the mastery of his pen , and write a 
 plain hand , to spell without any other mistake than must in fairness be 
 attribute to the unreasonable character of orthography rather than to 
 any deficiency in the speller , and , moreover , to learn his musical note 
 and part - singing . besides all this , he have read his Bible , include 
 the apocryphal book ; Poor Richard 's Almanac , Taylor 's Holy Living and 
 Dying , the Pilgrim 's Progress , with Bunyan 's Life and Holy War , a great 
 deal of Bailey 's Dictionary , Valentine and Orson , and part of a history 
 of Babylon , which Bartle Massey have lend he . he might have have many 
 more book from Bartle Massey , but he have no time for read " the 
 commin print , " as Lisbeth call it , so busy as he be with figure in 
 all the leisure moment which he do not fill up with extra carpentry . 
 Adam , you perceive , be by no mean a marvellous man , nor , properly 
 speaking , a genius , yet I will not pretend that his be an ordinary 
 character among workman ; and it would not be at all a safe conclusion 
 that the next good man you may happen to see with a basket of tool over 
 his shoulder and a paper cap on his head have the strong conscience and 
 the strong sense , the blend susceptibility and self - command , of our 
 friend Adam . he be not an average man . yet such man as he be rear 
 here and there in every generation of our peasant artisan - with an 
 inheritance of affection nurture by a simple family life of common 
 need and common industry , and an inheritance of faculty train 
 in skilful courageous labour : they make their way upwards , rarely as 
 genius , most commonly as painstaking honest man , with the skill and 
 conscience to do well the task that lie before they . their life have 
 no discernible echo beyond the neighbourhood where they dwelt , but you 
 be almost sure to find there some good piece of road , some building , 
 some application of mineral produce , some improvement in farming 
 practice , some reform of parish abuse , with which their name be 
 associate by one or two generation after they . their employer be 
 the rich for they , the work of their hand have wear well , and the work 
 of their brain have guide well the hand of other man . they go about 
 in their youth in flannel or paper cap , in coat black with coal - dust 
 or streak with lime and red paint ; in old age their white hair be 
 see in a place of honour at church and at market , and they tell their 
 well - dress son and daughter , seat round the bright hearth on 
 winter evening , how pleased they be when they first earn their 
 twopence a - day . other there be who die poor and never put off the 
 workman 's coal on weekday . they have not have the art of get rich , 
 but they be man of trust , and when they die before the work be all out 
 of they , it be as if some main screw have get loose in a machine ; the 
 master who employ they say , " where shall I find their like ? " 
 chapter XX 
 Adam visit the Hall Farm

 change his clothe - and be ready to set out to the Hall Farm when it 
 still want a quarter to seven . 
 " what be thee get thy Sunday cloose on for ? " say Lisbeth complainingly , 
 as he come downstairs . " Thee artna go to th ' school I ' thy good 
 coat ? " 
 " no , Mother , " say Adam , quietly . " I be go to the Hall Farm , but 
 mayhap I may go to the school after , so thee mustna wonder if I be a 
 bit late . Seth ' ull be at home in half an hour - he be only go to the 
 village ; so thee wutna mind . " 
 " eh , an ' what be thee get thy good cloose on for to go to th ' Hall Farm ? 
 the Poyser folk see'd thee in them yesterday , I warrand . what dost mean 
 by turnin ' worki'day into Sunday a - that'n ? it be poor keepin ' company wi ' 
 folk as donna like to see thee I ' thy workin ' jacket . "

 nought to do but to sit by hersen an ' think on thee ? "

 like . so let we have no more word about it . "

 tooth 's all come . "

 recurrent sensation of a sweet odour breathe in a far - off hour

 and Hetty ? you know quite well that Adam be mistake about she . like

 chapter XXI 
 the Night - School and the Schoolmaster 
 Bartle Massey 's be one of a few scatter house on the edge of a 
 common , which be divide by the road to Treddleston . Adam reach it 
 in a quarter of an hour after leave the Hall Farm ; and when he have his 
 hand on the door - latch , he could see , through the curtainless window , 
 that there be eight or nine head bend over the desk , light by 
 thin dip . 
 when he enter , a reading lesson be go forward and Bartle Massey 
 merely nod , leave he to take his place where he please . he have 
 not come for the sake of a lesson to - night , and his mind be too full 
 of personal matter , too full of the last two hour he have pass in 
 Hetty 's presence , for he to amuse himself with a book till school be 
 over ; so he sit down in a corner and look on with an absent mind . it 
 be a sort of scene which Adam have behold almost weekly for year ; he 
 know by heart every arabesque flourish in the framed speciman of Bartle 
 Massey 's handwriting which hang over the schoolmaster 's head , by way of 
 keep a lofty ideal before the mind of his pupil ; he know the back 
 of all the book on the shelf run along the whitewashed wall above 
 the peg for the slate ; he know exactly how many grain be go out 
 of the ear of indian corn that hang from one of the rafter ; he have long 
 ago exhaust the resource of his imagination in try to think 
 how the bunch of leathery seaweed have look and grow in its native 
 element ; and from the place where he sit , he could make nothing of the 
 old map of England that hang against the opposite wall , for age have 
 turn it of a fine yellow brown , something like that of a well - season 
 meerschaum . the drama that be go on be almost as familiar as the 
 scene , nevertheless habit have not make he indifferent to it , and even 
 in his present self - absorb mood , Adam feel a momentary stirring of the 
 old fellow - feeling , as he look at the rough man painfully hold pen 
 or pencil with their cramp hand , or humbly labour through their 
 reading lesson . 
 the reading class now seat on the form in front of the schoolmaster 's 
 desk consist of the three most backward pupil . Adam would have know 
 it only by see Bartle Massey 's face as he look over his spectacle , 
 which he have shift to the ridge of his nose , not require they for 
 present purpose . the face wear its mild expression : the grizzle 
 bushy eyebrow have take their more acute angle of compassionate 
 kindness , and the mouth , habitually compress with a pout of the low 
 lip , be relax so as to be ready to speak a helpful word or syllable 
 in a moment . this gentle expression be the more interesting because the 
 schoolmaster 's nose , an irregular aquiline twist a little on one side , 
 have rather a formidable character ; and his brow , moreover , have that 
 peculiar tension which always impress one as a sign of a keen 
 impatient temperament : the blue vein stand out like cord under the 
 transparent yellow skin , and this intimidating brow be soften by no 
 tendency to baldness , for the grey bristly hair , cut down to about an 
 inch in length , stand round it in as close rank as ever . 
 " Nay , Bill , nay , " Bartle be say in a kind tone , as he nod to 
 Adam , " begin that again , and then perhaps , it will come to you what d - r - y 
 spell . it be the same lesson you read last week , you know . " 
 " Bill " be a sturdy fellow , aged four - and - twenty , an excellent 
 stone - sawyer , who could get as good wage as any man in the trade of his 
 year ; but he find a reading lesson in word of one syllable a hard 
 matter to deal with than the hard stone he have ever have to saw . the 
 letter , he complain , be so " uncommon alike , there be no tellin ' 
 them one from another , " the sawyer 's business not be concern with 
 minute difference such as exist between a letter with its tail 
 turn up and a letter with its tail turn down . but Bill have a firm 
 determination that he would learn to read , found chiefly on two 
 reason : first , that Tom Hazelow , his cousin , could read anything " right 
 off , " whether it be print or writing , and Tom have send he a letter 
 from twenty mile off , say how he be prosper in the world and have 
 get an overlooker 's place ; secondly , that Sam Phillips , who saw with 
 he , have learn to read when he be turn twenty , and what could be 
 do by a little fellow like Sam Phillips , Bill consider , could 
 be do by himself , see that he could pound Sam into wet clay if 
 circumstance require it . so here he be , point his big finger 
 towards three word at once , and turn his head on one side that he 
 might keep well hold with his eye of the one word which be to be 
 discriminate out of the group . the amount of knowledge Bartle Massey

 " now , you see , you do not   do this thing a bit well than you do a

 Book Three 
 Chapter XXII 
 go to the Birthday Feast 
 the thirtieth of July be come , and it be one of those half - dozen warm 
 day which sometimes occur in the middle of a rainy English summer . no 
 rain have fall for the last three or four day , and the weather be 
 perfect for that time of the year : there be less dust than usual on 
 the dark - green hedge - row and on the wild camomile that star the 
 roadside , yet the grass be dry enough for the little child to roll 
 on it , and there be no cloud but a long dash of light , downy ripple , 
 high , high up in the far - off blue sky . perfect weather for an outdoor 
 July merry - making , yet surely not the good time of year to be bear in . 
 Nature seem to make a hot pause just then : all the lovely flower 
 be go ; the sweet time of early growth and vague hope be past ; and 
 yet the time of harvest and ingathere be not come , and we tremble at 
 the possible storm that may ruin the precious fruit in the moment 
 of its ripeness . the wood be all one dark monotonous green ; the 
 waggon - load of hay no long creep along the lane , scatter their 
 sweet - smell fragment on the blackberry branch ; the pasture be 
 often a little tan , yet the corn have not get its last splendour 
 of red and gold ; the lamb and calf have lose all trace of their 
 innocent frisky prettiness , and have become stupid young sheep and cow . 
 but it be a time of leisure on the farm - that pause between hay - and 
 corn - harvest , and so the farmer and labourer in Hayslope and Broxton 
 think the captain do well to come of age just then , when they could 
 give their undivided mind to the flavour of the great cask of ale which 
 have be brew the autumn after " the heir " be bear , and be to be 
 tap on his twenty - first birthday . the air have be merry with the 
 ringing of church - bell very early this morning , and every one have make 
 haste to get through the needful work before twelve , when it would be 
 time to think of get ready to go to the Chase . 
 the midday sun be stream into Hetty 's bedchamber , and there be no 
 blind to temper the heat with which it fall on her head as she look at

 in which she could see her neck and arm , for the small hanging 
 glass she have fetch out of the next room - the room that have be 
 Dinah 's - would show she nothing below her little chin ; and that 
 beautiful bit of neck where the roundness of her cheek melt into 
 another roundness shadow by dark delicate curl . and to - day she 
 think more than usual about her neck and arm ; for at the dance this 
 evening she be not to wear any neckerchief , and she have be busy 
 yesterday with she spot pink - and - white frock , that she might make the 
 sleeve either long or short at will . she be dress now just as she 
 be to be in the evening , with a tucker make of " real " lace , which she 
 aunt have lend she for this unparalleled occasion , but with no ornament 
 besides ; she have even take out her small round ear - ring which she wear 
 every day . but there be something more to be do , apparently , before 
 she put on her neckerchief and long sleeve , which she be to wear in 
 the day - time , for now she unlock the drawer that hold her private 
 treasure . it be more than a month since we see her unlock that drawer 
 before , and now it hold new treasure , so much more precious than the 
 old one that these be thrust into the corner . Hetty would not care to 
 put the large coloured glass ear - ring into her ear now ; for see ! she 
 have get a beautiful pair of gold and pearl and garnet , lie snugly in 
 a pretty little box line with white satin . oh , the delight of take 
 out that little box and look at the ear - ring ! do not reason about 
 it , my philosphical reader , and say that Hetty , be very pretty , must 
 have know that it do not signify whether she have on any ornament 
 or not ; and that , moreover , to look at ear - ring which she could not 
 possibly wear out of her bedroom could hardly be a satisfaction , the 
 essence of vanity be a reference to the impression produce 
 on other ; you will never understand woman 's nature if you be so 
 excessively rational . try rather to divest yourself of all your rational 
 prejudice , as much as if you be study the psychology of a canary 
 bird , and only watch the movement of this pretty round creature as she 
 turn her head on one side with an unconscious smile at the ear - ring 
 nestle in the little box . ah , you think , it be for the sake of the 
 person who have give they to she , and her thought be go back now to 
 the moment when they be put into her hand . no ; else why should she 
 have care to have ear - ring rather than anything else ? and I know that 
 she have long for ear - ring from among all the ornament she could 
 imagine . 
 " little , little ear ! " Arthur have say , pretend to pinch they one 
 evening , as Hetty sit beside he on the grass without her hat . " I wish I 
 have some pretty ear - ring ! " she say in a moment , almost before she know 
 what she be say - the wish lie so close to her lip , it would flutter 
 past they at the slight breath . and the next day - it be only last 
 week - Arthur have ride over to Rosseter on purpose to buy they . that 
 little wish so naively utter seem to he the prettiest bit of 
 childishness ; he have never hear anything like it before ; and he have 
 wrap the box up in a great many cover , that he might see Hetty 
 unwrap it with grow curiosity , till at last her eye flash back 
 their new delight into his . 
 no , she be not think most of the giver when she smile at the 
 ear - ring , for now she be take they out of the box , not to press they

 Hetty 's dream of the future could not make she insensible to triumph in

 Chapter XXIII 
 Dinner - Time 
 when Adam hear that he be to dine upstairs with the large tenant , he 
 feel rather uncomfortable at the idea of be exalt in this way above

 Mr. Mills , the butler , assure he that Captain Donnithorne have give

 there . 
 Adam nod and go up to Seth , who be stand a few yard off . 
 " Seth , lad , " he say , " the captain have send to say I be to dine

 and mother , as if I be well than my own flesh and blood . thee't not

 " Nay , nay , lad , " say Seth , " thy honour be our honour ; and if thee get'st

 " I know what to do , never fear , " say Bartle , move on . " the news will 
 be good sauce to my dinner . aye , aye , my boy , you will get on . I will back

 any man in this county and you have   have good teaching - you have   have good

 when they get upstairs , the question which Arthur have leave unsettled , as

 " it stand to sense , " Mr. Casson be say , " as old Mr. Poyser , as be

 " why , the broad man , " say Bartle ; " and then he will not   take up other 
 folk ' room ; and the next broad must sit at bottom . "

 the Health - Drinking

 Chapter XXV 
 the Games 
 the great dance be not to begin until eight o'clock , but for any lad 
 and lass who like to dance on the shady grass before then , there be 
 music always at hand - for be not the band of the Benefit Club capable 
 of play excellent jig , reel , and hornpipe ? and , besides this , 
 there be a grand band hire from Rosseter , who , with their wonderful 
 wind - instrument and puff - out cheek , be themselves a delightful 
 show to the small boy and girl . to say nothing of Joshua Rann 's 
 fiddle , which , by an act of generous forethought , he have provide 
 himself with , in case any one should be of sufficiently pure taste to 
 prefer dance to a solo on that instrument . 
 meantime , when the sun have move off the great open space in front of 
 the house , the game begin . there be , of course , well - soap pole 
 to be climb by the boy and youth , race to be run by the old woman , 
 race to be run in sack , heavy weight to be lift by the strong man , 
 and a long list of challenge to such ambitious attempt as that 
 of walk as many yard possible on one leg - feat in which it be 
 generally remark that Wiry Ben , be " the lissom'st , springest fellow 
 I ' the country , " be sure to be pre - eminent . to crown all , there be to 
 be a donkey - race - that sublimest of all race , conduct on the grand 
 socialistic idea of everybody encourage everybody else 's donkey , and 
 the sorriest donkey win . 
 and soon after four o'clock , splendid old Mrs. Irwine , in her damask

 there be a sunk fence in front of the marquee , divide the lawn from

 " ah , I remember hear about she . but there be no end of people here

 near - sighted eye always wear the good . I want very strong spectacle to

 " see , " say Arthur , " the old woman be ready to set out on their race 
 now . which do you bet on , Gawaine ? " 
 " the long - legged one , unless they be go to have several heat , and 
 then the little wiry one may win . " 
 " there be the Poysers , Mother , not far off on the right hand , " say 
 Miss Irwine . " Mrs. Poyser be look at you . do take notice of she . "

 " no , Mother , " and Mr. Irwine , reply to her last word ; " I can not

 " bless I , Dauphin , what do an old bachelor like you know about it ? "

 married man , because they have time for more general contemplation .

 the Dance 
 arthur have choose the entrance - hall for the ballroom : very wisely , for

 come to curtsy and take leave ; " I think we should not part with any of

 " oh , your Honour , it be all right and proper for gentlefolk to stay up

 Book Four 
 Chapter XXVII 
 a crisis 
 it be beyond the middle of August - nearly three week after the 
 birthday feast . the reaping of the wheat have begin in our north midland 
 county of Loamshire , but the harvest be likely still to be retard 
 by the heavy rain , which be cause inundation and much damage 
 throughout the country . from this last trouble the Broxton and Hayslope 
 farmer , on their pleasant upland and in their brook - water 
 valley , have not suffer , and as I can not pretend that they be such 
 exceptional farmer as to love the general good well than their own , 
 you will infer that they be not in very low spirit about the rapid 
 rise in the price of bread , so long as there be hope of gather in 
 their own corn undamaged ; and occasional day of sunshine and dry 
 wind flatter this hope . 
 the eighteenth of August be one of these day when the sunshine look 
 brighter in all eye for the gloom that go before . grand mass of 
 cloud be hurry across the blue , and the great round hill behind the

 seem only part of the general gladness because the sun be shin . a

 and yet a day on which a blighting sorrow may fall upon a man . for if it

 Chapter XXVIII 
 A Dilemma 
 it be only a few minute measure by the clock - though Adam always 
 think it have be a long while - before he perceive a gleam of 
 consciousness in Arthur 's face and a slight shiver through his frame . 
 the intense joy that flood his soul bring back some of the old

 Chapter XXX 
 the Delivery of the Letter

 dear brother SETH - your letter lie three day beyond my knowing of it 
 at the post , for I have not money enough by I to pay the carriage , this 
 be a time of great need and sickness here , with the rain that have 
 fall , as if the window of heaven be open again ; and to lay 
 by money , from day to day , in such a time , when there be so many in 
 present need of all thing , would be a want of trust like the lay 
 up of the manna . I speak of this , because I would not have you think I 
 slow to answer , or that I have small joy in your rejoicing at the worldly 
 good that have befall your brother Adam . the honour and love you bear 
 he be nothing but meet , for God have give he great gift , and he use 
 they as the patriarch Joseph do , who , when he be exalt to a place of 
 power and trust , yet yearn with tenderness towards his parent and his 
 young brother . 
 " my heart be knit to your aged mother since it be grant I to be near 
 she in the day of trouble . speak to she of I , and tell she I often bear 
 she in my thought at evening time , when I be sit in the dim light 
 as I do with she , and we hold one another 's hand , and I speak the 
 word of comfort that be give to I . ah , that be a blessed time , 
 be not   it , Seth , when the outward light be fade , and the body be a 
 little weary with its work and its labour . then the inward light 
 shin the brighter , and we have a deep sense of rest on the Divine 
 strength . I sit on my chair in the dark room and close my eye , and it 
 be as if I be out of the body and could feel no want for evermore . for 
 then , the very hardship , and the sorrow , and the blindness , and the sin 
 I have beheld and be ready to weep over - yea , all the anguish of the 
 child of man , which sometimes wrap I round like sudden darkness - I 
 can bear with a willing pain , as if I be share the Redeemer 's cross . 
 for I feel it , I feel it - infinite love be suffer too - yea , in the 
 fulness of knowledge it suffer , it yearn , it mourn ; and that be a 
 blind self - seeking which want to be free from the sorrow wherewith 
 the whole creation groaneth and travaileth . surely it be not true 
 blessedness to be free from sorrow , while there be sorrow and sin in the 
 world : sorrow be then a part of love , and love do not seek to throw it 
 off . it be not the spirit only that tell I this - I see it in the whole 
 work and word of the Gospel . be there not plead in heaven ? be not the 
 Man of Sorrows there in that crucify body wherewith he ascend ? and 
 be he not one with the Infinite Love itself - as our love be one with our 
 sorrow ? 
 " these thought have be much bear in on I of late , and I have see 
 with new clearness the meaning of those word , ' if any man love I , let 
 he take up my cross . ' I have hear this enlarge on as if it mean the 
 trouble and persecution we bring on ourselves by confess Jesus . but

 sin and sorrow of this world - that be what lie heavy on his heart - and

 with his sorrow . 
 " in my outward lot , which you ask about , I have all thing and abound . I

 " farewell , dear brother - and yet not farewell . for those child of

 never more be sunder though the hill may lie between . for their soul

 Sister and fellow - worker in Christ , 
 " DINAH MORRIS . " 
 " I have not skill to write the word so small as you do and my pen move

 chapter xxxi 
 in Hetty 's Bed - Chamber 
 it be no long light enough to go to bed without a candle , even in

 now she would read her letter . it must - it must have comfort in it . how

 she set down the candle and take out the letter . it have a faint scent of

 " DEAREST HETTY - I have speak truly when I have say that I love you , 
 and I shall never forget our love . I shall be your true friend as long 
 as life last , and I hope to prove this to you in many way . if I say 
 anything to pain you in this letter , do not believe it be for want of 
 love and tenderness towards you , for there be nothing I would not do 
 for you , if I know it to be really for your happiness . I can not bear to 
 think of my little Hetty shed tear when I be not there to kiss they 
 away ; and if I follow only my own inclination , I should be with she 
 at this moment instead of write . it be very hard for I to part from 
 her - hard still for I to write word which may seem unkind , though 
 they spring from the true kindness . 
 " dear , dear Hetty , sweet as our love have be to I , sweet as it would 
 be to I for you to love I always , I feel that it would have be 
 well for we both if we have never have that happiness , and that it be 
 my duty to ask you to love I and care for I as little as you can . the 
 fault have all be mine , for though I have be unable to resist the 
 longing to be near you , I have feel all the while that your affection 
 for I might cause you grief . I ought to have resist my feeling . I 
 should have do so , if I have be a well fellow than I be ; but now , 
 since the past can not be alter , I be bind to save you from any evil 
 that I have power to prevent . and I feel it would be a great evil for 
 you if your affection continue so fixed on I that you could think of 
 no other man who might be able to make you happy by his love than I 
 ever can , and if you continue to look towards something in the future 
 which can not possibly happen . for , dear Hetty , if I be to do what you 
 one day speak of , and make you my wife , I should do what you yourself 
 would come to feel be for your misery instead of your welfare . I know 
 you can never be happy except by marry a man in your own station ; and 
 if I be to marry you now , I should only be add to any wrong I have 
 do , besides offend against my duty in the other relation of life . 
 you know nothing , dear Hetty , of the world in which I must always live , 
 and you would soon begin to dislike I , because there would be so little 
 in which we should be alike . 
 " and since I can not marry you , we must part - we must try not to feel 
 like lover any more . I be miserable while I say this , but nothing else 
 can be . be angry with I , my sweet one , I deserve it ; but do not believe 
 that I shall not always care for you - always be grateful to you - always 
 remember my Hetty ; and if any trouble should come that we do not now 
 foresee , trust in I to do everything that lie in my power . 
 " I have tell you where you be to direct a letter to , if you want to 
 write , but I put it down below lest you should have forget . do not 
 write unless there be something I can really do for you ; for , dear 
 Hetty , we must try to think of each other as little as we can . forgive 
 I , and try to forget everything about I , except that I shall be , as 
 long as I live , your affectionate friend , 
 " arthur DONNITHORNE . " 
 slowly Hetty have read this letter ; and when she look up from it there 
 be the reflection of a blanched face in the old dim glass - a white

 a child 's pain in it . Hetty do not see the face - she see nothing - she

 the shattering of all her little dream - world , the crush blow on

 there be a feeble dawn in the room when Hetty awoke , a little after

 she have no tear this morning . she have weep they all away last night ,

 every morning to come , as far as her imagination could stretch , she

 she pause in the midst of her languid undressing and lean against the

 so she lock up her drawer and go away to her early work . 
 in the evening , when Mr. Poyser be smoke his pipe , and his

 " I should like it - I should like it well than farm - work . "

 " strange ! " perhaps you will say , " this rush of impulse to - ward a course

 yes , the action of a little trivial soul like Hetty 's , struggle

 " let that man bear the loss who loose it from its mooring . " 
 but that will not save the vessel - the pretty thing that might have be

 " you be likely to find Mr. Thurle an excellent neighbour , I assure

 plan I be go to mention , especially as I hope you will find it as much 
 to your own advantage as his . " 
 " indeed , sir , if it be anything t ' our advantage , it will be the first 
 offer o ' the sort I have   hear on . it be they as take advantage that get 
 advantage I ' this world , I think . folk have to wait long enough afore 
 it be bring to them . " 
 " the fact be , Poyser , " say the squire , ignore Mrs. Poyser 's theory of 
 worldly prosperity , " there be too much dairy land , and too little plough 
 land , on the Chase Farm to suit Thurle 's purpose - indeed , he will only

 be not a clever dairy - woman , like your . now , the plan I be think of 
 be to effect a little exchange . if you be to have the Hollow Pastures ,

 wife 's management ; and I should request you , Mrs. Poyser , to supply my 
 house with milk , cream , and butter at the market price . on the other 
 hand , Poyser , you might let Thurle have the Lower and Upper Ridges , 
 which really , with our wet season , would be a good riddance for you . 
 there be much less risk in dairy land than corn land . " 
 Mr. Poyser be lean forward , with his elbow on his knee , his head 
 on one side , and his mouth screw up - apparently absorb in make the 
 tip of his finger meet so as to represent with perfect accuracy the 
 rib of a ship . he be much too acute a man not to see through the whole

 any day ; and , after all , it matter more to his wife than to he . so ,

 dost say ? " 
 Mrs. Poyser have have her eye fix on her husband with cold severity

 go into other folk 's pocket . I know there be they as be bear t ' own 
 the land , and they as be bear to sweat on't " - here Mrs. Poyser pause

 their better as fur as flesh and blood ' ull bear it ; but I will not make

 myself as if I be a churn wi ' butter a - come in't , for no landlord in 
 England , not if he be King George himself . " 
 " no , no , my dear Mrs. Poyser , certainly not , " say the squire , still 
 confident in his own power of persuasion , " you must not overwork 
 yourself ; but do not   you think your work will rather be lessen than 
 increase in this way ? there be so much milk require at the Abbey 
 that you will have little increase of cheese and butter making from

 profitable way of dispose of dairy produce , be it not ? " 
 " Aye , that be true , " say Mr. Poyser , unable to repress an opinion on a 
 question of farming profit , and forget that it be not in this case 
 a purely abstract question . 
 " I daresay , " say Mrs. Poyser bitterly , turn her head half - way

 dinner . how do I know whether the milk ' ull be want constant ? what be

 alone pay for it ; and we must fat pig till we be oblige to beg the 
 butcher on our knee to buy them , and lose half of them wi ' the measle .

 for a man an ' hoss - that be to be take out o ' the profit , I reckon ? but 
 there be folk ' ud hold a sieve under the pump and expect to carry away 
 the water . " 
 " that difficulty - about the fetching and carry - you will not have , 
 Mrs. Poyser , " say the squire , who think that this entrance into 
 particular indicate a distant inclination to compromise on Mrs. 
 Poyser 's part . " Bethell will do that regularly with the cart and pony . "

 gentlefolk 's servant come about my back place , a - make love to

 to be thrust out of the discussion in this way would have be enough to

 " then , sir , if I may speak - as , for all I be a woman , and there be folk

 there be occasion on which two servant - girl and a waggoner may be a 
 formidable audience , and as the squire ride away on his black pony , even

 " Thee'st do it now , " say Mr. Poyser , a little alarmed and uneasy , but

 " yes , I know I have   do it , " say Mrs. Poyser ; " but I have   have my say out ,

 More link 
 the barley be all carry at last , and the harvest supper go by 
 without wait for the dismal black crop of bean . the apple and 
 nut be gather and store ; the scent of whey depart from the

 " no , no , Mother , " say Mr. Irwine ; " it be a little bit of irregular

 have take notice of the quarrel , else I shall lose the little good

 " well , I like that woman even well than her cream - cheese , " say Mrs. 
 Irwine . " she have the spirit of three man , with that pale face of her . 
 and she say such sharp thing too . " 
 " sharp ! yes , her tongue be like a new - set razor . she be quite original 
 in her talk too ; one of those untaught wit that help to stock a country 
 with proverb . I tell you that capital thing I hear she say about 
 Craig - that he be like a cock , who think the sun have rise to hear 
 he crow . now that be an AEsop 's fable in a sentence . "

 " oh , that must not be ; and Poyser be such a good tenant that Donnithorne 
 be likely to think twice , and digest his spleen rather than turn they

 Irwine . " it strike I on Arthur 's birthday that the old man be a little 
 shake : he be eighty - three , you know . it be really an unconscionable age . 
 it be only woman who have a right to live as long as that . " 
 " when they have   get old - bachelor son who would be forlorn without they , " 
 say Mr. Irwine , laugh , and kiss his mother 's hand . 
 Mrs. Poyser , too , meet her husband 's occasional foreboding of a notice 
 to quit with " there be no knowing what may happen before Lady day " - one 
 of those undeniable general proposition which be usually intend to 
 convey a particular meaning very far from undeniable . but it be really 
 too hard upon human nature that it should be hold a criminal offence to

 apart from this foreboding , thing go on much as usual in the Poyser 
 household . Mrs. Poyser think she notice a surprising improvement 
 in Hetty . to be sure , the girl get " close temper , and sometimes she 
 seem as if there would be no draw a word from she with cart - rope , "

 Adam himself watch she at first with tremble anxiety , which give

 possibly you think that Adam be not at all sagacious in his

 have nothing more than her beauty to recommend she , attribute imaginary

 rule without exception . of course , I know that , as a rule , sensible 
 man fall in love with the most sensible woman of their acquaintance , 
 see through all the pretty deceit of coquettish beauty , never imagine 
 themselves love when they be not love , cease love on all 
 proper occasion , and marry the woman most fit for they in every

 lady in their neighbourhood . but even to this rule an exception will

 one . for my own part , however , I respect he none the less - nay , I think

 it any weakness , pray , to be work on by exquisite music ? to feel its

 delicate fibre of life where no memory can penetrate , and bind

 be scatter through the toilsome year , concentrate in one

 self - renounce sympathy , blend your present joy with past sorrow and

 a weakness to be so wrought upon by the exquisite curve of a woman 's 
 cheek and neck and arm , by the liquid depth of her beseech eye , or

 like music : what can one say more ? Beauty have an expression beyond and 
 far above the one woman 's soul that it clothe , as the word of genius 
 have a wide meaning than the thought that prompt they . it be more 
 than a woman 's love that move we in a woman 's eye - it seem to be a 
 far - off mighty love that have come near to we , and make speech for itself 
 there ; the rounded neck , the dimpled arm , move we by something more 
 than their prettiness - by their close kinship with all we have know 
 of tenderness and peace . the noble nature see the most of this 
 impersonal expression in beauty   it be needless to say that there be 
 gentleman with whisker dye and undyed who see none of it whatever , 
 and for this reason , the noble nature be often the most blind to 
 the character of the one woman 's soul that the beauty clothe . whence , I 
 fear , the tragedy of human life be likely to continue for a long time 
 to come , in spite of mental philosopher who be ready with the good 
 receipt for avoid all mistake of the kind . 
 our good Adam have no fine word into which he could put his feeling for 
 Hetty : he could not disguise mystery in this way with the appearance of 
 knowledge ; he call his love frankly a mystery , as you have hear he . 
 he only know that the sight and memory of she move he deeply , touch

 Chapter XXXIV 
 the Betrothal 
 it be a dry Sunday , and really a pleasant day for the 2d of November . 
 there be no sunshine , but the cloud be high , and the wind be so 
 still that the yellow leaf which flutter down from the hedgerow elm 
 must have fall from pure decay . nevertheless , Mrs. Poyser do not go

 the Hidden Dread

 Book five 
 chapter xxxvi 
 the Journey of Hope 
 a long , lonely journey , with sadness in the heart ; away from the 
 familiar to the strange : that be a hard and dreary thing even to the 
 rich , the strong , the instructed ; a hard thing , even when we be call 
 by duty , not urge by dread . 
 what be it then to Hetty ? with her poor narrow thought , no long

 to lie on the wool - pack , with a cranny leave between the curtain of the

 Chapter XXXVII 
 the Journey in despair

 here , that she find word at once . 
 " I lose my way , " she say . " I be travel - north'ard , and I get away 
 from the road into the field , and be overtake by the dark . will you 
 tell I the way to the near village ? " 
 she get up as she be speak , and put her hand to her bonnet to

 answer , for some second . then he turn away and walk towards the

 you will be so good as show I how to get to it . "

 way on ? " the man say , still more gruffly . " anybody ' ud think you be a

 he look slowly at the sixpence , and then say , " I want none o ' your 
 money . you would well take care on't , else you will get it stool from yer ,

 the man leave she without further speech , and Hetty hold on her way . 
 another day have rise , and she must wander on . it be no use to think of 
 drown herself - she could not do it , at least while she have money leave 
 to buy food and strength to journey on . but the incident on her wake 
 this morning heighten her dread of that time when her money would be 
 all go ; she would have to sell her basket and clothe then , and she 
 would really look like a beggar or a wild woman , as the man have say .

 wonder look at she , be as full of dread as death - it be bad ; it

 she take out her money from her purse , and look at it . she have still

 she must wander on and on , and wait for a low depth of despair to give 
 her courage . perhaps death would come to she , for she be get less 
 and less able to bear the day 's weariness . and yet - such be the strange 
 action of our soul , draw we by a lurking desire towards the very 
 end we dread - Hetty , when she set out again from Norton , ask the 
 straight road northward towards Stonyshire , and keep it all that

 Poor wander Hetty , with the rounded childish face and the hard , 
 unloving , despairing soul look out of it - with the narrow heart 
 and narrow thought , no room in they for any sorrow but her own , and 
 taste that sorrow with the more intense bitterness ! my heart bleed 
 for she as I see she toil along on her weary foot , or seat in 
 a cart , with her eye fix vacantly on the road before she , never 
 think or care whither it tend , till hunger come and make she 
 desire that a village may be near . 
 what will be the end , the end of her objectless wandering , apart from 
 all love , care for human being only through her pride , cling to 
 life only as the hunt wound brute cling to it ? 
 God preserve you and I from be the beginner of such misery ! 
 Chapter XXXVIII 
 the Quest 
 the first ten day after Hetty 's departure pass as quietly as any

 Dark and cheerless be the morn

 Glad my eye and warm my heart .

 Pierce the gloom of sin and grief -

 Scatter all my unbelief . 
 More and more thyself display , 
 shin to the perfect day .

 chapter XXXIX 
 the Tidings

 Chapter XL 
 the Bitter Waters Spread

 chapter XLI 
 the Eve of the Trial 
 AN upper room in a dull Stoniton street , with two bed in it - one lay

 you would hardly have know it be Adam without be tell . his face have

 " Adam , he will know - he will suffer , long and bitterly . he have a heart

 chapter XLII 
 the Morning of the Trial 
 AT one o'clock the next day , Adam be alone in his dull upper room ; 
 his watch lie before he on the table , as if he be count the 
 long minute . he have no knowledge of what be likely to be say by 
 the witness on the trial , for he have shrink from all the particular 
 connect with Hetty 's arrest and accusation . this brave active man , who 
 would have hasten towards any danger or toil to rescue Hetty from an 
 apprehend wrong or misfortune , feel himself powerless to contemplate 
 irremediable evil and suffering . the susceptibility which would have 
 be an impelling force where there be any possibility of action become 
 helpless anguish when he be oblige to be passive , or else seek an 
 active outlet in the thought of inflict justice on Arthur . energetic 
 nature , strong for all strenuous deed , will often rush away from a 
 hopeless sufferer , as if they be hard - hearted . it be the overmaster 
 sense of pain that drive they . they shrink by an ungovernable instinct , 
 as they would shrink from laceration . Adam have bring himself to think 
 of see Hetty , if she would consent to see he , because he think the

 see her change face , as a timid woman tremble at the thought of 
 the surgeon 's knife , and he choose now to bear the long hour of suspense

 witness her trial . 
 Deep unspeakable suffering may well be call a baptism , a regeneration , 
 the initiation into a new state . the yearn memory , the bitter

 Right - all the intense emotion which have fill the day and night of 
 the past week , and be compress themselves again like an eager crowd

 always before think it a light thing that man should suffer , as if all 
 that he have himself endure and call sorrow before be only a moment 's 
 stroke that have never leave a bruise . doubtless a great anguish may do 
 the work of year , and we may come out from that baptism of fire with a 
 soul full of new awe and new pity . 
 " o God , " Adam groan , as he lean on the table and look blankly at

 poor helpless young thing have suffer like she .... such a little while

 only to have have some good news to bring to you , my poor lad . "

 " why , the chief evidence yet have be the doctor ; all but Martin

 " Frightened , very frightened , when they first bring she in ; it be the

 " but the other evidence ... do it go hard against she ! " say Adam . " what

 chapter XLIII 
 the Verdict

 Chapter XLIV 
 Arthur 's Return 
 when Arthur Donnithorne land at Liverpool and read the letter from 
 his Aunt Lydia , briefly announce his grand - father 's death , his first 
 feeling be , " Poor Grandfather ! I wish I could have get to he to be 
 with he when he die . he might have feel or wish something at the 
 last that I shall never know now . it be a lonely death . "

 nature - only in human pretence - for a young man like Arthur , with a fine

 other think well of he , and have a very ardent intention to give 
 they more and more reason for that good opinion - it be not possible for

 himself rid over the hill in the breezy autumn day , look after 
 favourite plan of drainage and enclosure ; then admire on sombre

 what - Hetty ? 
 yes ; for Arthur be at ease about Hetty - not quite at ease about the

 pleasant the crack of the post - boy 's whip ! pleasant the sense of be

 day , he go back into his dressing - room to open his letter . the level 
 ray of the low afternoon sun enter directly at the window , and as 
 Arthur seat himself in his velvet chair with their pleasant warmth 
 upon he , he be conscious of that quiet well - being which perhaps you 
 and I have feel on a sunny afternoon when , in our bright youth and 
 health , life have open a new vista for we , and long to - morrow of 
 activity have stretch before we like a lovely plain which there be no 
 need for hurrying to look at , because it be all our own . 
 the top letter be place with its address upwards : it be in Mr. 
 Irwine 's handwriting , Arthur see at once ; and below the address be 
 write , " to be deliver as soon as he arrive . " nothing could have 
 be less surprising to he than a letter from Mr. Irwine at that 
 moment : of course , there be something he wish Arthur to know early 
 than it be possible for they to see each other . at such a time as that 
 it be quite natural that Irwine should have something press to say . 
 Arthur break the seal with an agreeable anticipation of soon see the 
 writer . 
 " I send this letter to meet you on your arrival , Arthur , because I may 
 then be at Stoniton , whither I be call by the most painful duty it have 
 ever be give I to perform , and it be right that you should know what 
 I have to tell you without delay . 
 " I will not attempt to add by one word of reproach to the retribution 
 that be now fall on you : any other word that I could write at this 
 moment must be weak and unmeaning by the side of those in which I must 
 tell you the simple fact . 
 " Hetty Sorrel be in prison , and will be try on Friday for the crime of 
 child - murder . " ... 
 Arthur read no more . he start up from his chair and stand for a single 
 minute with a sense of violent convulsion in his whole frame , as if the 
 life be go out of he with horrible throb ; but the next minute he 
 have rush out of the room , still clutch the letter - he be hurry 
 along the corridor , and down the stair into the hall . mill be still 
 there , but Arthur do not see he , as he pass like a hunted man across 
 the hall and out along the gravel . the butler hurry out after he 
 as fast as his elderly limb could run : he guess , he know , where the 
 young squire be go . 
 when Mills get to the stable , a horse be be saddle , and Arthur be 
 force himself to read the remain word of the letter . he thrust 
 it into his pocket as the horse be lead up to he , and at that moment 
 catch sight of Mills ' anxious face in front of he . 
 " tell they I be go - go to Stoniton , " he say in a muffled tone of 
 agitation - spring into the saddle , and set off at a gallop . 
 Chapter XLV 
 in the Prison 
 NEAR sunset that evening an elderly gentleman be stand with his back 
 against the small entrance - door of Stoniton jail , say a few last 
 word to the depart chaplain . the chaplain walk away , but the 
 elderly gentleman stand still , look down on the pavement and stroke 
 his chin with a ruminate air , when he be rouse by a sweet clear 
 woman 's voice , say , " can I get into the prison , if you please ? " 
 he turn his head and look fixedly at the speaker for a few moment 
 without answer . 
 " I have see you before , " he say at last . " do you remember preach on 
 the village green at Hayslope in Loamshire ? " 
 " yes , sir , surely . be you the gentleman that stay to listen on 
 horseback ? " 
 " yes . why do you want to go into the prison ? " 
 " I want to go to Hetty Sorrel , the young woman who have be condemn 
 to death - and to stay with she , if I may be permit . have you power in 
 the prison , sir ? " 
 " yes ; I be a magistrate , and can get admittance for you . but do you 
 know this criminal , Hetty Sorrel ? " 
 " yes , we be kin . my own aunt marry her uncle , Martin Poyser . but I 
 be away at leed , and do not   know of this great trouble in time to get 
 here before to - day . I entreat you , sir , for the love of our heavenly 
 Father , to let I go to she and stay with she . " 
 " how do you know she be condemn to death , if you be only just come 
 from leed ? " 
 " I have see my uncle since the trial , sir . he be go back to his home 
 now , and the poor sinner be forsake of all . I beseech you to get leave 
 for I to be with she . " 
 " what ! have you courage to stay all night in the prison ? she be very 
 sullen , and will scarcely make answer when she be speak to . " 
 " oh , sir , it may please God to open her heart still . do not   let we 
 delay . " 
 " come , then , " say the elderly gentleman , ring and gain admission , 
 " I know you have a key to unlock heart . " 
 Dinah mechanically take off her bonnet and shawl as soon as they be 
 within the prison court , from the habit she have of throw they off

 the jailer 's room , she lay they down on a chair unthinkingly . there be

 even when she be speak , her soul be in prayer reposing on an unseen 
 support . 
 after speak to the jailer , the magistrate turn to she and say ,

 hear you preach , and recognize he in court to - day , ill as he look . "

 remember . " 
 " close by here . I inquire all about he of Mr. Irwine . he lodge over 
 a tinman 's shop , in the street on the right hand as you enter the 
 prison . there be an old school - master with he . now , good - bye : I wish 
 you success . " 
 " Farewell , sir . I be grateful to you . " 
 as Dinah cross the prison court with the turnkey , the solemn evening 
 light seem to make the wall high than they be by day , and the 
 sweet pale face in the cap be more than ever like a white flower on 
 this background of gloom . the turnkey look askance at she all the

 voice would be grate just then . he strike a light as they enter the

 civil tone , " it will be pretty nigh dark in the cell a'ready , but I can 
 stop with my light a bit , if you like . " 
 " Nay , friend , thank you , " say Dinah . " I wish to go in alone . " 
 " as you like , " say the jailer , turn the harsh key in the lock and

 to waken she . 
 the door close again , and the only light in the cell be that of the 
 evening sky , through the small high grating - enough to discern human

 again there be a slight startled movement through Hetty 's frame ,

 listen . 
 " Hetty ... Dinah be come to you . " 
 after a moment 's pause , Hetty lift her head slowly and timidly from

 " do not   you know I , Hetty ? do not   you remember Dinah ? do you think I 
 would not   come to you in trouble ? " 
 Hetty keep her eye fix on Dinah 's face - at first like an animal that

 " I be come to be with you , Hetty - not to leave you - to stay with you - to 
 be your sister to the last . " 
 slowly , while Dinah be speak , Hetty rise , take a step forward , and

 not a word be speak . Dinah wait , hop for a spontaneous word from

 feeling nor pain again ? if you could believe he love you and would

 " but I can not   know anything about it , " Hetty say , with sullen sadness .

 " Jesus , thou present Saviour ! Thou hast know the depth of all sorrow :

 " let we pray , poor sinner . let we fall on our knee again , and pray to 
 the God of all mercy . " 
 Chapter XLVI 
 the hour of Suspense 
 ON Sunday morning , when the church bell in Stoniton be ring for 
 morning service , Bartle Massey re - enter Adam 's room , after a short 
 absence , and say , " Adam , here be a visitor want to see you . " 
 Adam be seat with be back towards the door , but he start up and 
 turn round instantly , with a flushed face and an eager look . his face 
 be even thin and more worn than we have see it before , but he be 
 wash and shaven this Sunday morning . 
 " be it any news ? " he say . 
 " keep yourself quiet , my lad , " say Bartle ; " keep quiet . it be not what 
 you be think of . it be the young Methodist woman come from the prison . 
 she be at the bottom o ' the stair , and want to know if you think 
 well to see she , for she have something to say to you about that poor

 he be stand with his face towards the door , and as Dinah enter ,

 they could neither of they say any more just yet , but stand before each

 pardon . Mr. Irwine say there be hope . he say , I need not   quite give it

 " I can not , " Adam say . " I can not   say good - bye while there be any hope . I be

 " I will not urge you against the voice of your own heart , " say Dinah .

 " not i , lad - not I. " 
 so the night wear on in agitation till the chill dawn and the grow

 there would soon be no more suspense . 
 " let we go to the prison now , Mr. Massey , " say Adam , when he see the

 chapter XLVII 
 the last Moment 
 it be a sight that some people remember well even than their own 
 sorrow - the sight in that grey clear morning , when the fatal cart 
 with the two young woman in it be descry by the waiting watch 
 multitude , cleave its way towards the hideous symbol of a deliberately 
 inflict sudden death . 
 all Stoniton have hear of Dinah Morris , the young Methodist woman who 
 have bring the obstinate criminal to confess , and there be as much 
 eagerness to see she as to see the wretched Hetty . 
 but Dinah be hardly conscious of the multitude . when Hetty have

 " close your eye , Hetty , " Dinah say , " and let we pray without cease 
 to God . " 
 and in a low voice , as the cart go slowly along through the midst of 
 the gaze crowd , she pour forth her soul with the wrestling intensity

 clutch she as the only visible sign of love and pity . 
 Dinah do not know that the crowd be silent , gaze at she with a sort 
 of awe - she do not even know how near they be to the fatal spot , when 
 the cart stop , and she shrink appal at a loud shout hideous to her 
 ear , like a vast yell of demon . Hetty 's shriek mingle with the sound , 
 and they clasp each other in mutual horror . 
 but it be not a shout of execration - not a yell of exultant cruelty . 
 it be a shout of sudden excitement at the appearance of a horseman 
 cleave the crowd at full gallop . the horse be hot and distressed , but 
 answer to the desperate spurring ; the rider look as if his eye be 
 glaze by madness , and he see nothing but what be unseen by other . 
 see , he have something in his hand - he be hold it up as if it be a 
 signal . 
 the Sheriff know he : it be Arthur Donnithorne , carry in his hand a 
 hard - win release from death .

 Book Six 
 chapter XLIX 
 at the Hall Farm 
 the first autumnal afternoon sunshine of 1801 - more than eighteen month 
 after that parting of Adam and Arthur in the Hermitage - be on the 
 yard at the Hall Farm ; and the bull - dog be in one of his most excited 
 moment , for it be that hour of the day when the cow be be drive 
 into the yard for their afternoon milking . no wonder the patient beast 
 run confusedly into the wrong place , for the alarming din of the 
 bull - dog be mingle with more distant sound which the timid feminine 
 creature , with pardonable superstition , imagine also to have some 
 relation to their own movement - with the tremendous crack of the 
 waggoner 's whip , the roar of his voice , and the booming thunder of the 
 waggon , as it leave the rick - yard empty of its golden load . 
 the milking of the cow be a sight Mrs. Poyser love , and at this 
 hour on mild day she be usually stand at the house door , with she 
 knitting in her hand , in quiet contemplation , only heighten to a 
 keener interest when the vicious yellow cow , who have once kick over a 
 pailful of precious milk , be about to undergo the preventive punishment 
 of have her hinder - leg strap . 
 to - day , however , Mrs. Poyser give but a divided attention to the

 outward change now discernible in our old friend , or in the pleasant 
 house - place , bright with polished oak and pewter . 
 " I never see the like to you , Dinah , " Mrs. Poyser be say , " when

 Chapter L 
 in the Cottage 
 ADAM do not ask Dinah to take his arm when they get out into the lane . 
 he have never yet do so , often as they have walk together , for he have 
 observe that she never walk arm - in - arm with Seth , and he think , 
 perhaps , that kind of support be not agreeable to she . so they walk 
 apart , though side by side , and the close poke of her little black 
 bonnet hide her face from he . 
 " you can not   be happy , then , to make the Hall Farm your home , Dinah ? " 
 Adam say , with the quiet interest of a brother , who have no anxiety for 
 himself in the matter . " it be a pity , see they be so fond of you . " 
 " you know , Adam , my heart be as their heart , so far as love for they 
 and care for their welfare go , but they be in no present need . their 
 sorrow be heal , and I feel that I be call back to my old work , in 
 which I find a blessing that I have miss of late in the midst of too 
 abundant worldly good . I know it be a vain thought to flee from the work 
 that God appoint we , for the sake of find a great blessing to our 
 own soul , as if we could choose for ourselves where we shall find the 
 fulness of the Divine Presence , instead of seek it where alone it 
 be to be find , in love obedience . but now , I believe , I have a clear 
 show that my work lie elsewhere - at least for a time . in the year 
 to come , if my aunt 's health should fail , or she should otherwise need 
 I , I shall return . " 
 " you know well , Dinah , " say Adam . " I do not   believe you would go against the 
 wish of they that love you , and be akin to you , without a good and 
 sufficient reason in your own conscience . I have   no right to say anything 
 about my be sorry : you know well enough what cause I have to put you 
 above every other friend I have   get ; and if it have be order so that 
 you could ha ' be my sister , and live with we all our life , I should 
 ha ' count it the great blessing as could happen to we now . but 
 Seth tell I there be no hope o ' that : your feeling be different , and 
 perhaps I be take too much upon I to speak about it . " 
 Dinah make no answer , and they walk on in silence for some yard , till 
 they come to the stone stile , where , as Adam have pass through first 
 and turn round to give she his hand while she mount the unusually 
 high step , she could not prevent he from see her face . it strike 
 he with surprise , for the grey eye , usually so mild and grave , have 
 the bright uneasy glance which accompany suppress agitation , and
