 heighten to a deep rose - colour . she look as if she be only sister 
 to Dinah . Adam be silent with surprise and conjecture for some moment , 
 and then he say , " I hope I have   not hurt or displease you by what I have 
 say , Dinah . perhaps I be make too free . I have   no wish different from 
 what you see to be good , and I be satisfied for you to live thirty mile 
 off , if you think it right . I shall think of you just as much as I do 
 now , for you be bind up with what I can no more help remember than I 
 can help my heart beat . " 
 Poor Adam ! thus do man blunder . Dinah make no answer , but she presently 
 say , " have you hear any news from that poor young man , since we last 
 speak of he ? " 
 Dinah always call Arthur so ; she have never lose the image of he as 
 she have see he in the prison . 
 " yes , " say Adam . " Mr. Irwine read I part of a letter from he 
 yesterday . it be pretty certain , they say , that there will be a peace soon ,

 come home . he be no heart for it yet , and it be well for other that he

 a sorrowful letter . he ask about you and the Poysers , as he always 
 do . there be one thing in the letter cut I a good deal : ' you can not 
 think what an old fellow I feel , ' he say ; ' I make no scheme now . I be 
 the good when I have   a good day 's march or fight before I . ' " 
 " he be of a rash , warm - hearted nature , like Esau , for whom I have always 
 feel great pity , " say Dinah . " that meeting between the brother , where 
 Esau be so loving and generous , and Jacob so timid and distrustful , 
 notwithstanding his sense of the Divine favour , have always touch I 
 greatly . truly , I have be tempt sometimes to say that Jacob be of a 
 mean spirit . but that be our trial : we must learn to see the good in the

 " ah , " say Adam , " I like to read about Moses well , in th ' Old Testament . 
 he carry a hard business well through , and die when other folk be

 so , and think what will come of it after he be dead and go . a good solid

 " yes , I tell he last Sabbath . "

 " An ' how be thee t ' know if thee dostna coom ? Th ' lad on'y know what

 to - night . I shall stay with you to - night . " 
 Lisbeth be pacify by this prospect . and she have the whole evening 
 to talk with Dinah alone ; for there be a new room in the cottage , 
 you remember , build nearly two year ago , in the expectation of a new 
 inmate ; and here Adam always sit when he have write to do or plan to 
 make . Seth sit there too this evening , for he know his mother would like 
 to have Dinah all to herself . 
 there be two pretty picture on the two side of the wall in the 
 cottage . on one side there be the broad - shoulder , large - feature , 
 hardy old woman , in her blue jacket and buff kerchief , with her dim - eye 
 anxious look turn continually on the lily face and the slight form 
 in the black dress that be either move lightly about in helpful 
 activity , or seat close by the old woman 's arm - chair , hold she 
 wither hand , with eye lift up towards she to speak a language which 
 Lisbeth understand far well than the Bible or the hymn - book . she would 
 scarcely listen to read at all to - night . " Nay , nay , shut the book , " 
 she say . " we mun talk . I want t ' know what thee be cryin ' about . Hast 
 get trouble o ' thy own , like other folk ? " 
 on the other side of the wall there be the two brother so like each 
 other in the midst of their unlikeness : Adam with knit brow , shaggy 
 hair , and dark vigorous colour , absorb in his " figure " ; Seth , with 
 large rugged feature , the close copy of his brother 's , but with thin , 
 wavy , brown hair and blue dreamy eye , as often as not look vaguely 
 out of the window instead of at his book , although it be a newly buy 
 book - Wesley 's abridgment of Madame Guyon 's life , which be full of 
 wonder and interest for he . Seth have say to Adam , " can I help thee 
 with anything in here to - night ? I do not   want to make a noise in the 
 shop . " 
 " no , lad , " Adam answer , " there be nothing but what I must do myself . 
 Thee'st get thy new book to read . " 
 and often , when Seth be quite unconscious , Adam , as he pause after 
 draw a line with his ruler , look at his brother with a kind smile 
 dawn in his eye . he know " th ' lad like to sit full o ' thought he 
 could give no account of ; they would never come t ' anything , but they make 
 he happy , " and in the last year or so , Adam have be get more and 
 more indulgent to Seth . it be part of that grow tenderness which 
 come from the sorrow at work within he . 
 for Adam , though you see he quite master of himself , work hard and 
 delight in his work after his inborn inalienable nature , have not 
 outlive his sorrow - have not feel it slip from he as a temporary 
 burden , and leave he the same man again . do any of we ? God forbid . it 
 would be a poor result of all our anguish and our wrestling if we win 
 nothing but our old self at the end of it - if we could return to the 
 same blind love , the same self - confident blame , the same light thought 
 of human suffering , the same frivolous gossip over blighted human life , 
 the same feeble sense of that unknown towards which we have send forth 
 irrepressible cry in our loneliness . let we rather be thankful that 
 our sorrow live in we as an indestructible force , only change its 
 form , as force do , and pass from pain into sympathy - the one poor 
 word which include all our good insight and our good love . not that 
 this transformation of pain into sympathy have completely take place 
 in Adam yet . there be still a great remnant of pain , and this he feel 
 would subsist as long as her pain be not a memory , but an exist 
 thing , which he must think of as renew with the light of every 
 new morning . but we get accustomed to mental as well as bodily pain , 
 without , for all that , lose our sensibility to it . it become a habit 
 of our life , and we cease to imagine a condition of perfect ease 
 as possible for we . Desire be chasten into submission , and we be 
 content with our day when we have be able to bear our grief in 
 silence and act as if we be not suffer . for it be at such period 
 that the sense of our life have visible and invisible relation , 
 beyond any of which either our present or prospective self be the 
 centre , grow like a muscle that we be oblige to lean on and exert . 
 that be Adam 's state of mind in this second autumn of his sorrow . his 
 work , as you know , have always be part of his religion , and from very 
 early day he see clearly that good carpentry be God 's will - be that 
 form of God 's will that most immediately concern he . but now there 
 be no margin of dream for he beyond this daylight reality , no 
 holiday - time in the working - day world , no moment in the distance when 
 duty would take off her iron glove and breast - plate and clasp he gently 
 into rest . he conceive no picture of the future but one make up of 
 hard - work day such as he live through , with grow contentment and 
 intensity of interest , every fresh week . love , he think , could never 
 be anything to he but a live memory - a limb lop off , but not go 
 from consciousness . he do not know that the power of love be all the 
 while gain new force within he ; that the new sensibility buy by 
 a deep experience be so many new fibre by which it be possible , nay , 
 necessary to he , that his nature should intertwine with another . yet he 
 be aware that common affection and friendship be more precious to he 
 than they use to be - that he cling more to his mother and Seth , and 
 have an unspeakable satisfaction in the sight or imagination of any small 
 addition to their happiness . the Poysers , too - hardly three or four day 
 pass but he feel the need of see they and interchange word and 
 look of friendliness with they . he would have feel this , probably , even 
 if Dinah have not be with they , but he have only say the simple truth 
 in tell Dinah that he put she above all other friend in the world . 
 could anything be more natural ? for in the dark moment of memory the 
 thought of she always come as the first ray of return comfort . the 
 early day of gloom at the Hall Farm have be gradually turn into soft 
 moonlight by her presence ; and in the cottage , too , for she have come 
 at every spare moment to soothe and cheer poor Lisbeth , who have be 
 stricken with a fear that subdue even her querulousness at the sight of 
 her darle Adam 's grief - wear face . he have become use to watch she 
 light quiet movement , her pretty love way to the child , when he 
 go to the Hall Farm ; to listen for her voice as for a recurrent music ; 
 to think everything she say and do be just right , and could not have 
 be well . in spite of his wisdom , he could not find fault with she 
 for her overindulgence of the child , who have manage to convert Dinah 
 the preacher , before whom a circle of rough man have often tremble a 
 little , into a convenient household slave - though Dinah herself be 
 rather ashamed of this weakness , and have some inward conflict as to she 
 departure from the precept of Solomon . yes , there be one thing that 
 might have be well ; she might have love Seth and consent to marry 
 he . he feel a little vex , for his brother 's sake , and he could not 
 help think regretfully how Dinah , as Seth 's wife , would have make 
 their home as happy as it could be for they all - how she be the one 
 being that would have soothe their mother 's last day into peacefulness 
 and rest . 
 " it be wonderful she do not   love th ' lad , " Adam have say sometimes to 
 himself , " for anybody ' ud think he be just cut out for she . but her 
 heart 's so take up with other thing . she be one o ' those woman that 
 feel no drawing towards have a husband and child o ' their own . she 
 think she should be fill up with her own life then , and she be be 
 use so to live in other folk 's care , she can not   bear the thought of 
 her heart be shut up from them . I see how it be , well enough . she be 
 cut out o ' different stuff from most woman : I see that long ago . she be 
 never easy but when she be help somebody , and marriage ' ud interfere 
 with her way - that be true . I have   no right to be contrive and think 
 it ' ud be well if she would have Seth , as if I be wise than she be - or 
 than God either , for he make she what she be , and that be one o ' the 
 great blessing I have   ever have from his hand , and other besides I . " 
 this self - reproof have recur strongly to Adam 's mind when he gather 
 from Dinah 's face that he have wound she by refer to his wish 
 that she have accept Seth , and so he have endeavour to put into the 
 strong word his confidence in her decision as right - his resignation 
 even to she go away from they and cease to make part of their life 
 otherwise than by live in their thought , if that separation be 
 choose by herself . he feel sure she know quite well enough how much 
 he care to see she continually - to talk to she with the silent 
 consciousness of a mutual great remembrance . it be not possible she 
 should hear anything but self - renounce affection and respect in 
 his assurance that he be content for she to go away ; and yet there 
 remain an uneasy feeling in his mind that he have not say quite the 
 right thing - that , somehow , Dinah have not understand he . 
 Dinah must have rise a little before the sun the next morning , for she 
 be downstairs about five o'clock . so be Seth , for , through Lisbeth 's 
 obstinate refusal to have any woman - helper in the house , he have learn 
 to make himself , as Adam say , " very handy in the housework , " that he 
 might save his mother from too great weariness ; on which ground I hope 
 you will not think he unmanly , any more than you can have think the 
 gallant Colonel Bath unmanly when he make the gruel for his invalid 
 sister . Adam , who have sit up late at his writing , be still asleep , 
 and be not likely , Seth say , to be down till breakfast - time . often as 
 Dinah have visit Lisbeth during the last eighteen month , she have never 
 sleep in the cottage since that night after Thias 's death , when , you 
 remember , Lisbeth praise her deft movement and even give a modify 
 approval to her porridge . but in that long interval Dinah have make great 
 advance in household cleverness , and this morning , since Seth be there 
 to help , she be bent on bring everything to a pitch of cleanliness 
 and order that would have satisfy her Aunt Poyser . the cottage be far 
 from that standard at present , for Lisbeth 's rheumatism have force she 
 to give up her old habit of dilettante scour and polish . when the 
 kitchen be to her mind , Dinah go into the new room , where Adam have 
 be write the night before , to see what sweeping and dust be 
 need there . she open the window and let in the fresh morning air , 
 and the smell of the sweet - brier , and the bright low - slant ray of 
 the early sun , which make a glory about her pale face and pale auburn 
 hair as she hold the long brush , and sweep , sing to herself in a very 
 low tone - like a sweet summer murmur that you have to listen for very 
 closely - one of Charles Wesley 's hymn : 
 Eternal Beam of Light Divine , 
 fountain of unexhausted love , 
 in whom the Father 's glory shine , 
 through earth beneath and heaven above ; 
 Jesus ! the weary wanderer 's rest , 
 give I thy easy yoke to bear ; 
 with steadfast patience arm my breast , 
 with spotless love and holy fear . 
 speak to my war passion , " peace ! " 
 say to my trembling heart , " be still ! " 
 Thy power my strength and fortress be , 
 for all thing serve thy sovereign will . 
 she lay by the brush and take up the duster ; and if you have ever live 
 in Mrs. Poyser 's household , you would know how the duster behave in 
 Dinah 's hand - how it go into every small corner , and on every ledge 
 in and out of sight - how it go again and again round every bar of the 
 chair , and every leg , and under and over everything that lie on the 
 table , till it come to Adam 's paper and ruler and the open desk near 
 they . Dinah dust up to the very edge of these and then hesitate , 
 look at they with a longing but timid eye . it be painful to see 
 how much dust there be among they . as she be look in this way , she 
 hear Seth 's step just outside the open door , towards which her back 
 be turn , and say , raise her clear treble , " Seth , be your brother 
 wrathful when his paper be stir ? " 
 " yes , very , when they be not put back in the right place , " say a deep 
 strong voice , not Seth 's . 
 it be as if Dinah have put her hand unawares on a vibrate chord . 
 she be shake with an intense thrill , and for the instant feel nothing 
 else ; then she know her cheek be glow , and dare not look round , 
 but stand still , distressed because she could not say good - morning in a 
 friendly way . Adam , find that she do not look round so as to see 
 the smile on his face , be afraid she have think he serious about his 
 wrathfulness , and go up to she , so that she be oblige to look at 
 he . 
 " what ! you think I be a cross fellow at home , Dinah ? " he say , smilingly . 
 " Nay , " say Dinah , look up with timid eye , " not so . but you might 
 be put about by find thing meddle with ; and even the man Moses , the 
 meek of man , be wrathful sometimes . " 
 " come , then , " say Adam , look at she affectionately , " I will help you 
 move the thing , and put them back again , and then they can not   get wrong . 
 you be get to be your aunt 's own niece , I see , for particularness . " 
 they begin their little task together , but Dinah have not recover 
 herself sufficiently to think of any remark , and Adam look at she 
 uneasily . Dinah , he think , have seem to disapprove he somehow 
 lately ; she have not be so kind and open to he as she use to be . 
 he want she to look at he , and be as pleased as he be himself with 
 do this bit of playful work . but Dinah do not look at he - it be 
 easy for she to avoid look at the tall man - and when at last there 
 be no more dusting to be do and no further excuse for he to linger 
 near she , he could bear it no long , and say , in rather a plead 
 tone , " Dinah , you be not displeased with I for anything , be you ? I have 
 not say or do anything to make you think ill of I ? " 
 the question surprise she , and relieve she by give a new course to 
 her feeling . she look up at he now , quite earnestly , almost with the 
 tear come , and say , " oh , no , Adam ! how could you think so ? " 
 " I could not   bear you not to feel as much a friend to I as I do to you , " 
 say Adam . " and you do not   know the value I set on the very thought of 
 you , Dinah . that be what I mean yesterday , when I say I would be content 
 for you to go , if you think right . I mean , the thought of you be 
 worth so much to I , I should feel I ought to be thankful , and not 
 grumble , if you see right to go away . you know I do mind part with 
 you , Dinah ? " 
 " yes , dear friend , " say Dinah , trembling , but try to speak calmly , 
 " I know you have a brother 's heart towards I , and we shall often be 
 with one another in spirit ; but at this season I be in heaviness through 
 manifold temptation . you must not mark I . I feel call to leave my 
 kindre for a while ; but it be a trial - the flesh be weak . " 
 Adam see that it pain she to be oblige to answer . 
 " I hurt you by talk about it , Dinah , " he say . " I will say no more . 
 let us see if Seth 's ready with breakfast now . " 
 that be a simple scene , reader . but it be almost certain that you , too , 
 have be in love - perhaps , even , more than once , though you may not 
 choose to say so to all your feminine friend . if so , you will no more 
 think the slight word , the timid look , the tremulous touch , by which 
 two human soul approach each other gradually , like two little quivering 
 rain - stream , before they mingle into one - you will no more think these 
 thing trivial than you will think the first - detect sign of come 
 spring trivial , though they be but a faint indescribable something 
 in the air and in the song of the bird , and the tiny perceptible 
 bud on the hedge - row branch . those slight word and look and 
 touch be part of the soul 's language ; and the fine language , 
 I believe , be chiefly make up of unimposing word , such as " light , " 
 ' sound , " ' star , " " music " - word really not worth look at , or 
 hearing , in themselves , any more than " chip " or ' sawdust . " it be only 
 that they happen to be the sign of something unspeakably great and 
 beautiful . I be of opinion that love be a great and beautiful thing too , 
 and if you agree with I , the small sign of it will not be chip and 
 sawdust to you : they will rather be like those little word , " light " and 
 " music , " stir the long - wind fibre of your memory and enrich 
 your present with your most precious past . 
 Chapter LI 
 Sunday Morning 
 LISBETH 's touch of rheumatism could not be make to appear serious enough 
 to detain Dinah another night from the Hall Farm , now she have make up 
 her mind to leave she aunt so soon , and at evening the friend must 
 part . " for a long while , " Dinah have say , for she have tell Lisbeth of 
 her resolve . 
 " then it will be for all my life , an ' I shall ne'er see thee again , " say 
 Lisbeth . " long while ! I'n get no long while t ' live . an ' I shall be 
 take bad an ' die , an ' thee canst ne'er come a - nigh I , an ' I shall die 
 a - longing for thee . " 
 that have be the key - note of her wail talk all day ; for Adam be not 
 in the house , and so she put no restraint on her complaining . she have 
 try poor Dinah by return again and again to the question , why 
 she must go away ; and refuse to accept reason , which seem to she 
 nothing but whim and " contrairiness " ; and still more , by regret that 
 she " couldna ' ha ' one o ' the lad " and be her daughter . 
 " Thee couldstna put up wi ' Seth , " she say . " he isna cliver enough for 
 thee , happen , but he would ha ' be very good t ' thee - he be as handy as can 
 be at do thing for I when I be bad , an ' he be as fond o ' the Bible 
 an ' chappellin ' as thee art thysen . but happen , thee'dst like a husband 
 well as isna just the cut o ' thysen : the runnin ' brook isna athirst 
 for th ' rain . Adam ' ud ha ' do for thee - I know he would - an ' he might 
 come t ' like thee well enough , if thee'dst stop . but he be as stubborn 
 as th ' iron bar - there be no bend he no way but 's own . but he would be 
 a fine husband for anybody , be they who they will , so look - on an ' so 
 cliver as he be . and he would be rare an ' lovin' : it do I good on'y a 
 look o ' the lad 's eye when he mean kind tow'rt I . " 
 Dinah try to escape from Lisbeth 's close look and question by 
 find little task of housework that keep she move about , and as 
 soon as Seth come home in the evening she put on her bonnet to go . it 
 touch Dinah keenly to say the last good - bye , and still more to look 
 round on her way across the field and see the old woman still stand 
 at the door , gaze after she till she must have be the faint speck 
 in the dim aged eye . " the God of love and peace be with they , " 
 Dinah pray , as she look back from the last stile . " make they glad 
 accord to the day wherein thou hast afflict they , and the year 
 wherein they have see evil . it be thy will that I should part from 
 they ; let I have no will but thine . " 
 Lisbeth turn into the house at last and sit down in the workshop near 
 Seth , who be busy himself there with fit some bit of turn 
 wood he have bring from the village into a small work - box , which he 
 mean to give to Dinah before she go away . 
 " thee't see she again o ' Sunday afore she go , " be her first word . 
 " if thee wast good for anything , thee'dst make she come in again o ' 
 Sunday night wi ' thee , and see I once more . " 
 " Nay , Mother , " say Seth . " Dinah ' ud be sure to come again if she see 
 right to come . I should have no need to persuade she . she only think it 
 ' ud be troubling thee for nought , just to come in to say good - bye over 
 again . " 
 " she would ne'er go away , I know , if Adam ' ud be fond on she an ' marry she , 
 but everything be so contrairy , " say Lisbeth , with a burst of vexation . 
 Seth pause a moment and look up , with a slight blush , at his mother 's 
 face . " what ! have she say anything o ' that sort to thee , Mother ? " he 
 say , in a low tone . 
 " say ? Nay , she will say nothing . it be on'y the man as have to wait till 
 folk say thing afore they find them out . " 
 " well , but what make thee think so , Mother ? what be put it into thy 
 head ? " 
 " it be no matter what be put it into my head . my head 's none so hollow as 
 it must get in , an ' nought to put it there . I know she be fond on he , as 
 I know th ' wind 's comin ' in at the door , an ' that be anoof . an ' he might 
 be willin ' to marry she if he know'd she be fond on he , but he will ne'er 
 think on't   if somebody doesna put it into 's head . " 
 his mother 's suggestion about Dinah 's feeling towards Adam be not quite 
 a new thought to Seth , but her last word alarm he , lest she should 
 herself undertake to open Adam 's eye . he be not sure about Dinah 's 
 feeling , and he think he be sure about Adam 's . 
 " Nay , Mother , nay , " he say , earnestly , " thee mustna think o ' speak 
 o ' such thing to Adam . Thee'st no right to say what Dinah 's feeling 
 be if she hasna tell thee , and it ' ud do nothing but mischief to say 
 such thing to Adam . he feel very grateful and affectionate toward 
 Dinah , but he be no thought towards she that ' ud incline he to make she 
 his wife , and I do not   believe Dinah ' ud marry he either . I do not   think 
 she will marry at all . " 
 " eh , " say Lisbeth , impatiently . " thee think'st so 'cause she wouldna 
 ha ' thee . she will ne'er marry thee ; thee mightst as well like her t ' ha ' 
 thy brother . " 
 Seth be hurt . " Mother , " he say , in a remonstrate tone , " do not   think 
 that of I . I should be as thankful t ' have she for a sister as thee 
 wouldst t ' have she for a daughter . I have   no more thought about myself 
 in that thing , and I shall take it hard if ever thee say'st it again . " 
 " well , well , then thee shouldstna cross I wi ' sayin ' thing arena as I 
 say they be . " 
 " but , Mother , " say Seth , " thee'dst be do Dinah a wrong by tell 
 Adam what thee think'st about she . it ' ud do nothing but mischief , 
 for it ' ud make Adam uneasy if he doesna feel the same to she . and I be 
 pretty sure he feel nothing o ' the sort . " 
 " eh , donna tell I what thee't sure on ; thee know'st nought about it . 
 what be he allay go to the Poysers ' for , if he didna want t ' see she ? 
 he go twice where he use t ' go once . happen he knowsna as he want 
 t ' see she ; he knowsna as I put salt in 's broth , but he would miss it pretty 
 quick if it warna there . he will ne'er think o ' marry if it isna put 
 into 's head , an ' if thee'dst any love for thy mother , thee'dst put he 
 up to't an ' not let she go away out o ' my sight , when I might ha ' she to 
 make a bit o ' comfort for I afore I go to bed to my old man under the 
 white thorn . " 
 " Nay , Mother , " say Seth , " thee mustna think I unkind , but I should 
 be go against my conscience if I take upon I to say what Dinah 's 
 feeling be . and besides that , I think I should give offence to Adam by 
 speak to he at all about marrying ; and I counsel thee not to do't . 
 Thee may'st be quite deceive about Dinah . Nay , I be pretty sure , by 
 word she say to I last Sabbath , as she be no mind to marry . " 
 " eh , thee't as contrairy as the rest on them . if it war summat I didna 
 want , it ' ud be do fast enough . " 
 Lisbeth rise from the bench at this , and go out of the workshop , 
 leave Seth in much anxiety lest she should disturb Adam 's mind about 
 Dinah . he console himself after a time with reflect that , since 
 Adam 's trouble , Lisbeth have be very timid about speak to he on

 tender of all subject . even if she do , he hop Adam would not take

 Seth be right in believe that Lisbeth would be hold in restraint by

 her regretful thought about Dinah , till they have grow very near that

 Seth go away to chapel at Treddleston , the dangerous opportunity come . 
 Sunday morning be the happy time in all the week to Lisbeth , for 
 as there be no service at Hayslope church till the afternoon , Adam be 
 always at home , do nothing but reading , an occupation in which she 
 could venture to interrupt he . moreover , she have always a well dinner 
 than usual to prepare for her son - very frequently for Adam and herself 
 alone , Seth be often away the entire day - and the smell of the roast 
 meat before the clear fire in the clean kitchen , the clock tick in 
 a peaceful Sunday manner , her darle Adam seat near she in his good 
 clothe , do nothing very important , so that she could go and stroke 
 her hand across his hair if she like , and see he look up at her and 
 smile , while Gyp , rather jealous , poke his muzzle up between they - all 
 these thing make poor Lisbeth 's earthly paradise . 
 the book Adam most often read on a Sunday morning be his large picture 
 Bible , and this morning it lie open before he on the round white deal 
 table in the kitchen ; for he sit there in spite of the fire , because he 
 know his mother like to have he with she , and it be the only day in 
 the week when he could indulge she in that way . you would have like to 
 see Adam read his Bible . he never open it on a weekday , and so he 
 come to it as a holiday book , serve he for history , biography , and 
 poetry . he hold one hand thrust between his waistcoat button , and the 
 other ready to turn the page , and in the course of the morning you 
 would have see many change in his face . sometimes his lip move in 
 semi - articulation - it be when he come to a speech that he could fancy 
 himself uttering , such as Samuel 's die speech to the people ; then his 
 eyebrow would be raise , and the corner of his mouth would quiver a 
 little with sad sympathy - something , perhaps old Isaac 's meeting with 
 his son , touch he closely ; at other time , over the New Testament , 
 a very solemn look would come upon his face , and he would every now and 
 then shake his head in serious assent , or just lift up his hand and let 
 it fall again . and on some morning , when he read in the Apocrypha , of 
 which he be very fond , the son of Sirach 's keen - edge word would bring 
 a delighted smile , though he also enjoy the freedom of occasionally 
 differ from an Apocryphal writer . for Adam know the Articles quite 
 well , as become a good churchman . 
 Lisbeth , in the pause of attend to her dinner , always sit opposite 
 to he and watch he , till she could rest no long without go 
 up to he and give he a caress , to call his attention to she . this 
 morning he be read the Gospel accord to St. Matthew , and Lisbeth 
 have be stand close by he for some minute , stroke his hair , 
 which be smooth than usual this morning , and look down at the 
 large page with silent wonderment at the mystery of letter . she be 
 encourage to continue this caress , because when she first go up 
 to he , he have throw himself back in his chair to look at she

 morning . eh , Gyp want I t ' look at he . he can not   abide to think I love 
 thee the good . " Lisbeth say nothing , because she want to say so many 
 thing . and now there be a new leaf to be turn over , and it be 
 a picture - that of the angel seat on the great stone that have be 
 roll away from the sepulchre . this picture have one strong association

 she - that be Dinah . " 
 Adam smile , and , look more intently at the angel 's face , say , " it 
 be a bit like she ; but Dinah 's pretty , I think . " 
 " well , then , if thee think'st she so pretty , why arn't   fond on she ? " 
 Adam look up in surprise . " why , Mother , dost think I do not   set store 
 by Dinah ? " 
 " Nay , " say Lisbeth , frighten at her own courage , yet feel that 
 she have break the ice , and the water must flow , whatever mischief they

 " but I have   no right t ' hinder she , if she think well , " say Adam , 
 look at his book as if he want to go on reading . he foresee a 
 series of complaint tend to nothing . Lisbeth sit down again in the 
 chair opposite to he , as she say : 
 " but she wouldna think well if thee wastna so contrairy . " Lisbeth dare 
 not venture beyond a vague phrase yet . 
 " Contrairy , mother ? " Adam say , look up again in some anxiety . " what 
 have I do ? what dost mean ? " 
 " why , thee't never look at nothing , nor think o ' nothing , but thy 
 figurin , an ' thy work , " say Lisbeth , half - cry . " an ' dost think thee 
 canst go on so all thy life , as if thee wast a man cut out o ' timber ? 
 An ' what wut do when thy mother 's gone , an ' nobody to take care on thee 
 as thee gett'st a bit o ' victual comfortable I ' the mornin ' ? " 
 " what hast get I ' thy mind , Mother ? " say Adam , vex at this 
 whimpering . " I canna see what thee't drive at . be there anything I 
 could do for thee as I do not   do ? " 
 " Aye , an ' that there be . Thee might'st do as I should ha ' somebody wi ' 
 I to comfort I a bit , an ' wait on I when I be bad , an ' be good to I . " 
 " well , Mother , whose fault be it there isna some tidy body I ' th ' house 
 t ' help thee ? it isna by my wish as thee hast a stroke o ' work to do . we 
 can afford it - I have   tell thee often enough . it ' ud be a deal well for

 Chapter LII 
 Adam and Dinah 
 it be about three o'clock when Adam enter the farmyard and rouse 
 Alick and the dog from their Sunday dozing . Alick say everybody be 
 go to church " but th ' young missis " - so he call Dinah - but this 
 do not disappoint Adam , although the " everybody " be so liberal as 
 to include Nancy the dairymaid , whose work of necessity be not 
 unfrequently incompatible with church - go . 
 there be perfect stillness about the house . the door be all close , 
 and the very stone and tub seem quieter than usual . Adam hear the 
 water gently dripping from the pump - that be the only sound - and 
 he knock at the house door rather softly , as be suitable in that 
 stillness . 
 the door open , and Dinah stand before he , colour deeply with the 
 great surprise of see Adam at this hour , when she know it be his 
 regular practice to be at church . yesterday he would have say to she

 Poyser 's three - cornered chair .

 be hinder from go to church to - day , doubtless . "

 about you : that be the reason . " 
 this confession be very awkward and sudden , Adam feel , for he think

 careful and trouble for I , Adam . I have all thing and abound at

 go . "

 Dinah be too entirely reliant on the Supreme guidance to attempt to 
 achieve any end by a deceptive concealment . she be recover now from

 eye as she say , " yes , Adam , my heart be draw strongly towards you ;

 " then , Dinah , " Adam say at last , " how can there be anything contrary

 ought not   to do this and you ought not   to do that . you would follow your 
 conscience as much as you do now . " 
 " yes , Adam , " Dinah say , " I know marriage be a holy state for those who 
 be truly call to it , and have no other drawing ; but from my childhood

 have come from have no life of my own , no want , no wish for myself , 
 and live only in God and those of his creature whose sorrow and joy 
 he have give I to know . those have be very blessed year to I , and I

 " but if a new feeling have come into your mind , Dinah , and if you love I

 make it right when nothing else would ? "

 surely all other leisure be hurry compare with a sunny walk through the 
 field from " afternoon church " - as such walk use to be in those old 
 leisurely time , when the boat , glide sleepily along the canal , be 
 the new locomotive wonder ; when Sunday book have most of they old 
 brown - leather cover , and open with remarkable precision always in one 
 place . leisure be go - go where the spinning - wheel be go , and the 
 pack - horse , and the slow waggon , and the pedlar , who bring bargain 
 to the door on sunny afternoon . ingenious philosopher tell you , 
 perhaps , that the great work of the steam - engine be to create leisure 
 for mankind . do not believe they : it only create a vacuum for eager 
 think to rush in . even idleness be eager now - eager for amusement ; 
 prone to excursion - train , art museum , periodical literature , and 
 exciting novel ; prone even to scientific theorizing and cursory peep 
 through microscope . old Leisure be quite a different personage . he 
 only read one newspaper , innocent of leader , and be free from 
 that periodicity of sensation which we call post - time . he be a 
 contemplative , rather stout gentleman , of excellent digestion ; of quiet 
 perception , undisease by hypothesis ; happy in his inability to know 
 the cause of thing , prefer the thing themselves . he live chiefly 
 in the country , among pleasant seat and homestead , and be fond of 
 saunter by the fruit - tree wall and scent the apricot when they 
 be warm by the morning sunshine , or of shelter himself under 
 the orchard bough at noon , when the summer pear be fall . he know 
 nothing of weekday service , and think none the bad of the Sunday 
 sermon if it allow he to sleep from the text to the blessing ; like 
 the afternoon service well , because the prayer be the short , 
 and not ashamed to say so ; for he have an easy , jolly conscience , 
 broad - back like himself , and able to carry a great deal of beer or 
 port - wine , not be make squeamish by doubt and qualm and lofty 
 aspiration . life be not a task to he , but a sinecure . he finger the 
 guineas in his pocket , and eat his dinner , and sleep the sleep of the 
 irresponsible , for have he not keep up his character by go to church 
 on the Sunday afternoon ? 
 fine old leisure ! do not be severe upon he , and judge he by our modern 
 standard . he never go to Exeter Hall , or hear a popular preacher , or 
 read Tracts for the Times or Sartor Resartus . 
 Chapter LIII 
 the Harvest supper 
 as Adam be go homeward , on Wednesday evening , in the six o'clock 
 sunlight , he see in the distance the last load of barley wind its way 
 towards the yard - gate of the Hall Farm , and hear the chant of " Harvest 
 home ! " rise and sink like a wave . fainter and fainter , and more 
 musical through the grow distance , the fall die sound still 
 reach he , as he near the Willow Brook . the low westere sun shine 
 right on the shoulder of the old Binton Hills , turn the unconscious 
 sheep into bright spot of light ; shine on the window of the cottage 
 too , and make they a - flame with a glory beyond that of amber or 
 amethyst . it be enough to make Adam feel that he be in a great temple , 
 and that the distant chant be a sacred song . 
 " it be wonderful , " he think , " how that sound go to one 's heart almost 
 like a funeral bell , for all it tell one o ' the joyfull time o ' the 
 year , and the time when man be mostly the thankfull . I suppose it be 
 a bit hard to we to think anything be over and go in our life ; and 
 there be a parting at the root of all our joy . it be like what I feel 
 about Dinah . I should never ha ' come to know that her love ' ud be the 
 great o ' blessing to I , if what I count a blessing have not   be 
 wrench and tear away from I , and leave I with a great need , so as I 
 could crave and hunger for a great and a well comfort . " 
 he expect to see Dinah again this evening , and get leave to accompany 
 she as far as Oakbourne ; and then he would ask she to fix some time when 
 he might go to Snowfield , and learn whether the last good hope that have 
 be bear to he must be resign like the rest . the work he have to do 
 at home , besides put on his good clothe , make it seven before he 
 be on his way again to the Hall Farm , and it be questionable whether , 
 with his long and quick stride , he should be there in time even 
 for the roast beef , which come after the plum pudding , for Mrs. Poyser 's 
 supper would be punctual . 
 Great be the clatter of knife and pewter plate and tin can when Adam 
 enter the house , but there be no hum of voice to this accompaniment : 
 the eating of excellent roast beef , provide free of expense , be too 
 serious a business to those good farm - labourer to be perform with 
 a divided attention , even if they have have anything to say to each 
 other - which they have not . and Mr. Poyser , at the head of the table , be 
 too busy with his carving to listen to Bartle Massey 's or Mr. Craig 's 
 ready talk . 
 " here , Adam , " say Mrs. Poyser , who be stand and look on to see 
 that Molly and Nancy do their duty as waiter , " here be a place keep for 
 you between Mr. Massey and the boy . it be a poor tale you could not   come 
 to see the pudding when it be whole . " 
 Adam look anxiously round for a fourth woman 's figure , but Dinah 
 be not there . he be almost afraid of ask about she ; besides , his 
 attention be claim by greeting , and there remain the hope that 
 Dinah be in the house , though perhaps disinclined to festivity on the 
 eve of her departure . 
 it be a goodly sight - that table , with Martin Poyser 's round 
 good - humour face and large person at the head of it help his 
 servant to the fragrant roast beef and please when the empty plate 
 come again . Martin , though usually bl with a good appetite , really 
 forget to finish his own beef to - night - it be so pleasant to he to 
 look on in the interval of carve and see how the other enjoy their 
 supper ; for be they not man who , on all the day of the year except 
 Christmas Day and Sundays , eat their cold dinner , in a makeshift manner , 
 under the hedgerow , and drink their beer out of wooden bottle - with 
 relish certainly , but with their mouth towards the zenith , after a 
 fashion more endurable to duck than to human bipeds . Martin Poyser have 
 some faint conception of the flavour such man must find in hot roast 
 beef and fresh - draw ale . he hold his head on one side and screw 
 up his mouth , as he nudge Bartle Massey , and watch half - witted Tom 
 Tholer , otherwise know as " Tom Saft , " receive his second plateful of 
 beef . a grin of delight break over Tom 's face as the plate be set down 
 before he , between his knife and fork , which he hold erect , as if 
 they have be sacred taper . but the delight be too strong to continue 
 smoulder in a grin - it burst out the next instant in a long - draw 
 " haw , haw ! " follow by a sudden collapse into utter gravity , as the 
 knife and fork dart down on the prey . Martin Poyser 's large person 
 shake with his silent unctuous laugh . he turn towards Mrs. Poyser to 
 see if she too have be observant of Tom , and the eye of husband and 
 wife meet in a glance of good - natured amusement . 
 " Tom Saft " be a great favourite on the farm , where he play the part 
 of the old jester , and make up for his practical deficiency by his 
 success in repartee . his hit , I imagine , be those of the flail , which 
 fall quite at random , but nevertheless smash an insect now and then . 
 they be much quote at sheep - shearing and haymake time , but I 
 refrain from record they here , lest Tom 's wit should prove to be 
 like that of many other bygone jester eminent in their day - rather of a 
 temporary nature , not deal with the deep and more lasting relation 
 of thing . 
 Tom except , Martin Poyser have some pride in his servant and 
 labourer , think with satisfaction that they be the good worth 
 their pay of any set on the estate . there be Kester Bale , for example 
  Beale , probably , if the truth be know , but he be call Bale , and 
 be not conscious of any claim to a fifth letter , the old man with the 
 close leather cap and the network of wrinkle on his sun - brown face . 
 be there any man in Loamshire who know well the " natur " of all 
 farming work ? he be one of those invaluable labourer who can not only 
 turn their hand to everything , but excel in everything they turn their 
 hand to . it be true Kester 's knee be much bent outward by this time , 
 and he walk with a perpetual curtsy , as if he be among the most 
 reverent of man . and so he be ; but I be oblige to admit that the 
 object of his reverence be his own skill , towards which he perform 
 some rather affect act of worship . he always thatch the rick - for 
 if anything be his forte more than another , it be thatch - and when 
 the last touch have be put to the last beehive rick , Kester , whose home 
 lie at some distance from the farm , would take a walk to the rick - yard 
 in his good clothe on a Sunday morning and stand in the lane , at a due 
 distance , to contemplate his own thatching walk about to get each 
 rick from the proper point of view . as he curtsy along , with his eye 
 upturned to the straw knob imitative of golden globe at the summit 
 of the beehive rick , which indeed be gold of the good sort , you might 
 have imagine he to be engage in some pagan act of adoration . 
 Kester be an old bachelor and repute to have stocking full of coin , 
 concern which his master crack a joke with he every pay - night : 
 not a new unseasoned joke , but a good old one , that have be try 
 many time before and have wear well . " Th ' young measter 's a merry mon , " 
 Kester frequently remark ; for have begin his career by frighten 
 away the crow under the last Martin Poyser but one , he could never 
 cease to account the reign Martin a young master . I be not ashamed 
 of commemorate old Kester . you and I be indebted to the hard hand of 
 such man - hand that have long ago mingle with the soil they till so 
 faithfully , thriftily make the good they could of the earth 's fruit , 
 and receive the small share as their own wage . 
 then , at the end of the table , opposite his master , there be Alick , the 
 shepherd and head - man , with the ruddy face and broad shoulder , not on 
 the good term with old Kester ; indeed , their intercourse be confine 
 to an occasional snarl , for though they probably differ little 
 concern hedging and ditching and the treatment of ewe , there be a 
 profound difference of opinion between they as to their own respective 
 merit . when Tityrus and Meliboeus happen to be on the same farm , they 
 be not sentimentally polite to each other . Alick , indeed , be not by 
 any mean a honeyed man . his speech have usually something of a snarl 
 in it , and his broad - shoulder aspect something of the bull - dog 
 expression - " do not   you meddle with I , and I will not   meddle with you . " but 
 he be honest even to the splitting of an oat - grain rather than he 
 would take beyond his acknowledge share , and as " close - fisted " with 
 his master 's property as if it have be his own - throw very small 
 handfuls of damage barley to the chicken , because a large handful 
 affect his imagination painfully with a sense of profusion . 
 good - temper Tim , the waggoner , who love his horse , have his grudge 
 against Alick in the matter of corn . they rarely speak to each other , 
 and never look at each other , even over their dish of cold potato ; 
 but then , as this be their usual mode of behaviour towards all mankind , 
 it would be an unsafe conclusion that they have more than transient fit 
 of unfriendliness . the bucolic character at Hayslope , you perceive , 
 be not of that entirely genial , merry , broad - grinning sort , apparently 
 observe in most district visit by artist . the mild radiance of a 
 smile be a rare sight on a field - labourer 's face , and there be seldom 
 any gradation between bovine gravity and a laugh . nor be every labourer 
 so honest as our friend Alick . at this very table , among Mr. Poyser 's 
 man , there be that big Ben Tholoway , a very powerful thresher , but 
 detect more than once in carry away his master 's corn in his 
 pocket - an action which , as Ben be not a philosopher , could hardly be 
 ascribe to absence of mind . however , his master have forgive he , and 
 continue to employ he , for the Tholoways have live on the common time 
 out of mind , and have always work for the Poysers . and on the whole , I 
 daresay , society be not much the bad because Ben have not six month 
 of it at the treadmill , for his view of depredation be narrow , and 
 the House of Correction might have enlarge they . as it be , Ben eat his 
 roast beef to - night with a serene sense of have steal nothing more 
 than a few pea and bean as seed for his garden since the last harvest 
 supper , and feel warrant in think that Alick 's suspicious eye , for 
 ever upon he , be an injury to his innocence . 
 but now the roast beef be finish and the cloth be draw , leave 
 a fair large deal table for the bright drinking - can , and the foaming 
 brown jug , and the bright brass candlestick , pleasant to behold . now , 
 the great ceremony of the evening be to begin - the harvest - song , 
 in which every man must join . he might be in tune , if he like to be 
 singular , but he must not sit with closed lip . the movement be oblige 
 to be in triple time ; the rest be ad libitum . 
 as to the origin of this song - whether it come in its actual state from 
 the brain of a single rhapsodist , or be gradually perfect by a school 
 or succession of rhapsodist , I be ignorant . there be a stamp of 
 unity , of individual genius upon it , which incline I to the former 
 hypothesis , though I be not blind to the consideration that this unity 
 may rather have arise from that consensus of many mind which be a 
 condition of primitive thought , foreign to our modern consciousness . 
 some will perhaps think that they detect in the first quatrain 
 an indication of a lost line , which later rhapsodist , fail in 
 imaginative vigour , have supply by the feeble device of iteration . 
 other , however , may rather maintain that this very iteration be an 
 original felicity , to which none but the most prosaic mind can be 
 insensible . 
 the ceremony connect with the song be a drinking ceremony .   that 
 be perhaps a painful fact , but then , you know , we can not reform our 
 forefather .   during the first and second quatrain , sing decidedly 
 forte , no can be fill . 
 here be a health unto our master , 
 the founder of the feast ; 
 here be a health unto our master 
 and to our mistress ! 
 and may his doing prosper , 
 Whate'er he take in hand , 
 for we be all his servant , 
 and be at his command . 
 but now , immediately before the third quatrain or chorus , sung 
 fortissimo , with emphatic rap of the table , which give the effect of 
 cymbal and drum together , Alick 's can be fill , and he be bind to 
 empty it before the chorus cease . 
 then drink , boy , drink ! 
 and see ye do not spill , 
 for if ye do , ye shall drink two , 
 for ' tis our master 's will . 
 when Alick have go successfully through this test of steady - handed 
 manliness , it be the turn of old Kester , at his right hand - and so on , 
 till every man have drink his initiatory pint under the stimulus of the 
 chorus . Tom Saft - the rogue - take care to spill a little by accident ; 
 but Mrs. Poyser   too officiously , Tom think   interfere to prevent the 
 exaction of the penalty . 
 to any listener outside the door it would have be the reverse of 
 obvious why the " Drink , boy , drink ! " should have such an immediate and 
 often - repeat encore ; but once enter , he would have see that all 
 face be at present sober , and most of they serious - it be the 
 regular and respectable thing for those excellent farm - labourer to do , 
 as much as for elegant lady and gentleman to smirk and bow over their 
 wine - glass . Bartle Massey , whose ear be rather sensitive , have 
 go out to see what sort of evening it be at an early stage in the 
 ceremony , and have not finish his contemplation until a silence of 
 five minute declare that " drink , boy , drink ! " be not likely to 
 begin again for the next twelvemonth . much to the regret of the boy 
 and Totty : on they the stillness fall rather flat , after that glorious 
 thumping of the table , towards which Totty , seat on her father 's knee , 
 contribute with her small might and small fist . 
 when Bartle re - enter , however , there appear to be a general desire 
 for solo music after the choral . Nancy declare that Tim the waggoner 
 know a song and be " allay singing like a lark I ' the stable , " 
 whereupon Mr. Poyser say encouragingly , " Come , Tim , lad , let us hear 
 it . " Tim look sheepish , tuck down his head , and say he could not 
 sing , but this encourage invitation of the master 's be echo all 
 round the table . it be a conversational opportunity : everybody could 
 say , " Come , Tim , " except Alick , who never relax into the frivolity of 
 unnecessary speech . at last , Tim 's next neighbour , Ben Tholoway , begin 
 to give emphasis to his speech by nudge , at which Tim , grow rather 
 savage , say , " let I alooan , will ye ? else I will ma ' ye sing a toon ye 
 wonna like . " a good - temper waggoner 's patience have limit , and Tim be 
 not to be urge far . 
 " well , then , David , ye're the lad to sing , " say Ben , willing to show 
 that he be not discomfit by this check . " sing ' my loove be a roo 
 wi'out a thorn . ' " 
 the amatory David be a young man of an unconscious abstract 
 expression , which be due probably to a squint of superior intensity 
 rather than to any mental characteristic ; for he be not indifferent to 
 Ben 's invitation , but blush and laugh and rub his sleeve over his 
 mouth in a way that be regard as a symptom of yield . and for some 
 time the company appear to be much in earnest about the desire to hear 
 David 's song . but in vain . the lyricism of the evening be in the cellar 
 at present , and be not to be draw from that retreat just yet . 
 meanwhile the conversation at the head of the table have take a 
 political turn . Mr. Craig be not above talk politic occasionally , 
 though he pique himself rather on a wise insight than on specific 
 information . he see so far beyond the mere fact of a case that really 
 it be superfluous to know they . 
 " I be no reader o ' the paper myself , " he observe to - night , as he fill 
 his pipe , " though I might read it fast enough if I like , for there be 
 Miss Lyddy have them and be do with them I ' no time . but there be Mills , 
 now , sit I ' the chimney - corner and read the paper pretty nigh 
 from morning to night , and when he be get to th ' end on't   he be more 
 addle - headed than he be at the beginning . he be full o ' this peace now , 
 as they talk on ; he be be read and read , and think he be get to 
 the bottom on't . ' why , Lor ' bless you , mill , ' say I , ' you see no more 
 into this thing nor you can see into the middle of a potato . I will tell 
 you what it be : you think it will be a fine thing for the country . and I be 
 not again ' it - mark my word - I be not again ' it . but it be my opinion as 
 there be they at the head o ' this country as be bad enemy to we 
 nor Bony and all the mounseer he be get at 's back ; for as for the 
 mounseer , you may skewer half - a - dozen of them at once as if they war 
 frog . ' " 
 " aye , aye , " say Martin Poyser , listen with an air of much 
 intelligence and edification , " they ne'er eat a bit o ' beef I ' their 
 life . mostly sallet , I reckon . " 
 " and say I to Mills , " continue Mr. Craig , " ' will you try to make I 
 believe as furriner like they can do we half th ' harm they minister 
 do with their bad government ? if King George ' ud turn them all away and 
 govern by himself , he would see everything right . he might take on Billy 
 Pitt again if he like ; but I do not   see myself what we want wi ' anybody 
 besides King and Parliament . it be that nest o ' minister do the 
 mischief , I tell you . ' " 
 " ah , it be fine talk , " observe Mrs. Poyser , who be now seat near 
 her husband , with Totty on her lap - " it be fine talk . it be hard work 
 to tell which be Old Harry when everybody be get boot on . " 
 " as for this peace , " say Mr. Poyser , turn his head on one side in 
 a dubitative manner and give a precautionary puff to his pipe between 
 each sentence , " I do not   know . Th ' war 's a fine thing for the country , 
 an ' how will you keep up price wi'out it ? an ' they French be a wicked 
 sort o ' folk , by what I can make out . what can you do well nor fight 
 them ? " 
 " Ye're partly right there , Poyser , " say Mr. Craig , " but I be not again ' 
 the peace - to make a holiday for a bit . we can break it when we like , 
 an ' I be in no fear o ' Bony , for all they talk so much o ' his cliverness . 
 that be what I say to Mills this morning . Lor ' bless you , he see no 
 more through Bony! ... why , I put he up to more in three minute than he 
 get from 's paper all the year round . say I , ' be I a gardener as know 
 his business , or arn't   I , mill ? answer I that . ' ' to be sure y ' be , 
 Craig , ' say he - he be not a bad fellow , Mills be not , for a butler , but 
 weak I ' the head . ' well , ' say I , ' you talk o ' Bony 's cliverness ; would 
 it be any use my be a first - rate gardener if I would get nought but a 
 quagmire to work on ? ' ' no , ' say he . ' well , ' I say , ' that be just 
 what it be wi ' Bony . I will not deny but he may be a bit cliver - he be 
 no Frenchman bear , as I understand - but what be he get at 's back but 
 mounseer ? ' " 
 Mr. Craig pause a moment with an emphatic stare after this triumphant 
 speciman of socratic argument , and then add , thump the table rather 
 fiercely , " why , it be a sure thing - and there be they ' ull bear witness 
 to't - as I ' one regiment where there be one man a - miss , they put 
 the regimental on a big monkey , and they fit he as the shell fit the 
 walnut , and you could not   tell the monkey from the mounseer ! " 
 " ah ! think o ' that , now ! " say Mr. Poyser , impress at once with the 
 political bearing of the fact and with its striking interest as an

 " Come , Craig , " say Adam , " that be a little too strong . you do not   believe 
 that . it be all nonsense about the French be such poor stick . Mr. 
 Irwine be see them in their own country , and he say they have   plenty o ' 
 fine fellow among them . and as for knowledge , and contrivance , and

 Mr. Poyser look doubtfully at Mr. Craig , puzzle by this opposition of

 Martin have never " hear tell " of the French be good for much . Mr.

 own leg , which he turn a little outward for that purpose , when Bartle

 without you . be you go to disgrace your schoolmaster in his old 
 age ? " 
 " no , Mr. Massey , " say Adam . " Mr. and Mrs. Poyser can tell you where I 
 be . I be in no bad company . " 
 " she be go , Adam - go to Snowfield , " say Mr. Poyser , remind of

 " what ! " say Bartle , with an air of disgust . " be there a woman 
 concern ? then I give you up , Adam . " 
 " but it be a woman you'n speak well on , Bartle , " say Mr. Poyser . " come

 " I mean her voice , man - I mean her voice , that be all , " say Bartle .

 " aye , aye ! " say Mrs. Poyser ; " one ' ud think , an ' hear some folk talk ,

 only smell at it . they can see through a barn - door , they can . perhaps

 " ah ! " say Bartle sneeringly , " the woman be quick enough - they be quick

 " like enough , " say Mrs. Poyser , " for the man be mostly so slow , their 
 thought overrun them , an ' they can only catch them by the tail . I can 
 count a stocking - top while a man 's get 's tongue ready an ' when he 
 out wi ' his speech at last , there be little broth to be make on't . it be 
 your dead chick take the long hatchin ' . Howiver , I be not denyin ' the 
 woman be foolish : God Almighty make them to match the man . " 
 " Match ! " say Bartle . " Aye , as vinegar match one 's tooth . if a man 
 say a word , his wife will match it with a contradiction ; if he be a 
 mind for hot meat , his wife will match it with cold bacon ; if he laugh , 
 she will match he with whimpering . she be such a match as the horse - fly 
 be to th ' horse : she be get the right venom to ste he with - the right

 " yes , " say Mrs. Poyser , " I know what the man like - a poor soft , as ' ud 
 simper at them like the picture o ' the sun , whether they do right or

 " well , " say Mr. Craig , willing to conciliate Mrs. Poyser and set a

 " you be out there , Craig , " say Bartle , dryly ; " you be out there . you 
 judge o ' your garden - stuff on a well plan than that . you pick the

 to much - never come to much - but they make excellent simpleton , ripe 
 and strong - flavour . " 
 " what dost say to that ? " say Mr. Poyser , throw himself back and 
 look merrily at his wife . 
 " say ! " answer Mrs. Poyser , with dangerous fire kindling in her 
 eye . " why , I say as some folk ' tongue be like the clock as run 
 on strikin ' , not to tell you the time o ' the day , but because there be 
 summat wrong I ' their own inside ... " 
 Mrs. Poyser would probably have bring her rejoinder to a further

 the company at Alick 's end of the table take this form of vocal

 be split . "

 never get hold of you now . "

 but Adam be resolute , so the good - night be say , and the two friend
